problem:
Exercise 1.1
Is the set $\{a \in \mathbf{R}^k \mid p(0) = 1,\ |p(t)| \leq 1 \text{ for } \alpha \leq t \leq \beta\}$, where

\[
p(t) = a_1 + a_2 t + \cdots + a_k t^{k-1},
\]

convex?
problem_with_context:
Exercise 1.1
Is the set \(\{a \in \mathbf{R}^k \mid p(0) = 1,\ |p(t)| \leq 1 \text{ for } \alpha \leq t \leq \beta\}\), where

\(p(t) = a_1 + a_2 t + \cdots + a_k t^{k-1},\)

convex?
problem_standardized_math:
Determine whether the set
\(\left\{a \in \mathbf{R}^k \;\middle|\; p(0)=1,\; |p(t)| \leq 1 \text{ for all } t \in [\alpha,\beta]\right\}\)
is convex, where
\(p(t)=a_1+a_2 t+\cdots + a_k t^{k-1}.\)
problem_finally:
Let \(k \in \mathbf{N}\) and \(\alpha,\beta \in \mathbf{R}\) with \(\alpha \le \beta\). For each \(a=(a_1,\dots,a_k) \in \mathbf{R}^k\), define
\(p_a(t)=a_1+a_2 t+\cdots+a_k t^{k-1}, \qquad t \in \mathbf{R}.\)
Define
\(S=\left\{a \in \mathbf{R}^k \;\middle|\; p_a(0)=1,\; |p_a(t)| \le 1 \text{ for all } t \in [\alpha,\beta]\right\}.\)
Determine whether \(S\) is convex.

problem:
Exercise 1.2
\emph{Set distributive characterization of convexity} [Rockafellar]. Show that $C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$ is convex if and only if $(\alpha + \beta)C = \alpha C + \beta C$ for all nonnegative $\alpha, \beta$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 1.2
\emph{Set distributive characterization of convexity} [Rockafellar]. Show that \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\) is convex if and only if \((\alpha + \beta)C = \alpha C + \beta C\) for all nonnegative \(\alpha, \beta\).
problem_standardized_math:
Show that a set \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\) is convex if and only if
\((\alpha+\beta)C=\alpha C+\beta C\)\nfor all nonnegative scalars \(\alpha,\beta\).
problem_finally:
Let \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\). For \(\lambda \ge 0\), define
\(\lambda C := \{\lambda x : x \in C\},\)\nand for \(A,B \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\), define
\(A + B := \{a+b : a \in A,\ b \in B\}.\)
Prove that the following are equivalent:
1. \(C\) is convex.
2. For all scalars \(\alpha,\beta \ge 0\),
\((\alpha+\beta)C = \alpha C + \beta C.\)

problem:
Exercise 1.3
\emph{Composition of linear-fractional functions.} Suppose $\phi : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}^m$ and $\psi : \mathbf{R}^m \to \mathbf{R}^p$ are the linear-fractional functions

\[
\phi(x) = \frac{Ax + b}{c^T x + d}, \qquad
\psi(y) = \frac{Ey + f}{g^T y + h},
\]

with domains $\mathbf{dom}\,\phi = \{x \mid c^T x + d > 0\}$, $\mathbf{dom}\,\psi = \{y \mid g^T x + h > 0\}$. We associate with $\phi$ and $\psi$ the matrices

\[
\begin{bmatrix}
A & b \\
c^T & d
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
\begin{bmatrix}
E & f \\
g^T & h
\end{bmatrix},
\]

respectively.

Now consider the composition $\Gamma$ of $\psi$ and $\phi$, i.e., $\Gamma(x) = \psi(\phi(x))$, with domain

\[
\mathbf{dom}\,\Gamma = \{x \in \mathbf{dom}\,\phi \mid \phi(x) \in \mathbf{dom}\,\psi\}.
\]

Show that $\Gamma$ is linear-fractional, and that the matrix associated with it is the product

\[
\begin{bmatrix}
E & f \\
g^T & h
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
A & b \\
c^T & d
\end{bmatrix}.
\]
problem_with_context:
Exercise 1.3
\emph{Composition of linear-fractional functions.} Suppose \(\phi : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}^m\) and \(\psi : \mathbf{R}^m \to \mathbf{R}^p\) are the linear-fractional functions

\[
\phi(x) = \frac{Ax + b}{c^T x + d}, \qquad
\psi(y) = \frac{Ey + f}{g^T y + h},
\]

with domains \(\mathbf{dom}\,\phi = \{x \mid c^T x + d > 0\}\), \(\mathbf{dom}\,\psi = \{y \mid g^T x + h > 0\}\). We associate with \(\phi\) and \(\psi\) the matrices

\[
\begin{bmatrix}
A & b \c^T & d
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
\begin{bmatrix}
E & f \g^T & h
\end{bmatrix},
\]

respectively.

Now consider the composition \(\Gamma\) of \(\psi\) and \(\phi\), i.e., \(\Gamma(x) = \psi(\phi(x))\), with domain

\(\mathbf{dom}\,\Gamma = \{x \in \mathbf{dom}\,\phi \mid \phi(x) \in \mathbf{dom}\,\psi\}.\)

Show that \(\Gamma\) is linear-fractional, and that the matrix associated with it is the product

\[
\begin{bmatrix}
E & f \g^T & h
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
A & b \c^T & d
\end{bmatrix}.
\]
problem_standardized_math:
Let
\(\phi : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}^m, \qquad \phi(x)=\frac{Ax+b}{c^T x+d},\)
and
\(\psi : \mathbf{R}^m \to \mathbf{R}^p, \qquad \psi(y)=\frac{Ey+f}{g^T y+h},\)
be linear-fractional functions with domains
\(\operatorname{dom}\phi=\{x\mid c^T x+d>0\}, \qquad \operatorname{dom}\psi=\{y\mid g^T x+h>0\}.\)
Associate with \(\phi\) and \(\psi\) the matrices
\[
\begin{bmatrix}
A & b \c^T & d
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
\begin{bmatrix}
E & f \g^T & h
\end{bmatrix},
\]
respectively.

Now consider the composition
\(\Gamma(x)=\psi(\phi(x)),\)
with domain
\(\operatorname{dom}\Gamma=\{x\in \operatorname{dom}\phi\mid \phi(x)\in \operatorname{dom}\psi\}.\)
Show that \(\Gamma\) is linear-fractional and that the matrix associated with \(\Gamma\) is
\[
\begin{bmatrix}
E & f \g^T & h
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
A & b \c^T & d
\end{bmatrix}.
\]
problem_finally:
Let
\(\phi : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}^m, \qquad \phi(x)=\frac{Ax+b}{c^T x+d},\)
and
\(\psi : \mathbf{R}^m \to \mathbf{R}^p, \qquad \psi(y)=\frac{Ey+f}{g^T y+h},\)
be linear-fractional functions, where
\(A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}, \quad b \in \mathbf{R}^m, \quad c \in \mathbf{R}^n, \quad d \in \mathbf{R},\)
\(E \in \mathbf{R}^{p \times m}, \quad f \in \mathbf{R}^p, \quad g \in \mathbf{R}^m, \quad h \in \mathbf{R}.\)
Their domains are
\[
\operatorname{dom}\phi = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid c^T x + d > 0\},
\qquad
\operatorname{dom}\psi = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^m \mid g^T y + h > 0\}.
\]
Associate to \(\phi\) and \(\psi\) the block matrices
\[
\begin{bmatrix}
A & b \c^T & d
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
\begin{bmatrix}
E & f \g^T & h
\end{bmatrix}.
\]
Define
\(\Gamma(x) = \psi(\phi(x)),\)
with domain
\(\operatorname{dom}\Gamma = \{x \in \operatorname{dom}\phi \mid \phi(x) \in \operatorname{dom}\psi\}.\)

1. Show that \(\Gamma\) is a linear-fractional function.

2. Prove that there exist matrices and vectors of compatible dimensions, denoted \(\widetilde{A}, \widetilde{b}, \widetilde{c}, \widetilde{d}\), such that
\(\Gamma(x) = \frac{\widetilde{A}x + \widetilde{b}}{\widetilde{c}^T x + \widetilde{d}}.\)

3. Prove that the block matrix associated with \(\Gamma\) is
\[
\begin{bmatrix}
\widetilde{A} & \widetilde{b} \\
\widetilde{c}^T & \widetilde{d}
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
E & f \g^T & h
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
A & b \c^T & d
\end{bmatrix}.
\]

problem:
Exercise 1.4
Dual of exponential cone. The exponential cone $K_{\exp} \subseteq \mathbf{R}^3$ is defined as

\[
K_{\exp} = \{(x,y,z) \mid y > 0,\ ye^{x/y} \leq z\}.
\]

Find the dual cone $K_{\exp}^*$.

We are not worried here about the fine details of what happens on the boundaries of these cones, so you really needn't worry about it. But we make some comments here for those who do care about such things.

The cone $K_{\exp}$ as defined above is not closed. To obtain its closure, we need to add the points

\[
\{(x,y,z) \mid x \leq 0,\ y = 0,\ z \geq 0\}.
\]

(This makes no difference, since the dual of a cone is equal to the dual of its closure.)
problem_with_context:
Exercise 1.4
Dual of exponential cone. The exponential cone \(K_{\exp} \subseteq \mathbf{R}^3\) is defined as

\(K_{\exp} = \{(x,y,z) \mid y > 0,\ ye^{x/y} \leq z\}.\)

Find the dual cone \(K_{\exp}^*\).

We are not worried here about the fine details of what happens on the boundaries of these cones, so you really needn't worry about it. But we make some comments here for those who do care about such things.

The cone \(K_{\exp}\) as defined above is not closed. To obtain its closure, we need to add the points

\(\{(x,y,z) \mid x \leq 0,\ y = 0,\ z \geq 0\}.\)

(This makes no difference, since the dual of a cone is equal to the dual of its closure.)
problem_standardized_math:
Let the exponential cone \(K_{\exp} \subseteq \mathbf{R}^3\) be defined by
\(K_{\exp} = \{(x,y,z) \mid y > 0,\; y e^{x/y} \leq z\}.\)
Find the dual cone \(K_{\exp}^*\).

Note: \(K_{\exp}\) as defined above is not closed; its closure is obtained by adjoining the points
\(\{(x,y,z) \mid x \leq 0,\; y = 0,\; z \geq 0\}.\)
This does not affect the problem, since the dual of a cone is equal to the dual of its closure.
problem_finally:
Let the exponential cone be
\(K_{\exp} = \{(x,y,z) \in \mathbf{R}^3 \mid y > 0,\; y e^{x/y} \le z\}.\)
Its closure is
\(\overline{K_{\exp}} = K_{\exp} \cup \{(x,y,z) \in \mathbf{R}^3 \mid x \le 0,\; y = 0,\; z \ge 0\}.\)
The dual cone of a cone \(K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^3\) is defined by
\(K^* = \{(u,v,w) \in \mathbf{R}^3 \mid ux + vy + wz \ge 0 \text{ for all } (x,y,z) \in K\}.\)
It is known that a cone and its closure have the same dual. Determine the dual cone
\(K_{\exp}^*.\)

problem:
Exercise 1.5
Dual of intersection of cones. Let $C$ and $D$ be closed convex cones in $\mathbf{R}^n$. In this problem we will show that

\[
(C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*.
\]

Here, $+$ denotes set addition: $C^* + D^*$ is the set $\{u + v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}$. In other words, the dual of the intersection of two closed convex cones is the sum of the dual cones.

(a) Show that $C \cap D$ and $C^* + D^*$ are convex cones. (In fact, $C \cap D$ and $C^* + D^*$ are closed, but we won't ask you to show this.)
problem_with_context:
Exercise 1.5
Dual of intersection of cones. Let \(C\) and \(D\) be closed convex cones in \(\mathbf{R}^n\). In this problem we will show that

\((C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*.\)

Here, \(+\) denotes set addition: \(C^* + D^*\) is the set \(\{u + v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}\). In other words, the dual of the intersection of two closed convex cones is the sum of the dual cones.

(a) Show that \(C \cap D\) and \(C^* + D^*\) are convex cones. (In fact, \(C \cap D\) and \(C^* + D^*\) are closed, but we won't ask you to show this.)
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(C\) and \(D\) be closed convex cones in \(\mathbf{R}^n\). Show that \(C \cap D\) and
\(C^* + D^* = \{u+v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}\)
are convex cones.
problem_finally:
Let \(C, D \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\) be closed convex cones. For any cone \(K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\), define its dual cone by
\(K^* := \{ y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K \}.\)
Show that each of the following sets is a convex cone in \(\mathbf{R}^n\):

1. \(C \cap D\).
2. \(C^* + D^* := \{ u + v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^* \}\).

problem:
Exercise 1.5
Dual of intersection of cones. Let $C$ and $D$ be closed convex cones in $\mathbf{R}^n$. In this problem we will show that

\[
(C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*.
\]

Here, $+$ denotes set addition: $C^* + D^*$ is the set $\{u + v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}$. In other words, the dual of the intersection of two closed convex cones is the sum of the dual cones.

(b) Show that $(C \cap D)^* \supseteq C^* + D^*$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 1.5
Dual of intersection of cones. Let \(C\) and \(D\) be closed convex cones in \(\mathbf{R}^n\). In this problem we will show that

\((C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*.\)

Here, \(+\) denotes set addition: \(C^* + D^*\) is the set \(\{u + v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}\). In other words, the dual of the intersection of two closed convex cones is the sum of the dual cones.

(b) Show that \((C \cap D)^* \supseteq C^* + D^*\).
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(C\) and \(D\) be closed convex cones in \(\mathbf{R}^n\). Show that
\((C \cap D)^* \supseteq C^* + D^*,\)
where \(+\) denotes set addition, i.e.,
\(C^* + D^* = \{u+v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}.\)
problem_finally:
Let \(C,D \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\) be closed convex cones. For any cone \(K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\), define its dual cone by
\(K^* := \{ y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \ge 0 \ \text{for all } x \in K \}.\)
For sets \(A,B \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\), define their sum by
\(A+B := \{ a+b \mid a \in A,\ b \in B \}.\)
Show that
\((C \cap D)^* \supseteq C^* + D^*.\)

problem:
Exercise 1.5
Dual of intersection of cones. Let $C$ and $D$ be closed convex cones in $\mathbf{R}^n$. In this problem we will show that

\[
(C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*.
\]

Here, $+$ denotes set addition: $C^* + D^*$ is the set $\{u + v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}$. In other words, the dual of the intersection of two closed convex cones is the sum of the dual cones.

(c) Now let's show $(C \cap D)^* \subseteq C^* + D^*$. You can do this by first showing

\[
(C \cap D)^* \subseteq C^* + D^* \Longleftrightarrow C \cap D \supseteq (C^* + D^*)^* .
\]

You can use the following result:

\[
\text{If } K \text{ is a closed convex cone, then } K^{**} = K.
\]

Next, show that $C \cap D \supseteq (C^* + D^*)^*$ and conclude $(C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 1.5
Dual of intersection of cones. Let \(C\) and \(D\) be closed convex cones in \(\mathbf{R}^n\). In this problem we will show that

\((C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*.\)

Here, \(+\) denotes set addition: \(C^* + D^*\) is the set \(\{u + v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}\). In other words, the dual of the intersection of two closed convex cones is the sum of the dual cones.

(c) Now let's show \((C \cap D)^* \subseteq C^* + D^*\). You can do this by first showing

\((C \cap D)^* \subseteq C^* + D^* \Longleftrightarrow C \cap D \supseteq (C^* + D^*)^* .\)

You can use the following result:

\(\text{If } K \text{ is a closed convex cone, then } K^{**} = K.\)

Next, show that \(C \cap D \supseteq (C^* + D^*)^*\) and conclude \((C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*\).
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(C\) and \(D\) be closed convex cones in \(\mathbf{R}^n\). Show that \((C \cap D)^* \subseteq C^* + D^*\), where \(C^* + D^* = \{u+v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}\). You may do this by first proving
\((C \cap D)^* \subseteq C^* + D^* \iff C \cap D \supseteq (C^* + D^*)^*,\)\nand using the fact that if \(K\) is a closed convex cone, then \(K^{**} = K\). Then show that
\(C \cap D \supseteq (C^* + D^*)^*,\)\nand conclude that
\((C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*.\)
problem_finally:
Let \(C, D \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\) be closed convex cones. For any cone \(K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\), define its dual cone by
\(K^* := \{ y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K \}.\)
Define also the Minkowski sum
\(C^* + D^* := \{ u+v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^* \}.\)
Prove that
\((C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*.\)
You may proceed as follows:

1. Show that
\((C \cap D)^* \subseteq C^* + D^* \iff C \cap D \supseteq (C^* + D^*)^*.\)

2. Using the fact that for every closed convex cone \(K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\),
\(K^{**} = K,\)\nshow that
\(C \cap D \supseteq (C^* + D^*)^*.\)

3. Deduce that
\((C \cap D)^* \subseteq C^* + D^*.\)
Then prove the reverse inclusion
\(C^* + D^* \subseteq (C \cap D)^*,\)\nand conclude that
\((C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*.\)

problem:
Exercise 1.5
Dual of intersection of cones. Let $C$ and $D$ be closed convex cones in $\mathbf{R}^n$. In this problem we will show that

\[
(C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*.
\]

Here, $+$ denotes set addition: $C^* + D^*$ is the set $\{u + v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}$. In other words, the dual of the intersection of two closed convex cones is the sum of the dual cones.

(d) Show that the dual of the polyhedral cone $V = \{x \mid Ax \succeq 0\}$ can be expressed as

\[
V^* = \{A^T v \mid v \succeq 0\}.
\]
problem_with_context:
Exercise 1.5
Dual of intersection of cones. Let \(C\) and \(D\) be closed convex cones in \(\mathbf{R}^n\). In this problem we will show that

\((C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*.\)

Here, \(+\) denotes set addition: \(C^* + D^*\) is the set \(\{u + v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}\). In other words, the dual of the intersection of two closed convex cones is the sum of the dual cones.

(d) Show that the dual of the polyhedral cone \(V = \{x \mid Ax \succeq 0\}\) can be expressed as

\(V^* = \{A^T v \mid v \succeq 0\}.\)
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(V=\{x\mid Ax\succeq 0\}\) be a polyhedral cone. Show that its dual cone can be written as
\(V^*=\{A^T v\mid v\succeq 0\}.\)
problem_finally:
Let
\(V = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid Ax \ge 0\}, \qquad A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n},\)
where the inequality \(Ax \ge 0\) is interpreted componentwise in \(\mathbf{R}^m\). Define the dual cone of \(V\) by
\(V^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in V\}.\)
Show that
\(V^* = \{A^T v \mid v \in \mathbf{R}^m,\ v \ge 0\},\)
where \(v \ge 0\) is interpreted componentwise.

problem:
Exercise 1.6
Polar of a set. The polar of $C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$ is defined as the set

\[
C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.
\]

(a) Show that $C^\circ$ is convex (even if $C$ is not).
problem_with_context:
Exercise 1.6
Polar of a set. The polar of \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\) is defined as the set

\(C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.\)

(a) Show that \(C^\circ\) is convex (even if \(C\) is not).
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\), and define its polar by
\(C^\circ = \{\, y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C \,\}.\)
Show that \(C^\circ\) is convex, even if \(C\) is not.
problem_finally:
Let \(n \in \mathbf{N}\) and let \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\) be arbitrary. Define the polar set
\(C^{\circ} = \{ y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \le 1 \text{ for all } x \in C \}.\)
Prove that \(C^{\circ}\) is a convex subset of \(\mathbf{R}^n\), without assuming that \(C\) is convex.

problem:
Exercise 1.6
Polar of a set. The polar of $C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$ is defined as the set

\[
C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.
\]

(b) What is the polar of a cone?
problem_with_context:
Exercise 1.6
Polar of a set. The polar of \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\) is defined as the set

\(C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.\)

(b) What is the polar of a cone?
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\) be a cone. The polar of a set \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\) is defined by
\(C^{\circ} = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.\)
What is the polar \(C^{\circ}\) of a cone?
problem_finally:
Let \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\) be a cone, i.e., \(t x \in C\) for every \(x \in C\) and every \(t \ge 0\). The polar of a set \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\) is defined by
\(C^{\circ} = \{\, y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \le 1 \text{ for all } x \in C \,\}.\)
Determine \(C^{\circ}\) explicitly under the assumption that \(C\) is a cone.

problem:
Exercise 1.6
Polar of a set. The polar of $C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$ is defined as the set

\[
C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.
\]

(c) What is the polar of the unit ball for a norm $\|\cdot\|$?
problem_with_context:
Exercise 1.6
Polar of a set. The polar of \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\) is defined as the set

\(C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.\)

(c) What is the polar of the unit ball for a norm \(\|\cdot\|\)?
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\). The polar of \(C\) is defined by
\(C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.\)
What is the polar of the unit ball of a norm \(\|\cdot\|\)?
problem_finally:
Let \(n \in \mathbf{N}\), let \(\|\cdot\|\) be a norm on \(\mathbf{R}^n\), and let
\(B = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid \|x\| \le 1\}\)
be its closed unit ball. For any set \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\), define its polar by
\(C^{\circ} = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^{T}x \le 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.\)
Determine \(B^{\circ}\), and express it in terms of the dual norm of \(\|\cdot\|\).

problem:
Exercise 1.6
Polar of a set. The polar of $C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$ is defined as the set

\[
C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.
\]

(d) What is the polar of the set $C = \{x \mid \mathbf{1}^T x = 1,\, x \succeq 0\}$?
problem_with_context:
Exercise 1.6
Polar of a set. The polar of \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\) is defined as the set

\(C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.\)

(d) What is the polar of the set \(C = \{x \mid \mathbf{1}^T x = 1,\, x \succeq 0\}\)?
problem_standardized_math:
Let the polar of a set \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\) be defined by
\(C^\circ = \{\, y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C \,\}.\)
What is the polar of the set
\(C = \{\, x \mid \mathbf{1}^T x = 1,\; x \succeq 0 \,\}?\)
problem_finally:
Let
\(C = \{ x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid \mathbf{1}^T x = 1,\ x \ge 0 \},\)
where \(\mathbf{1} \in \mathbf{R}^n\) is the all-ones vector and \(x \ge 0\) means \(x_i \ge 0\) for all \(i = 1,\dots,n\).

The polar of a set \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\) is defined by
\(C^{\circ} = \{ y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \le 1 \text{ for all } x \in C \}.\)

Determine an explicit description of \(C^{\circ}\).

problem:
Exercise 1.6
Polar of a set. The polar of $C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$ is defined as the set

\[
C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.
\]

(e) Show that if $C$ is closed and convex, with $0 \in C$, then $(C^\circ)^\circ = C$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 1.6
Polar of a set. The polar of \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\) is defined as the set

\(C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.\)

(e) Show that if \(C\) is closed and convex, with \(0 \in C\), then \((C^\circ)^\circ = C\).
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\), and define its polar by
\(C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.\)
Show that if \(C\) is closed and convex and \(0 \in C\), then
\((C^\circ)^\circ = C.\)
problem_finally:
Let \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\) be closed and convex, with \(0 \in C\). Define the polar set
\(C^{\circ} = \{ y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \le 1 \text{ for all } x \in C \}.\)
Show that the bipolar of \(C\) equals \(C\), i.e.,
\((C^{\circ})^{\circ} = C.\)

problem:
Exercise 1.7
Dual cones in $\mathbf{R}^2$. Describe the dual cone for each of the following cones.

(a) $K = \{0\}$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 1.7
Dual cones in \(\mathbf{R}^2\). Describe the dual cone for each of the following cones.

(a) \(K = \{0\}\).
problem_standardized_math: Let \(K = \{0\} \subset \mathbf{R}^2\). Describe the dual cone \(K^*\).
problem_finally:
Let \(K = \{0\} \subseteq \mathbf{R}^2\), where \(0\) is the zero vector. The dual cone of \(K\) is defined by
\(K^* = \{ y \in \mathbf{R}^2 : y \cdot x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K \}.\)
Determine \(K^*\) explicitly.

problem:
Exercise 1.7
Dual cones in $\mathbf{R}^2$. Describe the dual cone for each of the following cones.

(c) $K = \{(x_1, x_2) \mid |x_1| \leq x_2\}$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 1.7

(c) \(K = \{(x_1, x_2) \mid |x_1| \leq x_2\}\).
problem_standardized_math: Let \(K = \,\{(x_1,x_2) \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid |x_1| \le x_2\}\). Describe the dual cone of \(K\).
problem_finally:
Let
\(K = \{(x_1,x_2) \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid |x_1| \le x_2\}.\)
Determine the dual cone
\(K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid y^{\top}x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}.\)
Express \(K^*\) explicitly as a subset of \(\mathbf{R}^2\).

problem:
Exercise 1.7
Dual cones in $\mathbf{R}^2$. Describe the dual cone for each of the following cones.

(d) $K = \{(x_1, x_2) \mid x_1 + x_2 = 0\}$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 1.7
Dual cones in \(\mathbf{R}^2\). Describe the dual cone for each of the following cones.

(d) \(K = \{(x_1, x_2) \mid x_1 + x_2 = 0\}\).
problem_standardized_math:
Describe the dual cone of the cone
\(K=\{(x_1,x_2)\mid x_1+x_2=0\}\)
in \(\mathbf{R}^2\).
problem_finally:
Let
\(K = \{(x_1,x_2) \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid x_1 + x_2 = 0\}.\)
The dual cone of \(K\) is defined by
\(K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}.\)
1. Compute the dual cone \(K^*\).
2. Express \(K^*\) explicitly as a subset of \(\mathbf{R}^2\).

problem:
Exercise 2.1
Maximum of a convex function over a polyhedron. Show that the maximum of a convex function $f$ over the polyhedron $P=\operatorname{conv}\{v_1,\ldots,v_k\}$ is achieved at one of its vertices, \emph{i.e.},

\[
\sup_{x\in P} f(x)=\max_{i=1,\ldots,k} f(v_i).
\]

(A stronger statement is: the maximum of a convex function over a closed bounded convex set is achieved at an extreme point, \emph{i.e.}, a point in the set that is not a convex combination of any other points in the set.) \emph{Hint.} Assume the statement is false, and use Jensen's inequality.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.1
Maximum of a convex function over a polyhedron. Show that the maximum of a convex function \(f\) over the polyhedron \(P=\operatorname{conv}\{v_1,\ldots,v_k\}\) is achieved at one of its vertices, \emph{i.e.},

\(\sup_{x\in P} f(x)=\max_{i=1,\ldots,k} f(v_i).\)

(A stronger statement is: the maximum of a convex function over a closed bounded convex set is achieved at an extreme point, \emph{i.e.}, a point in the set that is not a convex combination of any other points in the set.) \emph{Hint.} Assume the statement is false, and use Jensen's inequality.
problem_standardized_math:
Show that if \(P=\operatorname{conv}\{v_1,\ldots,v_k\}\), then the maximum of a convex function \(f\) over \(P\) is attained at one of the vertices \(v_1,\ldots,v_k\); that is,
\(\sup_{x\in P} f(x)=\max_{i=1,\ldots,k} f(v_i).\)
A stronger statement is that the maximum of a convex function over a closed bounded convex set is attained at an extreme point, i.e., a point in the set that is not a convex combination of any other points in the set.

Hint: Assume the statement is false and use Jensen's inequality.
problem_finally:
Let \(v_1,\ldots,v_k \in \mathbf{R}^n\) and define
\(P = \operatorname{conv}\{v_1,\ldots,v_k\}.\)
Let \(f : P \to \mathbf{R}\) be convex.

1. Prove that the maximum of \(f\) on \(P\) is attained at a vertex \(v_i\). Equivalently, show that
\(\sup_{x \in P} f(x) = \max_{1 \le i \le k} f(v_i).\)

2. Let \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\) be nonempty, closed, bounded, and convex, and let \(f : C \to \mathbf{R}\) be convex. Prove that the maximum of \(f\) on \(C\) is attained at an extreme point of \(C\), where \(x \in C\) is an extreme point if there do not exist distinct \(y,z \in C\) and \(\lambda \in (0,1)\) such that
\(x = \lambda y + (1-\lambda)z.\)

You may use Jensen's inequality: for any \(x_1,\ldots,x_m \in P\) (or \(C\)) and any \(\lambda_1,\ldots,\lambda_m \ge 0\) with \(\sum_{i=1}^m \lambda_i = 1\),
\(f\left(\sum_{i=1}^m \lambda_i x_i\right) \le \sum_{i=1}^m \lambda_i f(x_i).\)

Hint: argue by contradiction by assuming that a maximizer is not a vertex in part 1 or not an extreme point in part 2, and apply Jensen's inequality.

problem:
Exercise 2.2
\emph{A general vector composition rule.} Suppose

\[
f(x)=h(g_1(x),g_2(x),\ldots,g_k(x))
\]

where $h:\mathbf{R}^k\to\mathbf{R}$ is convex, and $g_i:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$. Suppose that for each $i$, one of the following holds:

- $h$ is nondecreasing in the $i$th argument, and $g_i$ is convex

- $h$ is nonincreasing in the $i$th argument, and $g_i$ is concave

- $g_i$ is affine.

Show that $f$ is convex. (This composition rule subsumes all the ones given in the book, and is the one used in software systems such as CVX.) You can assume that $\operatorname{dom} h=\mathbf{R}^k$; the result also holds in the general case when the monotonicity conditions listed above are imposed on $\tilde h$, the extended-valued extension of $h$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.2
\emph{A general vector composition rule.} Suppose

\(f(x)=h(g_1(x),g_2(x),\ldots,g_k(x))\)

where \(h:\mathbf{R}^k\to\mathbf{R}\) is convex, and \(g_i:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\). Suppose that for each \(i\), one of the following holds:

- \(h\) is nondecreasing in the \(i\)th argument, and \(g_i\) is convex

- \(h\) is nonincreasing in the \(i\)th argument, and \(g_i\) is concave

- \(g_i\) is affine.

Show that \(f\) is convex. (This composition rule subsumes all the ones given in the book, and is the one used in software systems such as CVX.) You can assume that \(\operatorname{dom} h=\mathbf{R}^k\); the result also holds in the general case when the monotonicity conditions listed above are imposed on \(\tilde h\), the extended-valued extension of \(h\).
problem_finally:
Let \(h:\mathbf{R}^k \to \mathbf{R}\) be convex, and let \(g_i:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) for \(i=1,\ldots,k\). Define
\(f(x)=h\bigl(g_1(x),\ldots,g_k(x)\bigr), \qquad x \in \mathbf{R}^n.\)
Assume \(\operatorname{dom} h=\mathbf{R}^k\). For each \(i \in \{1,\ldots,k\}\), assume at least one of the following:
1. \(h\) is nondecreasing in its \(i\)th argument, and \(g_i\) is convex.
2. \(h\) is nonincreasing in its \(i\)th argument, and \(g_i\) is concave.
3. \(g_i\) is affine.

The monotonicity conditions mean:
- \(h\) is nondecreasing in its \(i\)th argument if, for all \(u,v \in \mathbf{R}^k\) such that \(u_j=v_j\) for all \(j \ne i\) and \(u_i \le v_i\), one has \(h(u) \le h(v)\).
- \(h\) is nonincreasing in its \(i\)th argument if, under the same conditions, one has \(h(u) \ge h(v)\).

Show that \(f\) is convex on \(\mathbf{R}^n\).

Remark: The same conclusion holds when \(\operatorname{dom} h \subseteq \mathbf{R}^k\), provided the above monotonicity assumptions are imposed on the extended-value extension \(\tilde h:\mathbf{R}^k \to \mathbf{R} \cup \{+\infty\}\) of \(h\).

problem:
Exercise 2.3
\emph{Logarithmic barrier for the second-order cone.} The function $f(x,t)=-\log(t^2-x^Tx)$, with $\operatorname{dom} f=\{(x,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times\mathbf{R}\mid t>\|x\|_2\}$ (\emph{i.e.}, the second-order cone), is convex. (The function $f$ is called the logarithmic barrier function for the second-order cone.) This can be shown many ways, for example by evaluating the Hessian and demonstrating that it is positive semidefinite. In this exercise you establish convexity of $f$ using a relatively painless method, leveraging some composition rules and known convexity of a few other functions.

(a) Explain why $t-(1 / t) u^T u$ is a concave function on $\operatorname{dom} f$. \emph{Hint.} Use convexity of the quadratic over linear function.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.3
\emph{Logarithmic barrier for the second-order cone.} The function \(f(x,t)=-\log(t^2-x^Tx)\), with \(\operatorname{dom} f=\{(x,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times\mathbf{R}\mid t>\|x\|_2\}\) (\emph{i.e.}, the second-order cone), is convex. (The function \(f\) is called the logarithmic barrier function for the second-order cone.) This can be shown many ways, for example by evaluating the Hessian and demonstrating that it is positive semidefinite. In this exercise you establish convexity of \(f\) using a relatively painless method, leveraging some composition rules and known convexity of a few other functions.

(a) Explain why \(t-(1 / t) u^T u\) is a concave function on \(\operatorname{dom} f\). \emph{Hint.} Use convexity of the quadratic over linear function.
problem_standardized_math:
Let
\[
f(x,t)=-\log\bigl(t^2-x^T x\bigr),
\qquad
\operatorname{dom} f=\{(x,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times \mathbf{R}\mid t>\|x\|_2\}.
\]
Explain why
\(t-\frac{1}{t}u^T u\)
is a concave function on \(\operatorname{dom} f\). (Hint: use the convexity of the quadratic-over-linear function.)
problem_finally:
Consider
\[
f(x,t) = -\log\left(t^2 - x^T x\right),
\qquad
\operatorname{dom} f = \left\{(x,t) \in \mathbf{R}^n \times \mathbf{R} \mid t > \|x\|_2\right\}.
\]
Using the fact that the quadratic-over-linear function
\((u,t) \mapsto \frac{u^T u}{t}\)
is convex on
\(\left\{(u,t) \in \mathbf{R}^n \times \mathbf{R} \mid t > 0\right\},\)
explain why the function
\((u,t) \mapsto t - \frac{1}{t} u^T u\)
is concave on its natural domain
\(\left\{(u,t) \in \mathbf{R}^n \times \mathbf{R} \mid t > 0\right\}.\)
Relate this conclusion to the domain condition
\(t > \|x\|_2,\)
which implies
\(t > 0.\)

problem:
Exercise 2.3
\emph{Logarithmic barrier for the second-order cone.} The function $f(x,t)=-\log(t^2-x^Tx)$, with $\operatorname{dom} f=\{(x,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times\mathbf{R}\mid t>\|x\|_2\}$ (\emph{i.e.}, the second-order cone), is convex. (The function $f$ is called the logarithmic barrier function for the second-order cone.) This can be shown many ways, for example by evaluating the Hessian and demonstrating that it is positive semidefinite. In this exercise you establish convexity of $f$ using a relatively painless method, leveraging some composition rules and known convexity of a few other functions.

(b) From this, show that $-\log(t-(1/t)u^Tu)$ is a convex function on $\operatorname{dom} f$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.3
\emph{Logarithmic barrier for the second-order cone.} The function \(f(x,t)=-\log(t^2-x^Tx)\), with \(\operatorname{dom} f=\{(x,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times\mathbf{R}\mid t>\|x\|_2\}\) (\emph{i.e.}, the second-order cone), is convex. (The function \(f\) is called the logarithmic barrier function for the second-order cone.) This can be shown many ways, for example by evaluating the Hessian and demonstrating that it is positive semidefinite. In this exercise you establish convexity of \(f\) using a relatively painless method, leveraging some composition rules and known convexity of a few other functions.

(b) From this, show that \(-\log(t-(1/t)u^Tu)\) is a convex function on \(\operatorname{dom} f\).
problem_standardized_math:
Let
\[\nf(x,t)=-\log\bigl(t^2-x^Tx\bigr),
\qquad
\operatorname{dom} f=\{(x,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times \mathbf{R}\mid t>\\|x\\|_2\}.
\]
Given that \(f\) is convex on its domain, show that
\(-\log\left(t-\frac{1}{t}u^Tu\right)\)\nis a convex function on
\(\operatorname{dom} f=\{(u,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times \mathbf{R}\mid t>\\|u\\|_2\}.\)
problem_finally:
Let
\[\nf(x,t)=-\log\left(t^2-x^T x\right),
\qquad
\operatorname{dom} f=
\left\{(x,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times \mathbf{R}\mid t>\\|x\\|_2\right\}.
\]
Assume \(f\) is convex on \(\operatorname{dom} f\).

Show that the function
\(g(u,t)=-\log\left(t-\frac{1}{t}u^T u\right)\)\nis convex on
\[
\operatorname{dom} g=
\left\{(u,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times \mathbf{R}\mid t>\\|u\\|_2\right\}.
\]
Your argument must also verify that \(g(u,t)\) is well defined for all \((u,t)\in \operatorname{dom} g\).

problem:
Exercise 2.3
\emph{Logarithmic barrier for the second-order cone.} The function $f(x,t)=-\log(t^2-x^Tx)$, with $\operatorname{dom} f=\{(x,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times\mathbf{R}\mid t>\|x\|_2\}$ (\emph{i.e.}, the second-order cone), is convex. (The function $f$ is called the logarithmic barrier function for the second-order cone.) This can be shown many ways, for example by evaluating the Hessian and demonstrating that it is positive semidefinite. In this exercise you establish convexity of $f$ using a relatively painless method, leveraging some composition rules and known convexity of a few other functions.

(c) From this, show that $f$ is convex.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.3
\emph{Logarithmic barrier for the second-order cone.} The function \(f(x,t)=-\log(t^2-x^Tx)\), with \(\operatorname{dom} f=\{(x,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times\mathbf{R}\mid t>\|x\|_2\}\) (\emph{i.e.}, the second-order cone), is convex. (The function \(f\) is called the logarithmic barrier function for the second-order cone.) This can be shown many ways, for example by evaluating the Hessian and demonstrating that it is positive semidefinite. In this exercise you establish convexity of \(f\) using a relatively painless method, leveraging some composition rules and known convexity of a few other functions.

(c) From this, show that \(f\) is convex.
problem_standardized_math:
Let
\(f(x,t)=-\log(t^2-x^Tx),\)\nwith domain
\(\operatorname{dom} f=\{(x,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times\mathbf{R}\mid t>\\|x\\|_2\}.\)
Using the preceding results, show that \(f\) is convex.
problem_finally:
Let \(n \in \mathbf{N}\), and define \(f : \mathbf{R}^n \times \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{R}\) by
\(f(x,t) = -\log\left(t^2 - x^T x\right),\)\nwith domain
\(\operatorname{dom} f = \{(x,t) \in \mathbf{R}^n \times \mathbf{R} \mid t > \\|x\\|_2\}.\)
Prove that \(f\) is convex on \(\operatorname{dom} f\), i.e., on the set
\(\{(x,t) \in \mathbf{R}^n \times \mathbf{R} \mid t > \\|x\\|_2\}.\)
You may use previously established convexity-preserving operations and previously proved convex functions.

problem:
Exercise 2.4
\emph{A quadratic-over-linear composition theorem.} Suppose that $f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ is nonnegative and convex, and $g:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ is positive and concave. Show that the function $f^2/g$, with domain $\operatorname{dom} f \cap \operatorname{dom} g$, is convex.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.4
\emph{A quadratic-over-linear composition theorem.} Suppose that \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) is nonnegative and convex, and \(g:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) is positive and concave. Show that the function \(f^2/g\), with domain \(\operatorname{dom} f \cap \operatorname{dom} g\), is convex.
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be nonnegative and convex, and let \(g:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be positive and concave. Show that the function
\(x \mapsto \frac{f(x)^2}{g(x)}\)\nwith domain \(\operatorname{dom} f \cap \operatorname{dom} g\) is convex.
problem_finally:
Let \(f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be convex with \(f(x) \ge 0\) for all \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\). Let \(g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be concave with \(g(x) > 0\) for all \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\). Define
\(h(x) = \frac{f(x)^2}{g(x)}\)\nfor all \(x \in \operatorname{dom} f \cap \operatorname{dom} g\).

Prove that \(h\) is convex on \(\operatorname{dom} f \cap \operatorname{dom} g\); equivalently, show that for all \(x,y \in \operatorname{dom} f \cap \operatorname{dom} g\) and all \(t \in [0,1]\),
\(h(tx + (1-t)y) \le t\,h(x) + (1-t)\,h(y).\)

problem:
Exercise 2.5
\emph{A perspective composition rule} [Maréchal]. Let $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ be a convex function with $f(0)\leq 0$.

(a) Show that the perspective $t f(x/t)$, with domain $\{(x,t)\mid t>0,\ x/t\in \operatorname{dom} f\}$, is nonincreasing as a function of $t$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.5
\emph{A perspective composition rule} [Maréchal]. Let \(f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\) be a convex function with \(f(0)\leq 0\).

(a) Show that the perspective \(t f(x/t)\), with domain \(\{(x,t)\mid t>0,\ x/t\in \operatorname{dom} f\}\), is nonincreasing as a function of \(t\).
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be a convex function such that \(f(0) \le 0\). Show that the perspective function
\((x,t) \mapsto t\,f(x/t),\)
with domain
\(\{(x,t) \mid t>0,\ x/t \in \operatorname{dom} f\},\)
is nonincreasing as a function of \(t\).
problem_finally:
Let \(f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be convex and satisfy \(f(0) \le 0\). Define the perspective function
\(g(x,t) = t\,f(x/t)\)\nfor all \((x,t)\) such that \(t > 0\) and \(x/t \in \operatorname{dom} f\).
1. Prove that for each fixed \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\), the map \(t \mapsto g(x,t)\) is nonincreasing on its domain.
2. Equivalently, show that if \(0 < t_1 \le t_2\) and
\((x,t_1), (x,t_2) \in \{(x,t) \mid t > 0,\ x/t \in \operatorname{dom} f\},\)\nthen
\(t_2 f(x/t_2) \le t_1 f(x/t_1).\)

problem:
Exercise 2.5
\emph{A perspective composition rule} [Maréchal]. Let $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ be a convex function with $f(0)\leq 0$.

(b) Let $g$ be concave and positive on its domain. Show that the function

\[
h(x)=g(x)f(x/g(x)), \qquad \operatorname{dom} h=\{x\in \operatorname{dom} g\mid x/g(x)\in \operatorname{dom} f\}
\]

is convex.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.5
\emph{A perspective composition rule} [Maréchal]. Let \(f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\) be a convex function with \(f(0)\leq 0\).

(b) Let \(g\) be concave and positive on its domain. Show that the function

\(h(x)=g(x)f(x/g(x)), \qquad \operatorname{dom} h=\{x\in \operatorname{dom} g\mid x/g(x)\in \operatorname{dom} f\}\)

is convex.
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be convex and satisfy \(f(0)\le 0\). Let \(g\) be concave and positive on its domain. Show that the function
\(h(x)=g(x)\,f\!\left(\frac{x}{g(x)}\right),\qquad \operatorname{dom} h=\{x\in \operatorname{dom} g \mid x/g(x)\in \operatorname{dom} f\},\)\nis convex.
problem_finally:
Let \(f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be convex with \(f(0) \le 0\). Let \(g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be concave and satisfy
\(g(x) > 0 \quad \text{for all } x \in \operatorname{dom} g.\)
Define
\(h(x) = g(x)\,f\!\left(\frac{x}{g(x)}\right),\)\nwith domain
\(\operatorname{dom} h = \left\{ x \in \operatorname{dom} g \mid \frac{x}{g(x)} \in \operatorname{dom} f \right\}.\)
Prove that \(h\) is convex on \(\operatorname{dom} h\); equivalently, show that for all \(x,y \in \operatorname{dom} h\) and all \(\theta \in [0,1]\) such that \(\theta x + (1-\theta)y \in \operatorname{dom} h\),
\(h(\theta x + (1-\theta)y) \le \theta h(x) + (1-\theta)h(y).\)

problem:
Exercise 2.5
\emph{A perspective composition rule} [Maréchal]. Let $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ be a convex function with $f(0)\leq 0$.

(c) As an example, show that

\[
h(x)=\frac{x^Tx}{\left(\prod_{k=1}^n x_k\right)^{1/n}}, \qquad \operatorname{dom} h=\mathbf{R}_{++}^n
\]

is convex.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.5
\emph{A perspective composition rule} [Maréchal]. Let \(f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\) be a convex function with \(f(0)\leq 0\).

(c) As an example, show that

\(h(x)=\frac{x^Tx}{\left(\prod_{k=1}^n x_k\right)^{1/n}}, \qquad \operatorname{dom} h=\mathbf{R}_{++}^n\)

is convex.
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be a convex function with \(f(0) \le 0\). As an example of the perspective composition rule, show that the function
\[
h(x)=\frac{x^T x}{\left(\prod_{k=1}^n x_k\right)^{1/n}},
\qquad \operatorname{dom} h=\mathbf{R}_{++}^n,
\]
is convex.
problem_finally:
Let
\[\nh(x)=\frac{x^T x}{\left(\prod_{k=1}^n x_k\right)^{1/n}},
\qquad x=(x_1,\dots,x_n) \in \mathbf{R}_{++}^n,
\]\nwhere
\(\mathbf{R}_{++}^n=\{x\in \mathbf{R}^n \mid x_k>0 \text{ for } k=1,\dots,n\}.\)
Assume \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) is convex and satisfies \(f(0) \le 0\).

Prove, using the perspective composition rule for convex functions, that \(h:\mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) is convex on \(\mathbf{R}_{++}^n\).

problem:
Exercise 2.6
Perspective of log determinant. Show that $f(X,t)=nt\log t-t\log\det X$, with $\operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{S}_{++}^n\times \mathbf{R}_{++}$, is convex in $(X,t)$. Use this to show that

\[
g(X)=n(\operatorname{tr}X)\log(\operatorname{tr}X)-(\operatorname{tr}X)(\log\det X)
\]

\[
= n\left(\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i\right)\left(\log\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i-\sum_{i=1}^n \log\lambda_i\right),
\]

where $\lambda_i$ are the eigenvalues of $X$, is convex on $\mathbf{S}_{++}^n$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.6
Perspective of log determinant. Show that \(f(X,t)=nt\log t-t\log\det X\), with \(\operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{S}_{++}^n\times \mathbf{R}_{++}\), is convex in \((X,t)\). Use this to show that

\(g(X)=n(\operatorname{tr}X)\log(\operatorname{tr}X)-(\operatorname{tr}X)(\log\det X)\)

\(= n\left(\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i\right)\left(\log\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i-\sum_{i=1}^n \log\lambda_i\right),\)

where \(\lambda_i\) are the eigenvalues of \(X\), is convex on \(\mathbf{S}_{++}^n\).
problem_standardized_math:
Show that the function
\[
f(X,t)=nt\log t-t\log\det X,
\qquad \operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{S}_{++}^n\times \mathbf{R}_{++},
\]
is convex in \((X,t)\). Use this to show that the function
\(g(X)=n(\operatorname{tr}X)\log(\operatorname{tr}X)-(\operatorname{tr}X)(\log\det X)\)
which can be written as
\(g(X)=n\left(\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i\right)\left(\log\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i-\sum_{i=1}^n \log \lambda_i\right),\)
where \(\lambda_i\) are the eigenvalues of \(X\), is convex on \(\mathbf{S}_{++}^n\).
problem_finally:
Let \(\mathbf{S}_{++}^n\) be the set of real symmetric positive definite \(n \times n\) matrices, and let \(\mathbf{R}_{++} = \{t \in \mathbf{R} : t > 0\}\). Define
\[
f : \mathbf{S}_{++}^n \times \mathbf{R}_{++} \to \mathbf{R},
\qquad
f(X,t) = n t \log t - t \log \det X.
\]

Rewrite and prove the following statements.

1. Show that \(f\) is jointly convex on \(\mathbf{S}_{++}^n \times \mathbf{R}_{++}\).

2. Define \(g : \mathbf{S}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) by
\(g(X) = n \, \operatorname{tr}(X) \log(\operatorname{tr}(X)) - \operatorname{tr}(X) \log \det X.\)
Using the result of item 1, show that \(g\) is convex on \(\mathbf{S}_{++}^n\).

3. Let \(\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_n > 0\) be the eigenvalues of \(X \in \mathbf{S}_{++}^n\). Since
\[
\operatorname{tr}(X) = \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i,
\qquad
\log \det X = \sum_{i=1}^n \log \lambda_i,
\]
express \(g(X)\) in terms of \(\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_n\) to obtain its eigenvalue representation:
\[
g(X) = n \left(\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i\right)
\left(
\log\left(\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i\right) - \sum_{i=1}^n \log \lambda_i
\right).
\]
Interpret this as the eigenvalue form of \(g\).

problem:
Exercise 2.7
Pre-composition with a linear fractional mapping. Suppose $f:\mathbf{R}^m\to\mathbf{R}$ is convex, and $A\in\mathbf{R}^{m\times n}$, $b\in\mathbf{R}^m$, $c\in\mathbf{R}^n$, and $d\in\mathbf{R}$. Show that $g:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$, defined by

\[
g(x)=(c^Tx+d)\,f((Ax+b)/(c^Tx+d)), \qquad \operatorname{dom} g=\{x\mid c^Tx+d>0\},
\]

is convex.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.7
Pre-composition with a linear fractional mapping. Suppose \(f:\mathbf{R}^m\to\mathbf{R}\) is convex, and \(A\in\mathbf{R}^{m\times n}\), \(b\in\mathbf{R}^m\), \(c\in\mathbf{R}^n\), and \(d\in\mathbf{R}\). Show that \(g:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\), defined by

\(g(x)=(c^Tx+d)\,f((Ax+b)/(c^Tx+d)), \qquad \operatorname{dom} g=\{x\mid c^Tx+d>0\},\)

is convex.
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(f:\mathbf{R}^m \to \mathbf{R}\) be convex, and let \(A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}\), \(b \in \mathbf{R}^m\), \(c \in \mathbf{R}^n\), and \(d \in \mathbf{R}\). Define \(g:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) by
\[\ng(x) = (c^T x + d)\, f\\!\left(\frac{Ax+b}{c^T x + d}\right),
\qquad \operatorname{dom} g = \{x \mid c^T x + d > 0\}.
\]
Show that \(g\) is convex.
problem_finally:
Let \(f : \mathbf{R}^m \to \mathbf{R}\) be convex. Let \(A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}\), \(b \in \mathbf{R}^m\), \(c \in \mathbf{R}^n\), and \(d \in \mathbf{R}\). Define \(g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) on
\(\operatorname{dom} g = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid c^T x + d > 0\}\)\nby
\(g(x) = (c^T x + d)\, f\!\left(\frac{Ax+b}{c^T x + d}\right), \qquad x \in \operatorname{dom} g.\)
Prove that \(g\) is convex on \(\operatorname{dom} g\); equivalently, show that for all \(x,y \in \operatorname{dom} g\) and all \(\theta \in [0,1]\),
\(g(\theta x + (1-\theta)y) \le \theta g(x) + (1-\theta) g(y).\)

problem:
Exercise 2.8
Scalar valued linear fractional functions. A function $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ is called linear fractional if it has the form $f(x)=(a^T x+b)/(c^T x+d)$, with $\operatorname{dom} f=\{x\mid c^T x+d>0\}$. When is a linear fractional function convex? When is a linear fractional function quasiconvex?
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.8
Scalar valued linear fractional functions. A function \(f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\) is called linear fractional if it has the form \(f(x)=(a^T x+b)/(c^T x+d)\), with \(\operatorname{dom} f=\{x\mid c^T x+d>0\}\). When is a linear fractional function convex? When is a linear fractional function quasiconvex?
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be a linear fractional function of the form
\(f(x)=\frac{a^T x+b}{c^T x+d},\)\nwith domain
\(\operatorname{dom} f=\{x \mid c^T x+d>0\}.\)
Determine when a linear fractional function is convex, and when it is quasiconvex.
problem_finally:
Let \(a,c \in \mathbf{R}^n\) and \(b,d \in \mathbf{R}\). Define
\(f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}, \qquad f(x) = \frac{a^T x + b}{c^T x + d},\)\nwith domain
\(\operatorname{dom} f = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid c^T x + d > 0\},\)\nand assume \(\operatorname{dom} f \ne \varnothing\).

Characterize, in terms of \(a,b,c,d\), the necessary and sufficient conditions under which \(f\) is:

1. convex on \(\operatorname{dom} f\);
2. quasiconvex on \(\operatorname{dom} f\).

The characterization must be for \(f\) on the stated domain exactly.

problem:
Exercise 2.9
Show that the function

\[
f(x)=\frac{\|Ax-b\|_2^2}{1-x^Tx}
\]

is convex on $\{x \mid \|x\|_2 < 1\}$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.9
Show that the function

\(f(x)=\frac{\|Ax-b\|_2^2}{1-x^Tx}\)

is convex on \(\{x \mid \|x\|_2 < 1\}\).
problem_standardized_math:
Show that the function
\(f(x)=\frac{\|Ax-b\|_2^2}{1-x^T x}\)
is convex on the set
\(\{x\mid \|x\|_2<1\}.\)
problem_finally:
Let \(A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}\) and \(b \in \mathbf{R}^m\) be fixed. Define
\(f : D \to \mathbf{R}, \qquad f(x) = \frac{\|Ax - b\|_2^2}{1 - x^T x},\)
where
\(D = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid \|x\|_2 < 1\}.\)
Note that for all \(x \in D\), \(1 - x^T x > 0\). Prove that \(f\) is convex on \(D\).

problem:
Exercise 2.10
Weighted geometric mean. The geometric mean $f(x)=\left(\prod_k x_k\right)^{1/n}$ with $\operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{R}_{++}^n$ is concave, as shown on page 74. Extend the proof to show that

\[
f(x)=\prod_{k=1}^n x_k^{\alpha_k}, \qquad \operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{R}_{++}^n
\]

is concave, where $\alpha_k$ are nonnegative numbers with $\sum_k \alpha_k\leq 1$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.10
Weighted geometric mean. The geometric mean \(f(x)=\left(\prod_k x_k\right)^{1/n}\) with \(\operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{R}_{++}^n\) is concave, as shown on page 74. Extend the proof to show that

\(f(x)=\prod_{k=1}^n x_k^{\alpha_k}, \qquad \operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{R}_{++}^n\)

is concave, where \(\alpha_k\) are nonnegative numbers with \(\sum_k \alpha_k\leq 1\).
problem_standardized_math:
Show that the weighted geometric mean
\(f(x)=\prod_{k=1}^n x_k^{\alpha_k}, \qquad \operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{R}_{++}^n,\)
is concave, where \(\alpha_k \ge 0\) for all \(k\) and \(\sum_{k=1}^n \alpha_k \le 1\).
problem_finally:
Let \(n \in \mathbf{N}\). Let \(\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_n \in \mathbf{R}\) satisfy
\(\alpha_k \ge 0 \quad \text{for } k=1,\ldots,n, \qquad \sum_{k=1}^n \alpha_k \le 1.\)
Define \(f : \mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) by
\(f(x) = \prod_{k=1}^n x_k^{\alpha_k}, \qquad x = (x_1,\ldots,x_n).\)
Prove that \(f\) is concave on \(\mathbf{R}_{++}^n\); equivalently, show that for all \(x,y \in \mathbf{R}_{++}^n\) and all \(\theta \in [0,1]\),
\(f(\theta x + (1-\theta)y) \ge \theta f(x) + (1-\theta)f(y).\)

problem:
Exercise 2.11
Suppose that $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ is convex, and define

\[
g(x,t)=f(x/t), \qquad \operatorname{dom} g=\{(x,t)\mid x/t\in \operatorname{dom} f,\ t>0\}.
\]

Show that $g$ is quasiconvex.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.11
Suppose that \(f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\) is convex, and define

\(g(x,t)=f(x/t), \qquad \operatorname{dom} g=\{(x,t)\mid x/t\in \operatorname{dom} f,\ t>0\}.\)

Show that \(g\) is quasiconvex.
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be convex, and define
\(g(x,t)=f(x/t), \qquad \operatorname{dom} g=\{(x,t)\mid x/t\in \operatorname{dom} f,\ t>0\}.\)
Show that \(g\) is quasiconvex.
problem_finally:
Let \(f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be convex, with domain \(\operatorname{dom} f \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\). Define
\(g : \mathbf{R}^n \times \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{R}, \qquad g(x,t) = f\left(\frac{x}{t}\right),\)
with domain
\(\operatorname{dom} g = \left\{(x,t) \in \mathbf{R}^n \times \mathbf{R} \mid t > 0,\ \frac{x}{t} \in \operatorname{dom} f \right\}.\)
Prove that \(g\) is quasiconvex on \(\operatorname{dom} g\); equivalently, show that for every \(\alpha \in \mathbf{R}\), the set
\(\left\{(x,t) \in \operatorname{dom} g \mid g(x,t) \le \alpha \right\}\)
is convex in \(\mathbf{R}^n \times \mathbf{R}\).

problem:
Exercise 2.12
Continued fraction function. Show that the function

\[
f(x)=\frac{1}{x_1-\frac{1}{x_2-\frac{1}{x_3-\frac{1}{x_4}}}}
\]

defined where every denominator is positive, is convex and decreasing. (There is nothing special about $n=4$ here; the same holds for any number of variables.)
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.12
Continued fraction function. Show that the function

\(f(x)=\frac{1}{x_1-\frac{1}{x_2-\frac{1}{x_3-\frac{1}{x_4}}}}\)

defined where every denominator is positive, is convex and decreasing. (There is nothing special about \(n=4\) here; the same holds for any number of variables.)
problem_standardized_math:
Show that the continued-fraction function
\(f(x)=\frac{1}{x_1-\frac{1}{x_2-\frac{1}{x_3-\frac{1}{x_4}}}}\)
defined on the set where every denominator is positive, is convex and decreasing. (There is nothing special about the case of four variables; the same statement holds for any number of variables.)
problem_finally:
Show that the continued-fraction function
\(f(x)=\frac{1}{x_1-\frac{1}{x_2-\frac{1}{x_3-\frac{1}{x_4}}}}\)
defined on the set where every denominator is positive, is convex and decreasing. (There is nothing special about the case of four variables; the same statement holds for any number of variables.)

problem:
Exercise 2.13
Circularly symmetric Huber function. The scalar Huber function is defined as

\[
f_{\mathrm{hub}}(x)=
\begin{cases}
(1/2)x^2 & |x|\leq 1\\
|x|-1/2 & |x|>1.
\end{cases}
\]

This convex function comes up in several applications, including robust estimation. This problem concerns generalizations of the Huber function to $\mathbf{R}^n$. One generalization to $\mathbf{R}^n$ is given by $f_{\mathrm{hub}}(x_1)+\cdots+f_{\mathrm{hub}}(x_n)$, but this function is not circularly symmetric, i.e., invariant under transformation of $x$ by an orthogonal matrix. A generalization to $\mathbf{R}^n$ that is circularly symmetric is

\[
f_{\mathrm{cshub}}(x)=f_{\mathrm{hub}}(\|x\|)=
\begin{cases}
(1/2)\|x\|_2^2 & \|x\|_2\leq 1\\
\|x\|_2-1/2 & \|x\|_2>1.
\end{cases}
\]

(The subscript stands for `circularly symmetric Huber function'.) Show that $f_{\mathrm{cshub}}$ is convex. Find the conjugate function $f_{\mathrm{cshub}}^*$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.13
Circularly symmetric Huber function. The scalar Huber function is defined as

\[
f_{\mathrm{hub}}(x)=
\begin{cases}
(1/2)x^2 & |x|\leq 1\\
|x|-1/2 & |x|>1.
\end{cases}
\]

This convex function comes up in several applications, including robust estimation. This problem concerns generalizations of the Huber function to \(\mathbf{R}^n\). One generalization to \(\mathbf{R}^n\) is given by \(f_{\mathrm{hub}}(x_1)+\cdots+f_{\mathrm{hub}}(x_n)\), but this function is not circularly symmetric, i.e., invariant under transformation of \(x\) by an orthogonal matrix. A generalization to \(\mathbf{R}^n\) that is circularly symmetric is

\[
f_{\mathrm{cshub}}(x)=f_{\mathrm{hub}}(\|x\|)=
\begin{cases}
(1/2)\|x\|_2^2 & \|x\|_2\leq 1\\
\|x\|_2-1/2 & \|x\|_2>1.
\end{cases}
\]

(The subscript stands for `circularly symmetric Huber function'.) Show that \(f_{\mathrm{cshub}}\) is convex. Find the conjugate function \(f_{\mathrm{cshub}}^*\).
problem_standardized_math:
Let the scalar Huber function be defined by
\[
f_{\mathrm{hub}}(x)=
\begin{cases}
\frac{1}{2}x^2, & |x|\leq 1,\\
|x|-\frac{1}{2}, & |x|>1.
\end{cases}
\]
Define the circularly symmetric Huber function on \(\mathbf{R}^n\) by
\[
f_{\mathrm{cshub}}(x)=f_{\mathrm{hub}}(\|x\|)=
\begin{cases}
\frac{1}{2}\|x\|_2^2, & \|x\|_2\leq 1,\\
\|x\|_2-\frac{1}{2}, & \|x\|_2>1.
\end{cases}
\]
Show that \(f_{\mathrm{cshub}}\) is convex, and find its conjugate function \(f_{\mathrm{cshub}}^*\).
problem_finally:
Let the scalar Huber function \(f_{\mathrm{hub}} : \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{R}\) be defined by
\[\nf_{\mathrm{hub}}(t)=
\begin{cases}
\frac{1}{2}t^2, & |t| \le 1,\\\\
|t|-\frac{1}{2}, & |t| > 1.
\end{cases}
\]
Using the Euclidean norm \(\| \cdot \|_2\) on \(\mathbf{R}^n\), define \(f_{\mathrm{cshub}} : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) by
\[\nf_{\mathrm{cshub}}(x)=f_{\mathrm{hub}}(\|x\|_2)=
\begin{cases}
\frac{1}{2}\|x\|_2^2, & \|x\|_2 \le 1,\\\\
\|x\|_2-\frac{1}{2}, & \|x\|_2 > 1.
\end{cases}
\]
For \(y \in \mathbf{R}^n\), define the convex conjugate
\(f_{\mathrm{cshub}}^*(y)=\sup_{x \in \mathbf{R}^n}\left(y^T x-f_{\mathrm{cshub}}(x)\right).\)
Determine the following:
1. Prove that \(f_{\mathrm{cshub}}\) is convex on \(\mathbf{R}^n\).
2. Compute \(f_{\mathrm{cshub}}^*\) explicitly.

problem:
Exercise 2.14
Reverse Jensen inequality. Suppose $f$ is convex, $\lambda_1>0$, $\lambda_i\leq 0$, $i=2,\ldots,k$, and $\lambda_1+\cdots+\lambda_n=1$, and let $x_1,\ldots,x_n\in\operatorname{dom} f$. Show that the inequality

\[
f(\lambda_1x_1+\cdots+\lambda_nx_n)\geq \lambda_1f(x_1)+\cdots+\lambda_nf(x_n)
\]

always holds. Hints. Draw a picture for the $n=2$ case first. For the general case, express $x_1$ as a convex combination of $\lambda_1x_1+\cdots+\lambda_nx_n$ and $x_2,\ldots,x_n$, and use Jensen's inequality.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.14
Reverse Jensen inequality. Suppose \(f\) is convex, \(\lambda_1>0\), \(\lambda_i\leq 0\), \(i=2,\ldots,k\), and \(\lambda_1+\cdots+\lambda_n=1\), and let \(x_1,\ldots,x_n\in\operatorname{dom} f\). Show that the inequality

\(f(\lambda_1x_1+\cdots+\lambda_nx_n)\geq \lambda_1f(x_1)+\cdots+\lambda_nf(x_n)\)

always holds. Hints. Draw a picture for the \(n=2\) case first. For the general case, express \(x_1\) as a convex combination of \(\lambda_1x_1+\cdots+\lambda_nx_n\) and \(x_2,\ldots,x_n\), and use Jensen's inequality.
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(f\) be convex. Suppose \(\lambda_1>0\), \(\lambda_i\leq 0\) for \(i=2,\ldots,k\), and \(\lambda_1+\cdots+\lambda_n=1\). Let \(x_1,\ldots,x_n\in \operatorname{dom} f\). Show that
\(f(\lambda_1 x_1+\cdots+\lambda_n x_n)\geq \lambda_1 f(x_1)+\cdots+\lambda_n f(x_n).\)
problem_finally:
Let \(f : \mathbf{R}^m \to (-\infty,+\infty]\) be convex, and let \(x_1,\ldots,x_n \in \operatorname{dom} f\), where \(\operatorname{dom} f = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^m : f(x) < +\infty\}\). Let \(\lambda_1,\ldots,\lambda_n \in \mathbf{R}\) satisfy
\(\lambda_1 > 0, \qquad \lambda_i \le 0 \quad \text{for } i=2,\ldots,n, \qquad \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i = 1.\)
Assume that
\(\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i x_i \in \operatorname{dom} f.\)
Prove that
\(f\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i x_i\right) \ge \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i f(x_i).\)

problem:
Exercise 2.15
Monotone extension of a convex function. Suppose $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ is convex. Recall that a function $h:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ is monotone nondecreasing if $h(x)\geq h(y)$ whenever $x\geq y$. The monotone extension of $f$ is defined as

\[
g(x)=\inf_{z\geq 0} f(x+z).
\]

(We will assume that $g(x)>-\infty$.) Show that $g$ is convex and monotone nondecreasing, and satisfies $g(x)\leq f(x)$ for all $x$. Show that if $h$ is any other convex function that satisfies these properties, then $h(x)\leq g(x)$ for all $x$. Thus, $g$ is the maximum convex monotone underestimator of $f$.

Remark. For simple functions (say, on $\mathbf{R}$) it is easy to work out what $g$ is, given $f$. On $\mathbf{R}^n$, it can be very difficult to work out an explicit expression for $g$. However, systems such as CVX can immediately handle functions such as $g$, defined by partial minimization.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.15
Monotone extension of a convex function. Suppose \(f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\) is convex. Recall that a function \(h:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\) is monotone nondecreasing if \(h(x)\geq h(y)\) whenever \(x\geq y\). The monotone extension of \(f\) is defined as

\(g(x)=\inf_{z\geq 0} f(x+z).\)

(We will assume that \(g(x)>-\infty\).) Show that \(g\) is convex and monotone nondecreasing, and satisfies \(g(x)\leq f(x)\) for all \(x\). Show that if \(h\) is any other convex function that satisfies these properties, then \(h(x)\leq g(x)\) for all \(x\). Thus, \(g\) is the maximum convex monotone underestimator of \(f\).

Remark. For simple functions (say, on \(\mathbf{R}\)) it is easy to work out what \(g\) is, given \(f\). On \(\mathbf{R}^n\), it can be very difficult to work out an explicit expression for \(g\). However, systems such as CVX can immediately handle functions such as \(g\), defined by partial minimization.
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be convex. A function \(h:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) is called monotone nondecreasing if \(h(x) \geq h(y)\) whenever \(x \geq y\). Define the monotone extension of \(f\) by
\(g(x)=\inf_{z\geq 0} f(x+z),\)\nand assume that \(g(x)>-\infty\). Show that \(g\) is convex, monotone nondecreasing, and satisfies \(g(x)\leq f(x)\) for all \(x\). Show also that if \(h\) is any other convex function that is monotone nondecreasing and satisfies \(h(x)\leq f(x)\) for all \(x\), then \(h(x)\leq g(x)\) for all \(x\). Thus, \(g\) is the maximum convex monotone underestimator of \(f\).
problem_finally:
Let \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be convex. For \(x,y \in \mathbf{R}^n\), define \(x \ge y\) componentwise, i.e., \(x_i \ge y_i\) for all \(i=1,\dots,n\). A function \(h:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) is monotone nondecreasing if
\(x \ge y \implies h(x) \ge h(y).\)
Define
\(g(x)=\inf\{\,f(x+z): z \in \mathbf{R}^n,\ z \ge 0\,\},\)\nand assume \(g(x)>-\infty\) for every \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\).

Prove:

1. \(g\) is convex on \(\mathbf{R}^n\), monotone nondecreasing, and satisfies
\(g(x)\le f(x)\qquad \forall x \in \mathbf{R}^n.\)

2. If \(h:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) is convex, monotone nondecreasing, and satisfies
\(h(x)\le f(x)\qquad \forall x \in \mathbf{R}^n,\)\nthen
\(h(x)\le g(x)\qquad \forall x \in \mathbf{R}^n.\)

3. Deduce that \(g\) is the largest convex monotone nondecreasing function below \(f\); equivalently, \(g\) is the maximum convex monotone underestimator of \(f\).

problem:
Exercise 2.16
\emph{Circularly symmetric convex functions.} Suppose $f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ is convex and symmetric with respect to rotations, i.e., $f(x)$ depends only on $\|x\|_2$. Show that $f$ must have the form $f(x)=\phi(\|x\|_2)$, where $\phi:\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{R}$ is nondecreasing and convex, with $\operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{R}$. (Conversely, any function of this form is symmetric and convex, so this form characterizes such functions.)
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.16
\emph{Circularly symmetric convex functions.} Suppose \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) is convex and symmetric with respect to rotations, i.e., \(f(x)\) depends only on \(\|x\|_2\). Show that \(f\) must have the form \(f(x)=\phi(\|x\|_2)\), where \(\phi:\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{R}\) is nondecreasing and convex, with \(\operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{R}\). (Conversely, any function of this form is symmetric and convex, so this form characterizes such functions.)
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be a convex function that is invariant under rotations; that is, \(f(x)\) depends only on \(\|x\|_2\). Show that \(f\) can be written in the form
\(f(x)=\phi(\|x\|_2),\)
where \(\phi:\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{R}\) is nondecreasing and convex, and \(\operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{R}\). Conversely, any function of this form is rotation-invariant and convex, so this representation characterizes circularly symmetric convex functions.
problem_finally:
Let \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) satisfy
\[\nf(Qx)=f(x)
\quad \text{for all } x \in \mathbf{R}^n \text{ and all } Q \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n} \text{ with } Q^TQ=I.
\]
Equivalently, \(f\) depends only on \(\|x\|_2\).

1. Assume \(f\) is convex and rotation-invariant. Show that there exists \(\phi:\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{R}\) such that
\(f(x)=\phi(\|x\|_2) \quad \text{for all } x \in \mathbf{R}^n,\)\nand that \(\phi\) can be chosen so that
\(\phi \text{ is convex on } \mathbf{R}, \qquad \phi \text{ is nondecreasing on } [0,\infty), \qquad \operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{R}^n.\)

2. Conversely, let \(\phi:\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{R}\) be convex and nondecreasing on \([0,\infty)\), and define
\(f(x)=\phi(\|x\|_2), \qquad x \in \mathbf{R}^n.\)
Prove that \(f\) is rotation-invariant and convex on \(\mathbf{R}^n\).

3. Conclude that \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) is convex and rotation-invariant if and only if there exists \(\phi:\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{R}\) such that
\(f(x)=\phi(\|x\|_2) \quad \text{for all } x \in \mathbf{R}^n,\)\nwhere \(\phi\) is convex on \(\mathbf{R}\) and nondecreasing on \([0,\infty)\).

problem:
Exercise 2.17
\emph{Infimal convolution.} Let $f_1,\ldots,f_m$ be convex functions on $\mathbf{R}^n$. Their infimal convolution, denoted $g=f_1 \diamond \cdots \diamond f_m$ (several other notations are also used), is defined as

\[
g(x)=\inf\{f_1(x_1)+\cdots+f_m(x_m)\mid x_1+\cdots+x_m=x\},
\]

with the natural domain (i.e., defined by $g(x)<\infty$). In one simple interpretation, $f_i(x_i)$ is the cost for the $i$th firm to produce a mix of products given by $x_i$; $g(x)$ is then the optimal cost obtained if the firms can freely exchange products to produce, all together, the mix given by $x$. (The name `convolution' presumably comes from the observation that if we replace the sum above with the product, and the infimum above with integration, then we obtain the normal convolution.)

(a) Show that $g$ is convex.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.17
\emph{Infimal convolution.} Let \(f_1,\ldots,f_m\) be convex functions on \(\mathbf{R}^n\). Their infimal convolution, denoted \(g=f_1 \diamond \cdots \diamond f_m\) (several other notations are also used), is defined as

\(g(x)=\inf\{f_1(x_1)+\cdots+f_m(x_m)\mid x_1+\cdots+x_m=x\},\)

with the natural domain (i.e., defined by \(g(x)<\infty\)). In one simple interpretation, \(f_i(x_i)\) is the cost for the \(i\)th firm to produce a mix of products given by \(x_i\); \(g(x)\) is then the optimal cost obtained if the firms can freely exchange products to produce, all together, the mix given by \(x\). (The name `convolution' presumably comes from the observation that if we replace the sum above with the product, and the infimum above with integration, then we obtain the normal convolution.)

(a) Show that \(g\) is convex.
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(f_1,\ldots,f_m\) be convex functions on \(\mathbf{R}^n\). Define their infimal convolution \(g=f_1 \diamond \cdots \diamond f_m\) by
\(g(x)=\inf\{f_1(x_1)+\cdots+f_m(x_m)\mid x_1+\cdots+x_m=x\},\)\nwith natural domain \(\{x\mid g(x)<\infty\}\). Show that \(g\) is convex.
problem_finally:
Let \(m \in \mathbf{N}\), and let \(f_1,\ldots,f_m : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} \cup \{+\infty\}\) be convex functions. Define the infimal convolution
\(g = f_1 \diamond \cdots \diamond f_m\)\nby
\(g(x) = \inf \left\{ \sum_{i=1}^m f_i(x_i) \;\middle|\; x_1,\ldots,x_m \in \mathbf{R}^n,\ \sum_{i=1}^m x_i = x \right\}, \qquad x \in \mathbf{R}^n.\)
Its effective domain is
\(\operatorname{dom}(g) = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid g(x) < +\infty\}.\)
Prove that \(g\) is convex on \(\mathbf{R}^n\), i.e., show that for all \(x,y \in \mathbf{R}^n\) and all \(\lambda \in [0,1]\),
\(g(\lambda x + (1-\lambda)y) \le \lambda g(x) + (1-\lambda)g(y).\)

problem:
Exercise 2.17
\emph{Infimal convolution.} Let $f_1,\ldots,f_m$ be convex functions on $\mathbf{R}^n$. Their infimal convolution, denoted $g=f_1 \diamond \cdots \diamond f_m$ (several other notations are also used), is defined as

\[
g(x)=\inf\{f_1(x_1)+\cdots+f_m(x_m)\mid x_1+\cdots+x_m=x\},
\]

with the natural domain (i.e., defined by $g(x)<\infty$). In one simple interpretation, $f_i(x_i)$ is the cost for the $i$th firm to produce a mix of products given by $x_i$; $g(x)$ is then the optimal cost obtained if the firms can freely exchange products to produce, all together, the mix given by $x$. (The name `convolution' presumably comes from the observation that if we replace the sum above with the product, and the infimum above with integration, then we obtain the normal convolution.)

(b) Show that $g^* = f_1^* + \cdots + f_m^*$. In other words, the conjugate of the infimal convolution is the sum of the conjugates.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.17
\emph{Infimal convolution.} Let \(f_1,\ldots,f_m\) be convex functions on \(\mathbf{R}^n\). Their infimal convolution, denoted \(g=f_1 \diamond \cdots \diamond f_m\) (several other notations are also used), is defined as

\(g(x)=\inf\{f_1(x_1)+\cdots+f_m(x_m)\mid x_1+\cdots+x_m=x\},\)

with the natural domain (i.e., defined by \(g(x)<\infty\)). In one simple interpretation, \(f_i(x_i)\) is the cost for the \(i\)th firm to produce a mix of products given by \(x_i\); \(g(x)\) is then the optimal cost obtained if the firms can freely exchange products to produce, all together, the mix given by \(x\). (The name `convolution' presumably comes from the observation that if we replace the sum above with the product, and the infimum above with integration, then we obtain the normal convolution.)

(b) Show that \(g^* = f_1^* + \cdots + f_m^*\). In other words, the conjugate of the infimal convolution is the sum of the conjugates.
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(f_1,\ldots,f_m\) be convex functions on \(\mathbf{R}^n\), and define their infimal convolution \(g=f_1 \diamond \cdots \diamond f_m\) by
\(g(x)=\inf\{f_1(x_1)+\cdots+f_m(x_m)\mid x_1+\cdots+x_m=x\},\)\nwith natural domain \(\{x\mid g(x)<\infty\}\). Show that
\(g^*=f_1^*+\cdots+f_m^*.\)
That is, the conjugate of the infimal convolution is the sum of the conjugates.
problem_finally:
Let \(f_1,\ldots,f_m : \mathbf{R}^n \to (-\infty,\infty]\) be convex functions, where \(m \in \mathbf{N}\). Define the infimal convolution \(g : \mathbf{R}^n \to (-\infty,\infty]\) by
\(g(x)=\inf\left\{\sum_{i=1}^m f_i(x_i) \\;\middle|\\; x_1,\ldots,x_m \in \mathbf{R}^n,\\ \sum_{i=1}^m x_i=x \right\}, \qquad x \in \mathbf{R}^n.\)
Its effective domain is
\(\operatorname{dom} g=\{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid g(x)<\infty\}.\)
For a function \(h : \mathbf{R}^n \to (-\infty,\infty]\), its Fenchel conjugate \(h^* : \mathbf{R}^n \to (-\infty,\infty]\) is defined by
\(h^*(y)=\sup_{x \in \mathbf{R}^n}\{\langle y,x\rangle-h(x)\}, \qquad y \in \mathbf{R}^n.\)
Prove that
\(g^*(y)=\sum_{i=1}^m f_i^*(y), \qquad \forall y \in \mathbf{R}^n.\)

problem:
Exercise 2.18
*Conjugate of composition of convex and linear function.* Suppose $A\in\mathbf{R}^{m\times n}$ with $\operatorname{rank}A=m$, and $g$ is defined as $g(x)=f(Ax)$, where $f:\mathbf{R}^m\to\mathbf{R}$ is convex. Show that

\[
g^*(y)=f^*((A^\dagger)^T y), \qquad \operatorname{dom}(g^*)=A^T\operatorname{dom}(f^*),
\]

where $A^\dagger=(AA^T)^{-1}A$ is the pseudo-inverse of $A$. (This generalizes the formula given on page 95 for the case when $A$ is square and invertible.)
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.18
*Conjugate of composition of convex and linear function.* Suppose \(A\in\mathbf{R}^{m\times n}\) with \(\operatorname{rank}A=m\), and \(g\) is defined as \(g(x)=f(Ax)\), where \(f:\mathbf{R}^m\to\mathbf{R}\) is convex. Show that

\(g^*(y)=f^*((A^\dagger)^T y), \qquad \operatorname{dom}(g^*)=A^T\operatorname{dom}(f^*),\)

where \(A^\dagger=(AA^T)^{-1}A\) is the pseudo-inverse of \(A\). (This generalizes the formula given on page 95 for the case when \(A\) is square and invertible.)
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}\) satisfy \(\operatorname{rank} A = m\), and define \(g(x) = f(Ax)\), where \(f : \mathbf{R}^m \to \mathbf{R}\) is convex. Show that
\(g^*(y) = f^*\!\bigl((A^\dagger)^T y\bigr), \qquad \operatorname{dom}(g^*) = A^T \operatorname{dom}(f^*),\)\nwhere \(A^\dagger = (AA^T)^{-1}A\) is the pseudoinverse of \(A\).
problem_finally:
Let \(A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}\) satisfy \(\operatorname{rank}(A) = m\), and define its Moore--Penrose pseudoinverse by
\(A^{\dagger} = A^T (A A^T)^{-1}.\)
Let \(f : \mathbf{R}^m \to \mathbf{R}\) be convex, and define \(g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) by
\(g(x) = f(Ax), \qquad x \in \mathbf{R}^n.\)
The convex conjugates of \(f\) and \(g\) are
\(f^*(u) = \sup_{z \in \mathbf{R}^m} \{ u^T z - f(z) \}, \qquad u \in \mathbf{R}^m,\)
and
\(g^*(y) = \sup_{x \in \mathbf{R}^n} \{ y^T x - g(x) \}, \qquad y \in \mathbf{R}^n.\)
Show that:

1. For every \(y \in \mathbf{R}^n\) such that \(g^*(y)\) is finite,
\(g^*(y) = f^*\bigl( (A^{\dagger})^T y \bigr).\)

2. The effective domain of \(g^*\) is
\(\operatorname{dom}(g^*) = A^T \operatorname{dom}(f^*) = \{ A^T u : u \in \operatorname{dom}(f^*) \}.\)

problem:
Exercise 2.19
[Roberts and Varberg] Suppose $\lambda_1,\ldots,\lambda_n$ are positive. Show that the function $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$, given by

\[
f(x)=\prod_{i=1}^n (1-e^{-x_i})^{\lambda_i},
\]

is concave on

\[
\operatorname{dom} f=\left\{x\in\mathbf{R}_{++}^n\ \middle|\ \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i e^{-x_i}\leq 1\right\}.
\]

Hint. The Hessian is given by

\[
\nabla^2 f(x)=f(x)(yy^T-\operatorname{diag}(z))
\]

where $y_i=\lambda_i e^{-x_i}/(1-e^{-x_i})$ and $z_i=y_i/(1-e^{-x_i})$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.19
[Roberts and Varberg] Suppose \(\lambda_1,\ldots,\lambda_n\) are positive. Show that the function \(f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\), given by

\(f(x)=\prod_{i=1}^n (1-e^{-x_i})^{\lambda_i},\)

is concave on

\(\operatorname{dom} f=\left\{x\in\mathbf{R}_{++}^n\ \middle|\ \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i e^{-x_i}\leq 1\right\}.\)

Hint. The Hessian is given by

\(abla^2 f(x)=f(x)(yy^T-\operatorname{diag}(z))\)

where \(y_i=\lambda_i e^{-x_i}/(1-e^{-x_i})\) and \(z_i=y_i/(1-e^{-x_i})\).
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(\lambda_1,\ldots,\lambda_n\) be positive. Show that the function \(f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\) defined by
\(f(x)=\prod_{i=1}^n (1-e^{-x_i})^{\lambda_i}\)
is concave on
\(\operatorname{dom} f=\left\{x\in\mathbf{R}_{++}^n\ \middle|\ \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i e^{-x_i}\leq 1\right\}.\)
Hint: The Hessian is
\(abla^2 f(x)=f(x)\bigl(yy^T-\operatorname{diag}(z)\bigr),\)
where
\(y_i=\frac{\lambda_i e^{-x_i}}{1-e^{-x_i}},\qquad z_i=\frac{y_i}{1-e^{-x_i}}.\)
problem_finally:
Let \(n \in \mathbf{N}\) and let \(\lambda_1,\ldots,\lambda_n \in \mathbf{R}_{++}\). Define \(f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) by
\(f(x)=\prod_{i=1}^n \left(1-e^{-x_i}\right)^{\lambda_i},\)\nwith domain
\(\operatorname{dom} f=\left\{x=(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\in \mathbf{R}_{++}^n \mid \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i e^{-x_i}\le 1\right\},\)\nwhere
\(\mathbf{R}_{++}^n=\{x\in \mathbf{R}^n \mid x_i>0,\ i=1,\ldots,n\}.\)
Show that \(f\) is concave on \(\operatorname{dom} f\).

You may use that the Hessian of \(f\) is
\(abla^2 f(x)=f(x)\left(yy^T-\operatorname{diag}(z)\right),\)\nwhere \(y,z\in \mathbf{R}^n\) are defined componentwise by
\(y_i=\frac{\lambda_i e^{-x_i}}{1-e^{-x_i}}, \qquad z_i=\frac{y_i}{1-e^{-x_i}}, \qquad i=1,\ldots,n.\)

problem:
Exercise 2.20
Show that the following functions $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ are convex.

(a) The difference between the maximum and minimum value of a polynomial on a given interval, as a function of its coefficients:

\[
f(x)=\sup_{t\in[a,b]} p(t)-\inf_{t\in[a,b]} p(t)
\]

where

\[
p(t)=x_1+x_2 t+x_3 t^2+\cdots+x_n t^{n-1}.
\]

$a,b$ are real constants with $a<b$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.20
Show that the following functions \(f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\) are convex.

(a) The difference between the maximum and minimum value of a polynomial on a given interval, as a function of its coefficients:

\(f(x)=\sup_{t\in[a,b]} p(t)-\inf_{t\in[a,b]} p(t)\)

where

\(p(t)=x_1+x_2 t+x_3 t^2+\cdots+x_n t^{n-1}.\)

\(a,b\) are real constants with \(a<b\).
problem_standardized_math:
Show that the function \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) defined by
\(f(x)=\sup_{t\in[a,b]} p(t)-\inf_{t\in[a,b]} p(t),\)\nwhere
\(p(t)=x_1+x_2 t+x_3 t^2+\cdots+x_n t^{n-1},\)\nand \(a,b\) are real constants with \(a<b\), is convex.
problem_finally:
Let \(n \in \mathbf{N}\) with \(n \ge 1\), and let \(a,b \in \mathbf{R}\) satisfy \(a < b\). For each \(x = (x_1,\dots,x_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n\), define
\(p_x : [a,b] \to \mathbf{R}, \qquad p_x(t) = x_1 + x_2 t + x_3 t^2 + \cdots + x_n t^{n-1}.\)
Define \(f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) by
\(f(x) = \sup_{t \in [a,b]} p_x(t) - \inf_{t \in [a,b]} p_x(t).\)
Prove that \(f\) is convex on \(\mathbf{R}^n\), i.e., show that for all \(x,y \in \mathbf{R}^n\) and all \(\lambda \in [0,1]\),
\(f(\lambda x + (1-\lambda)y) \le \lambda f(x) + (1-\lambda) f(y).\)

problem:
Exercise 2.20
Show that the following functions $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ are convex.

(b) The ‘exponential barrier’ of a set of inequalities:

\[
f(x)=\sum_{i=1}^m e^{-1/f_i(x)}, \qquad \operatorname{dom} f=\{x\mid f_i(x)<0,\ i=1,\ldots,m\}.
\]

The functions $f_i$ are convex.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.20
Show that the following functions \(f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\) are convex.

(b) The ‘exponential barrier’ of a set of inequalities:

\(f(x)=\sum_{i=1}^m e^{-1/f_i(x)}, \qquad \operatorname{dom} f=\{x\mid f_i(x)<0,\ i=1,\ldots,m\}.\)

The functions \(f_i\) are convex.
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(f_i\) be convex functions, and define \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) by
\(f(x)=\sum_{i=1}^m e^{-1/f_i(x)},\qquad \operatorname{dom} f=\{x\mid f_i(x)<0,\ i=1,\ldots,m\}.\)
Show that \(f\) is convex.
problem_finally:
Let \(f_i : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\), \(i=1,\ldots,m\), be convex functions. Define
\(\operatorname{dom} f = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid f_i(x) < 0,\ i=1,\ldots,m\},\)
and define \(f : \operatorname{dom} f \to \mathbf{R}\) by
\(f(x) = \sum_{i=1}^m e^{-1/f_i(x)}.\)
Prove that \(f\) is convex on \(\operatorname{dom} f\), i.e., for all \(x,y \in \operatorname{dom} f\) and all \(\theta \in [0,1]\), if \(\theta x + (1-\theta)y \in \operatorname{dom} f\), then
\(f(\theta x + (1-\theta)y) \le \theta f(x) + (1-\theta)f(y).\)

problem:
Exercise 2.20
Show that the following functions $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ are convex.

(c) The function

\[
f(x)=\inf_{\alpha>0}\frac{g(y+\alpha x)-g(y)}{\alpha}
\]

if $g$ is convex and $y\in\operatorname{dom} g$. (It can be shown that this is the directional derivative of $g$ at $y$ in the direction $x$.)
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.20
Show that the following functions \(f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\) are convex.

(c) The function

\(f(x)=\inf_{\alpha>0}\frac{g(y+\alpha x)-g(y)}{\alpha}\)

if \(g\) is convex and \(y\in\operatorname{dom} g\). (It can be shown that this is the directional derivative of \(g\) at \(y\) in the direction \(x\).)
problem_standardized_math:
Show that the function \(f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\) defined by
\(f(x)=\inf_{\alpha>0}\frac{g(y+\alpha x)-g(y)}{\alpha}\)
is convex, assuming that \(g\) is convex and that \(y\in\operatorname{dom} g\). (This quantity is the directional derivative of \(g\) at \(y\) in the direction \(x\).)
problem_finally:
Let \(g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} \cup \{+\infty\}\) be convex, and let \(y \in \operatorname{dom} g\), where
\(\operatorname{dom} g = \{ z \in \mathbf{R}^n : g(z) < +\infty \}.\)
For each \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\), define
\(f(x) = \inf_{\alpha > 0} \frac{g(y + \alpha x) - g(y)}{\alpha}.\)
Prove that \(f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} \cup \{-\infty, +\infty\}\) is convex; i.e., show that for all \(x_1, x_2 \in \mathbf{R}^n\) and all \(\lambda \in [0,1]\),
\(f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda)x_2) \le \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2).\)

problem:
Exercise 2.21
Symmetric convex functions of eigenvalues. A function $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ is said to be symmetric if it is invariant with respect to a permutation of its arguments, i.e., $f(x)=f(Px)$ for any permutation matrix $P$. An example of a symmetric function is $f(x)=\log(\sum_{k=1}^n \exp x_k)$.

In this problem we show that if $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ is convex and symmetric, then the function $g:S^n\to\mathbf{R}$ defined as $g(X)=f(\lambda(X))$ is convex, where $\lambda(X)=(\lambda_1(X),\ \lambda_2(X),\ldots,\lambda_n(X))$ is the vector of eigenvalues of $X$. This implies, for example, that the function

\[
g(X)=\log \operatorname{tr} e^X=\log \sum_{k=1}^n e^{\lambda_k(X)}
\]

is convex on $S^n$.

(a) A square matrix $S$ is doubly stochastic if its elements are nonnegative and all row sums and column sums are equal to one. It can be shown that every doubly stochastic matrix is a convex combination of permutation matrices.

Show that if $f$ is convex and symmetric and $S$ is doubly stochastic, then

\[
f(Sx)\leq f(x).
\]
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.21
Symmetric convex functions of eigenvalues.
