A function \(f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\) is said to be symmetric if it is invariant with respect to a permutation of its arguments, i.e., \(f(x)=f(Px)\) for any permutation matrix \(P\). An example of a symmetric function is \(f(x)=\log(\sum_{k=1}^n \exp x_k)\).

In this problem we show that if \(f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\) is convex and symmetric, then the function \(g:S^n\to\mathbf{R}\) defined as \(g(X)=f(\lambda(X))\) is convex, where \(\lambda(X)=(\lambda_1(X),\ \lambda_2(X),\ldots,\lambda_n(X))\) is the vector of eigenvalues of \(X\). This implies, for example, that the function

\(g(X)=\log \operatorname{tr} e^X=\log \sum_{k=1}^n e^{\lambda_k(X)}\)

is convex on \(S^n\).

(a) A square matrix \(S\) is doubly stochastic if its elements are nonnegative and all row sums and column sums are equal to one. It can be shown that every doubly stochastic matrix is a convex combination of permutation matrices.

Show that if \(f\) is convex and symmetric and \(S\) is doubly stochastic, then

\(f(Sx)\leq f(x).\)
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be convex and symmetric, where symmetric means that \(f(x)=f(Px)\) for every permutation matrix \(P\). Let \(S\) be a doubly stochastic matrix, i.e., a square matrix with nonnegative entries whose row sums and column sums are all equal to \(1\). It is known that every doubly stochastic matrix is a convex combination of permutation matrices. Show that
\(f(Sx)\leq f(x).\)
problem_finally:
Let \(f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be convex and permutation-invariant, i.e.,
\(f(x) = f(Px) \quad \text{for every permutation matrix } P \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n} \text{ and every } x \in \mathbf{R}^n.\)
Let \(S \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n}\) be doubly stochastic, meaning
\(S_{ij} \ge 0 \quad \text{for all } i,j \in \{1,\dots,n\},\)\nand
\(\sum_{j=1}^n S_{ij} = 1 \quad \text{for all } i \in \{1,\dots,n\},\qquad \sum_{i=1}^n S_{ij} = 1 \quad \text{for all } j \in \{1,\dots,n\}.\)
Assume that every doubly stochastic matrix can be expressed as a convex combination of permutation matrices.

Show that, for every \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\),
\(f(Sx) \le f(x).\)

problem:
Exercise 2.21
Symmetric convex functions of eigenvalues. A function $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ is said to be symmetric if it is invariant with respect to a permutation of its arguments, i.e., $f(x)=f(Px)$ for any permutation matrix $P$. An example of a symmetric function is $f(x)=\log(\sum_{k=1}^n \exp x_k)$.

In this problem we show that if $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ is convex and symmetric, then the function $g:S^n\to\mathbf{R}$ defined as $g(X)=f(\lambda(X))$ is convex, where $\lambda(X)=(\lambda_1(X),\ \lambda_2(X),\ldots,\lambda_n(X))$ is the vector of eigenvalues of $X$. This implies, for example, that the function

\[
g(X)=\log \operatorname{tr} e^X=\log \sum_{k=1}^n e^{\lambda_k(X)}
\]

is convex on $S^n$.

(b) Let $Y = Q \operatorname{diag}(\lambda) Q^T$ be an eigenvalue decomposition of $Y \in S^n$ with $Q$ orthogonal. Show that the $n \times n$ matrix $S$ with elements $S_{ij} = Q_{ij}^2$ is doubly stochastic and that $\operatorname{diag}(Y) = S\lambda$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.21
Symmetric convex functions of eigenvalues. A function \(f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\) is said to be symmetric if it is invariant with respect to a permutation of its arguments, i.e., \(f(x)=f(Px)\) for any permutation matrix \(P\). An example of a symmetric function is \(f(x)=\log(\sum_{k=1}^n \exp x_k)\).

In this problem we show that if \(f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\) is convex and symmetric, then the function \(g:S^n\to\mathbf{R}\) defined as \(g(X)=f(\lambda(X))\) is convex, where \(\lambda(X)=(\lambda_1(X),\ \lambda_2(X),\ldots,\lambda_n(X))\) is the vector of eigenvalues of \(X\). This implies, for example, that the function

\(g(X)=\log \operatorname{tr} e^X=\log \sum_{k=1}^n e^{\lambda_k(X)}\)

is convex on \(S^n\).

(b) Let \(Y = Q \operatorname{diag}(\lambda) Q^T\) be an eigenvalue decomposition of \(Y \in S^n\) with \(Q\) orthogonal. Show that the \(n \times n\) matrix \(S\) with elements \(S_{ij} = Q_{ij}^2\) is doubly stochastic and that \(\operatorname{diag}(Y) = S\lambda\).
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(Y \in S^n\) have eigenvalue decomposition
\(Y = Q \operatorname{diag}(\lambda) Q^T,\)\nwhere \(Q\) is orthogonal and \(\lambda=(\lambda_1,\lambda_2,\ldots,\lambda_n)\) is the vector of eigenvalues of \(Y\). Define the \(n\times n\) matrix \(S\) by
\(S_{ij}=Q_{ij}^2.\)
Show that \(S\) is doubly stochastic and that
\(\operatorname{diag}(Y)=S\lambda.\)
problem_finally:
Let \(Y \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n}\) be symmetric, with eigenvalue decomposition
\(Y = Q \operatorname{diag}(\lambda) Q^T,\)\nwhere \(Q \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n}\) is orthogonal, so that \(Q^T Q = Q Q^T = I\), and \(\lambda = (\lambda_1,\lambda_2,\ldots,\lambda_n)^T \in \mathbf{R}^n\). Define \(S \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n}\) by
\(S_{ij} = Q_{ij}^2, \qquad 1 \le i,j \le n.\)
Prove:

1. \(S\) is doubly stochastic, i.e.
\(S_{ij} \ge 0 \qquad \text{for all } i,j,\)\nand
\(\sum_{j=1}^n S_{ij} = 1 \qquad \text{for } i=1,\ldots,n,\)
\(\sum_{i=1}^n S_{ij} = 1 \qquad \text{for } j=1,\ldots,n.\)

2. If \(\operatorname{diag}(Y) = (Y_{11},Y_{22},\ldots,Y_{nn})^T \in \mathbf{R}^n\), then
\(\operatorname{diag}(Y) = S \lambda.\)

problem:
Exercise 2.21
Symmetric convex functions of eigenvalues. A function $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ is said to be symmetric if it is invariant with respect to a permutation of its arguments, i.e., $f(x)=f(Px)$ for any permutation matrix $P$. An example of a symmetric function is $f(x)=\log(\sum_{k=1}^n \exp x_k)$.

In this problem we show that if $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ is convex and symmetric, then the function $g:S^n\to\mathbf{R}$ defined as $g(X)=f(\lambda(X))$ is convex, where $\lambda(X)=(\lambda_1(X),\ \lambda_2(X),\ldots,\lambda_n(X))$ is the vector of eigenvalues of $X$. This implies, for example, that the function

\[
g(X)=\log \operatorname{tr} e^X=\log \sum_{k=1}^n e^{\lambda_k(X)}
\]

is convex on $S^n$.

(c) Use the results in parts (a) and (b) to show that if $f$ is convex and symmetric and $X\in S^n$, then

\[
f(\lambda(X))=\sup_{V\in\mathcal{V}} f(\operatorname{diag}(V^T X V))
\]

where $\mathcal{V}$ is the set of $n\times n$ orthogonal matrices. Show that this implies that $f(\lambda(X))$ is convex in $X$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.21
Symmetric convex functions of eigenvalues. A function \(f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\) is said to be symmetric if it is invariant with respect to a permutation of its arguments, i.e., \(f(x)=f(Px)\) for any permutation matrix \(P\). An example of a symmetric function is \(f(x)=\log(\sum_{k=1}^n \exp x_k)\).

In this problem we show that if \(f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\) is convex and symmetric, then the function \(g:S^n\to\mathbf{R}\) defined as \(g(X)=f(\lambda(X))\) is convex, where \(\lambda(X)=(\lambda_1(X),\ \lambda_2(X),\ldots,\lambda_n(X))\) is the vector of eigenvalues of \(X\). This implies, for example, that the function

\(g(X)=\log \operatorname{tr} e^X=\log \sum_{k=1}^n e^{\lambda_k(X)}\)

is convex on \(S^n\).

(c) Use the results in parts (a) and (b) to show that if \(f\) is convex and symmetric and \(X\in S^n\), then

\(f(\lambda(X))=\sup_{V\in\mathcal{V}} f(\operatorname{diag}(V^T X V))\)

where \(\mathcal{V}\) is the set of \(n\times n\) orthogonal matrices. Show that this implies that \(f(\lambda(X))\) is convex in \(X\).
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be convex and symmetric, i.e., \(f(x)=f(Px)\) for every permutation matrix \(P\). For \(X \in S^n\), let \(\lambda(X)=(\lambda_1(X),\lambda_2(X),\ldots,\lambda_n(X))\) denote the vector of eigenvalues of \(X\), and let \(\mathcal{V}\) denote the set of \(n\times n\) orthogonal matrices. Show that
\(f(\lambda(X))=\sup_{V\in\mathcal{V}} f\bigl(\operatorname{diag}(V^T X V)\bigr).\)
Then show that this implies that \(f(\lambda(X))\) is convex in \(X\).
problem_finally:
Let \(S^n\) be the set of real \(n \times n\) symmetric matrices, and let \(\mathcal{V}\) be the set of real \(n \times n\) orthogonal matrices. For \(X \in S^n\), let \(\lambda(X) = (\lambda_1(X),\ldots,\lambda_n(X)) \in \mathbf{R}^n\) denote the vector of eigenvalues of \(X\). For any matrix \(A\), let \(\operatorname{diag}(A) \in \mathbf{R}^n\) denote the vector of diagonal entries of \(A\).

Assume \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) is convex and permutation-invariant, i.e.,
\(f(x) = f(Px)\)\nfor every permutation matrix \(P\) and every \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\).

Prove:

1. For every \(X \in S^n\),
\(f(\lambda(X)) = \sup_{V \in \mathcal{V}} f\bigl(\operatorname{diag}(V^T X V)\bigr).\)

2. Using item 1, show that the spectral mapping \(X \mapsto f(\lambda(X))\) is convex on \(S^n\). Equivalently, prove that for all \(X,Y \in S^n\) and all \(t \in [0,1]\),
\(f\bigl(\lambda(tX + (1-t)Y)\bigr) \le t\,f(\lambda(X)) + (1-t)\,f(\lambda(Y)).\)

problem:
Exercise 2.22
Convexity of nonsymmetric matrix fractional function. Consider the function $f:\mathbf{R}^{n\times n}\times\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$, defined by

\[
f(X,y)=y^T X^{-1} y, \qquad \operatorname{dom} f=\{(X,y)\mid X+X^T\succ 0\}.
\]

When this function is restricted to $X\in S^n$, it is convex.

Is $f$ convex? If so, prove it. If not, give a (simple) counterexample.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.22
Convexity of nonsymmetric matrix fractional function. Consider the function \(f:\mathbf{R}^{n\times n}\times\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\), defined by

\(f(X,y)=y^T X^{-1} y, \qquad \operatorname{dom} f=\{(X,y)\mid X+X^T\succ 0\}.\)

When this function is restricted to \(X\in S^n\), it is convex.

Is \(f\) convex? If so, prove it. If not, give a (simple) counterexample.
problem_standardized_math:
Consider the function \(f:\mathbf{R}^{n\times n}\times \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) defined by
\(f(X,y)=y^T X^{-1}y,\qquad \operatorname{dom} f=\{(X,y)\mid X+X^T\succ 0\}.\)
It is known that \(f\) is convex when restricted to \(X\in S^n\). Determine whether \(f\) is convex on its domain. If it is convex, prove it; otherwise, give a simple counterexample.
problem_finally:
Let
\(f : \mathbf{R}^{n \times n} \times \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}, \qquad f(X,y) = y^T X^{-1} y,\)
with domain
\(\operatorname{dom} f = \{(X,y) \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n} \times \mathbf{R}^n \mid X + X^T \succ 0\}.\)
Assume that \(X + X^T \succ 0\) implies \(X\) is invertible, so \(X^{-1}\) is well defined. It is known that \(f\) is convex when restricted to symmetric matrices \(X \in S^n\).

1. Determine whether \(f\) is convex in \((X,y)\) on the full domain \(\operatorname{dom} f\).
2. If it is convex, prove it.
3. If it is not convex, give a simple counterexample.

problem:
Exercise 2.23
Show that the following functions $f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ are convex.

(a) $f(x)=-\exp(-g(x))$ where $g:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ has a convex domain and satisfies

\[
\begin{bmatrix}
\nabla^2 g(x) & \nabla g(x) \\
\nabla g(x)^T & 1
\end{bmatrix} \succeq 0
\]

for $x \in \operatorname{dom} g$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.23
Show that the following functions \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) are convex.

(a) \(f(x)=-\exp(-g(x))\) where \(g:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) has a convex domain and satisfies

\[
\begin{bmatrix}
\nabla^2 g(x) & \nabla g(x) \\
\nabla g(x)^T & 1
\end{bmatrix} \succeq 0
\]

for \(x \in \operatorname{dom} g\).
problem_standardized_math:
Show that the function
\(f(x)=-\exp(-g(x)),\)\nwhere \(g:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\) has a convex domain and satisfies
\[
\begin{bmatrix}
\nabla^2 g(x) & \nabla g(x) \\\\
\nabla g(x)^T & 1
\end{bmatrix} \succeq 0
\]\nfor all \(x\in \operatorname{dom} g\), is convex.
problem_finally:
Let \(g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be twice continuously differentiable on its convex domain \(\operatorname{dom} g \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\). Define
\(f(x) = -\exp(-g(x)), \qquad x \in \operatorname{dom} g.\)
Assume that for every \(x \in \operatorname{dom} g\),
\[
\begin{bmatrix}
\nabla^2 g(x) & \nabla g(x) \\
\nabla g(x)^T & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\succeq 0.
\]
Show that \(f\) is convex on \(\operatorname{dom} g\).

problem:
Exercise 2.23
Show that the following functions $f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ are convex.

(b) The function

\[
f(x)=\max\{\|APx-b\| \mid P \text{ is a permutation matrix}\}
\]

with $A \in \mathbf{R}^{m\times n}$, $b \in \mathbf{R}^m$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.23
Show that the following functions \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) are convex.

(b) The function

\(f(x)=\max\{\|APx-b\| \mid P \text{ is a permutation matrix}\}\)

with \(A \in \mathbf{R}^{m\times n}\), \(b \in \mathbf{R}^m\).
problem_standardized_math:
Show that the function \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) defined by
\(f(x)=\max\{\\|APx-b\\| \mid P \text{ is a permutation matrix}\}\)\nis convex, where \(A\in\mathbf{R}^{m\times n}\) and \(b\in\mathbf{R}^m\).
problem_finally:
Let \(A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}\) and \(b \in \mathbf{R}^m\) be fixed. For each permutation matrix \(P \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n}\), define
\(\phi_P(x) = \|APx - b\|, \qquad x \in \mathbf{R}^n,\)
where \(\|\cdot\|\) is the Euclidean norm on \(\mathbf{R}^m\). Define
\(f(x) = \max\left\{ \|APx - b\| \mid P \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n} \text{ is a permutation matrix} \right\}, \qquad x \in \mathbf{R}^n.\)
Show that \(f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) is convex.

problem:
Exercise 2.24
\emph{Convex hull of functions.} Suppose $g$ and $h$ are convex functions, bounded below, with $\operatorname{dom} g=\operatorname{dom} h=\mathbf{R}^n$. The convex hull function of $g$ and $h$ is defined as

\[
f(x)=\inf\{\theta g(y)+(1-\theta)h(z)\mid \theta y+(1-\theta)z=x,\ 0\leq \theta\leq 1\},
\]

where the infimum is over $\theta$, $y$, $z$. Show that the convex hull of $h$ and $g$ is convex. Describe $\operatorname{epi} f$ in terms of $\operatorname{epi} g$ and $\operatorname{epi} h$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.24
\emph{Convex hull of functions.} Suppose \(g\) and \(h\) are convex functions, bounded below, with \(\operatorname{dom} g=\operatorname{dom} h=\mathbf{R}^n\). The convex hull function of \(g\) and \(h\) is defined as

\(f(x)=\inf\{\theta g(y)+(1-\theta)h(z)\mid \theta y+(1-\theta)z=x,\ 0\leq \theta\leq 1\},\)

where the infimum is over \(\theta\), \(y\), \(z\). Show that the convex hull of \(h\) and \(g\) is convex. Describe \(\operatorname{epi} f\) in terms of \(\operatorname{epi} g\) and \(\operatorname{epi} h\).
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(g\) and \(h\) be convex functions, bounded below, with \(\operatorname{dom} g = \operatorname{dom} h = \mathbf{R}^n\). Define their convex hull function by
\(f(x)=\inf\left\{\theta g(y)+(1-\theta)h(z)\mid \theta y+(1-\theta)z=x,\ 0\leq \theta\leq 1\right\},\)
where the infimum is taken over \(\theta\), \(y\), and \(z\). Show that \(f\) is convex. Describe \(\operatorname{epi} f\) in terms of \(\operatorname{epi} g\) and \(\operatorname{epi} h\).
problem_finally:
Let \(g,h:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be convex functions, bounded below, with \(\operatorname{dom} g=\operatorname{dom} h=\mathbf{R}^n\). Define \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) by
\(f(x)=\inf \left\{ \theta g(y)+(1-\theta)h(z)\ \middle|\ x=\theta y+(1-\theta)z,\ \theta \in [0,1],\ y,z \in \mathbf{R}^n \right\}.\)
Equivalently, the infimum is over all \((\theta,y,z) \in [0,1]\times \mathbf{R}^n \times \mathbf{R}^n\) such that \(x=\theta y+(1-\theta)z\).

1. Prove that \(f\) is convex on \(\mathbf{R}^n\).

2. Express the epigraph
\(\operatorname{epi} f=\{(x,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n \times \mathbf{R}\mid f(x)\le t\}\)\nin terms of
\(\operatorname{epi} g=\{(x,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n \times \mathbf{R}\mid g(x)\le t\},\qquad \operatorname{epi} h=\{(x,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n \times \mathbf{R}\mid h(x)\le t\}.\)

problem:
Exercise 2.25
Show that a function $f:\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{R}$ is convex if and only if $\operatorname{dom} f$ is convex and

\[
\det
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 1 & 1 \\
x & y & z \\
f(x) & f(y) & f(z)
\end{bmatrix}
\geq 0
\]

for all $x,y,z\in \operatorname{dom} f$ with $x<y<z$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.25
Show that a function \(f:\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{R}\) is convex if and only if \(\operatorname{dom} f\) is convex and

\[
\det
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 1 & 1 \x & y & z \f(x) & f(y) & f(z)
\end{bmatrix}
\geq 0
\]

for all \(x,y,z\in \operatorname{dom} f\) with \(x<y<z\).
problem_standardized_math:
Show that a function \(f:\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{R}\) is convex if and only if \(\operatorname{dom} f\) is convex and
\[
\det\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 1 & 1 \x & y & z \f(x) & f(y) & f(z)
\end{bmatrix} \ge 0
\]
for all \(x,y,z\in\operatorname{dom} f\) such that \(x<y<z\).
problem_finally:
Let \(f : \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{R}\) and let \(\operatorname{dom} f \subseteq \mathbf{R}\) be its effective domain. Rewrite and prove the following equivalence:

\(f\) is convex, i.e., for all \(x,z \in \operatorname{dom} f\) and all \(\lambda \in [0,1]\),
\(f(\lambda x + (1-\lambda) z) \le \lambda f(x) + (1-\lambda) f(z),\)\nif and only if both of the following hold:

1. \(\operatorname{dom} f\) is convex: for all \(x,z \in \operatorname{dom} f\) and all \(\lambda \in [0,1]\),
\(\lambda x + (1-\lambda) z \in \operatorname{dom} f.\)

2. For all \(x,y,z \in \operatorname{dom} f\) with \(x < y < z\),
\[
\det\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 1 & 1 \\nx & y & z \\nf(x) & f(y) & f(z)
\end{bmatrix} \ge 0.
\]

problem:
Exercise 2.26
\emph{Generalization of the convexity of $\log \det X^{-1}$.} Let $P \in \mathbf{R}^{n\times m}$ have rank $m$. In this problem we show that the function $f:\mathbf{S}^n \to \mathbf{R}$, with $\operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{S}_{++}^n$, and

\[
f(X)=\log\det(P^T X^{-1}P)
\]

is convex. To prove this, we assume (without loss of generality) that $P$ has the form

\[
P=
\begin{bmatrix}
I \\
0
\end{bmatrix},
\]

where $I$. The matrix $P^T X^{-1}P$ is then the leading $m\times m$ principal submatrix of $X^{-1}$.

(a) Let $Y$ and $Z$ be symmetric matrices with $0 \prec Y \preceq Z$. Show that $\det Y \leq \det Z$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.26
\emph{Generalization of the convexity of \(\log \det X^{-1}\).} Let \(P \in \mathbf{R}^{n\times m}\) have rank \(m\). In this problem we show that the function \(f:\mathbf{S}^n \to \mathbf{R}\), with \(\operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{S}_{++}^n\), and

\(f(X)=\log\det(P^T X^{-1}P)\)

is convex. To prove this, we assume (without loss of generality) that \(P\) has the form

\[
P=
\begin{bmatrix}
I \\
0
\end{bmatrix},
\]

where \(I\). The matrix \(P^T X^{-1}P\) is then the leading \(m\times m\) principal submatrix of \(X^{-1}\).

(a) Let \(Y\) and \(Z\) be symmetric matrices with \(0 \prec Y \preceq Z\). Show that \(\det Y \leq \det Z\).
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(Y\) and \(Z\) be symmetric matrices such that \(0 \prec Y \preceq Z\). Show that
\(\det Y \leq \det Z.\)
problem_finally:
Let \(Y, Z \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n}\) be symmetric matrices, with \(n \in \mathbf{N}\), satisfying
\(0 \prec Y \preceq Z,\)\ni.e., \(Y\) is positive definite and \(Z - Y\) is positive semidefinite. Prove that
\(\det(Y) \le \det(Z).\)

problem:
Exercise 2.26
\emph{Generalization of the convexity of $\log \det X^{-1}$.} Let $P \in \mathbf{R}^{n\times m}$ have rank $m$. In this problem we show that the function $f:\mathbf{S}^n \to \mathbf{R}$, with $\operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{S}_{++}^n$, and

\[
f(X)=\log\det(P^T X^{-1}P)
\]

is convex. To prove this, we assume (without loss of generality) that $P$ has the form

\[
P=
\begin{bmatrix}
I \\
0
\end{bmatrix},
\]

where $I$. The matrix $P^T X^{-1}P$ is then the leading $m\times m$ principal submatrix of $X^{-1}$.

(b) Let $X\in \mathbf{S}_{++}^n$, partitioned as

\[
X=
\begin{bmatrix}
X_{11} & X_{12} \\
X_{12}^T & X_{22}
\end{bmatrix},
\]

with $X_{11}\in \mathbf{S}^m$. Show that the optimization problem

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & \log\det Y^{-1} \\
\text{subject to}\quad & \begin{bmatrix}
Y 0 \\
& 0 0
\end{bmatrix}
\preceq
\begin{bmatrix}
X_{11} X_{12} \\
& X_{12}^T X_{22}
\end{bmatrix},
\end{aligned}
\]

with variable $Y \in \mathbf{S}^m$, has the solution

\[
Y = X_{11} - X_{12}X_{22}^{-1}X_{12}^T.
\]

(As usual, we take $\mathbf{S}_{++}^m$ as the domain of $\log \det Y^{-1}$.)
Hint. Use the Schur complement characterization of positive definite block matrices (page 651 of the book): if $C \succ 0$ then

\[
\begin{bmatrix}
A & B \\
B^T & C
\end{bmatrix}
\succeq 0
\]

if and only if $A - BC^{-1}B^T \succeq 0$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.26
\emph{Generalization of the convexity of \(\log \det X^{-1}\).} Let \(P \in \mathbf{R}^{n\times m}\) have rank \(m\). In this problem we show that the function \(f:\mathbf{S}^n \to \mathbf{R}\), with \(\operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{S}_{++}^n\), and

\(f(X)=\log\det(P^T X^{-1}P)\)

is convex. To prove this, we assume (without loss of generality) that \(P\) has the form

\[
P=
\begin{bmatrix}
I \\
0
\end{bmatrix},
\]

where \(I\). The matrix \(P^T X^{-1}P\) is then the leading \(m\times m\) principal submatrix of \(X^{-1}\).

(b) Let \(X\in \mathbf{S}_{++}^n\), partitioned as

\[
X=
\begin{bmatrix}
X_{11} & X_{12} \X_{12}^T & X_{22}
\end{bmatrix},
\]

with \(X_{11}\in \mathbf{S}^m\). Show that the optimization problem

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & \log\det Y^{-1} \\
\text{subject to}\quad & \begin{bmatrix}
Y 0 \\
& 0 0
\end{bmatrix}
\preceq
\begin{bmatrix}
X_{11} X_{12} \\
& X_{12}^T X_{22}
\end{bmatrix},
\end{aligned}
\]

with variable \(Y \in \mathbf{S}^m\), has the solution

\(Y = X_{11} - X_{12}X_{22}^{-1}X_{12}^T.\)

(As usual, we take \(\mathbf{S}_{++}^m\) as the domain of \(\log \det Y^{-1}\).)
Hint. Use the Schur complement characterization of positive definite block matrices (page 651 of the book): if \(C \succ 0\) then

\[
\begin{bmatrix}
A & B \B^T & C
\end{bmatrix}
\succeq 0
\]

if and only if \(A - BC^{-1}B^T \succeq 0\).
problem_standardized_math:
Let
\(X=\begin{bmatrix}X_{11} & X_{12}\\\\ X_{12}^T & X_{22}\end{bmatrix}\in \mathbf{S}_{++}^n,\)\nwhere \(X_{11}\in \mathbf{S}^m\). Consider the optimization problem
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & \log\det Y^{-1} \\\\
\text{subject to} \quad & \begin{bmatrix}Y & 0\\\\ 0 & 0\end{bmatrix} \preceq \begin{bmatrix}X_{11} & X_{12}\\\\ X_{12}^T & X_{22}\end{bmatrix},
\end{aligned}
\]\nwith variable \(Y\in \mathbf{S}^m\), and with domain \(Y\in \mathbf{S}_{++}^m\) for \(\log\det Y^{-1}\).

Show that this problem has the solution
\(Y = X_{11} - X_{12}X_{22}^{-1}X_{12}^T.\)

You may use the Schur complement characterization: if \(C\succ 0\), then
\(\begin{bmatrix}A & B\\\\ B^T & C\end{bmatrix} \succeq 0\)\nif and only if
\(A - BC^{-1}B^T \succeq 0.\)
problem_finally:
Let
\(X = \begin{bmatrix} X_{11} & X_{12} \\ X_{12}^T & X_{22} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbf{S}_{++}^n,\)
where \(X_{11} \in \mathbf{S}^m\), \(X_{22} \in \mathbf{S}^{n-m}\), and \(X_{12} \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times (n-m)}\). Consider
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & \log \det(Y^{-1}) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & \begin{bmatrix} Y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \preceq \begin{bmatrix} X_{11} & X_{12} \\ X_{12}^T & X_{22} \end{bmatrix}, \\
& Y \in \mathbf{S}_{++}^m,
\end{aligned}
\]
with decision variable \(Y \in \mathbf{S}^m\).

1. Show that the optimal solution is
\(Y = X_{11} - X_{12} X_{22}^{-1} X_{12}^T.\)

You may use the Schur complement fact: if \(C \succ 0\), then
\[
\begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B^T & C \end{bmatrix} \succeq 0
\quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad
A - B C^{-1} B^T \succeq 0.
\]

problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.26
\emph{Generalization of the convexity of \(\log \det X^{-1}\).} Let \(P \in \mathbf{R}^{n\times m}\) have rank \(m\). In this problem we show that the function \(f:\mathbf{S}^n \to \mathbf{R}\), with \(\operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{S}_{++}^n\), and

I \\
0

\]

where \(I\). The matrix \(P^T X^{-1}P\) is then the leading \(m\times m\) principal submatrix of \(X^{-1}\).

(c) Combine the result in part (b) and the minimization property (page 3-19, lecture notes) to show that the function

\(f(X) = \log \det (X_{11} - X_{12}X_{22}^{-1}X_{12}^T)^{-1},\)

with \(\mathbf{dom}\,f = \mathbf{S}_{++}^n\), is convex.
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(P \in \mathbf{R}^{n\times m}\) have rank \(m\), and assume without loss of generality that
\(P=\begin{bmatrix}I\\0\end{bmatrix},\)
so that \(P^T X^{-1}P\) is the leading \(m\times m\) principal submatrix of \(X^{-1}\). Combine the result of part (b) with the minimization property to show that the function
\(f(X)=\log\det\left(X_{11}-X_{12}X_{22}^{-1}X_{12}^T\right)^{-1},\)
with domain \(\operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{S}_{++}^n\), is convex.
problem_finally:
Let \(n,m \in \mathbf{N}\) with \(m \le n\). Let \(P \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times m}\) have rank \(m\), and assume without loss of generality that
\(P=\begin{bmatrix}I_m\\\\0\end{bmatrix}.\)
For \(X \in \mathbf{S}_{++}^n\), partition \(X\) conformably with \(n=m+(n-m)\) as
\[
X=\begin{bmatrix}
X_{11} & X_{12}\X_{12}^T & X_{22}
\end{bmatrix},
\]\nwhere \(X_{11} \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times m}\), \(X_{12} \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times (n-m)}\), and \(X_{22} \in \mathbf{R}^{(n-m)\times(n-m)}\). In this representation, \(P^T X^{-1} P\) is the leading principal \(m \times m\) submatrix of \(X^{-1}\).

Using the result from part (b) and the minimization property established in the preceding part, prove that the function
\(f(X)=\log\det\left(X_{11}-X_{12}X_{22}^{-1}X_{12}^T\right)^{-1}\)\nis convex on \(\mathbf{S}_{++}^n\), with domain
\(\operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{S}_{++}^n.\)

problem:
Exercise 2.26
\emph{Generalization of the convexity of $\log \det X^{-1}$.} Let $P \in \mathbf{R}^{n\times m}$ have rank $m$. In this problem we show that the function $f:\mathbf{S}^n \to \mathbf{R}$, with $\operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{S}_{++}^n$, and

\[
f(X)=\log\det(P^T X^{-1}P)
\]

is convex. To prove this, we assume (without loss of generality) that $P$ has the form

\[
P=
\begin{bmatrix}
I \\
0
\end{bmatrix},
\]

where $I$. The matrix $P^T X^{-1}P$ is then the leading $m\times m$ principal submatrix of $X^{-1}$.

(d) Show that $(X_{11} - X_{12}X_{22}^{-1}X_{12}^T)^{-1}$ is the leading $m \times m$ principal submatrix of $X^{-1}$, i.e.,

\[
(X_{11} - X_{12}X_{22}^{-1}X_{12}^T)^{-1} = P^T X^{-1} P.
\]

Hence, the convex function $f$ defined in part (c) can also be expressed as $f(X) = \log \det (P^T X^{-1}P)$.
Hint. Use the formula for the inverse of a symmetric block matrix:

\[
\begin{bmatrix}
A & B \\
B^T & C
\end{bmatrix}^{-1}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 \\
0 & C^{-1}
\end{bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix}
-I \\
C^{-1}B^T
\end{bmatrix}
(A - BC^{-1}B^T)^{-1}
\begin{bmatrix}
-I \\
C^{-1}B^T
\end{bmatrix}^T
\]

if $C$ and $A - BC^{-1}B^T$ are invertible.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.26
\emph{Generalization of the convexity of \(\log \det X^{-1}\).} Let \(P \in \mathbf{R}^{n\times m}\) have rank \(m\). In this problem we show that the function \(f:\mathbf{S}^n \to \mathbf{R}\), with \(\operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{S}_{++}^n\), and

\(f(X)=\log\det(P^T X^{-1}P)\)

is convex. To prove this, we assume (without loss of generality) that \(P\) has the form

\[
P=
\begin{bmatrix}
I \\
0
\end{bmatrix},
\]

where \(I\). The matrix \(P^T X^{-1}P\) is then the leading \(m\times m\) principal submatrix of \(X^{-1}\).

(d) Show that \((X_{11} - X_{12}X_{22}^{-1}X_{12}^T)^{-1}\) is the leading \(m \times m\) principal submatrix of \(X^{-1}\), i.e.,

\((X_{11} - X_{12}X_{22}^{-1}X_{12}^T)^{-1} = P^T X^{-1} P.\)

Hence, the convex function \(f\) defined in part (c) can also be expressed as \(f(X) = \log \det (P^T X^{-1}P)\).
Hint. Use the formula for the inverse of a symmetric block matrix:

\[
\begin{bmatrix}
A & B \B^T & C
\end{bmatrix}^{-1}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 \\
0 & C^{-1}
\end{bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix}
-I \C^{-1}B^T
\end{bmatrix}
(A - BC^{-1}B^T)^{-1}
\begin{bmatrix}
-I \C^{-1}B^T
\end{bmatrix}^T
\]

if \(C\) and \(A - BC^{-1}B^T\) are invertible.

problem:
Exercise 2.27
Functions of a random variable with log-concave density. Suppose the random variable $X$ on $\mathbf{R}^n$ has log-concave density, and let $Y = g(X)$, where $g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$. For each of the following statements, either give a counterexample, or show that the statement is true.

(a) If $g$ is affine and not constant, then $Y$ has log-concave density.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.27
Functions of a random variable with log-concave density. Suppose the random variable \(X\) on \(\mathbf{R}^n\) has log-concave density, and let \(Y = g(X)\), where \(g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\). For each of the following statements, either give a counterexample, or show that the statement is true.

(a) If \(g\) is affine and not constant, then \(Y\) has log-concave density.
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(X\) be a random variable on \(\mathbf{R}^n\) with log-concave density, and let \(Y=g(X)\), where \(g:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\). Determine whether the following statement is true or false; either prove it or give a counterexample:

If \(g\) is affine and not constant, then \(Y\) has a log-concave density.
problem_finally:
Let \(X\) be an \(\mathbf{R}^n\)-valued random variable with density \(f : \mathbf{R}^n \to [0,\infty)\) with respect to Lebesgue measure. Assume \(f\) is log-concave, i.e., for all \(x,z \in \mathbf{R}^n\) and all \(\lambda \in [0,1]\),
\(f(\lambda x + (1-\lambda) z) \ge f(x)^{\lambda} f(z)^{1-\lambda}.\)
Let \(g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be affine, with
\(g(x) = a \cdot x + b,\)\nwhere \(a \in \mathbf{R}^n\), \(b \in \mathbf{R}\), and \(a \ne 0\). Define
\(Y = g(X).\)
Determine whether the following statement is true or false:

1. \(Y\) has a log-concave density on \(\mathbf{R}\).

If the statement is true, provide a proof. If it is false, provide a counterexample.

problem:
Exercise 2.27
Functions of a random variable with log-concave density. Suppose the random variable $X$ on $\mathbf{R}^n$ has log-concave density, and let $Y = g(X)$, where $g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$. For each of the following statements, either give a counterexample, or show that the statement is true.

(b) If $g$ is convex, then $\mathbf{prob}(Y \leq a)$ is a log-concave function of $a$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.27
Functions of a random variable with log-concave density. Suppose the random variable \(X\) on \(\mathbf{R}^n\) has log-concave density, and let \(Y = g(X)\), where \(g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\). For each of the following statements, either give a counterexample, or show that the statement is true.

(b) If \(g\) is convex, then \(\mathbf{prob}(Y \leq a)\) is a log-concave function of \(a\).
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(X\) be a random variable on \(\mathbf{R}^n\) with log-concave density, and let \(Y=g(X)\), where \(g:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\). Determine whether the following statement is true or false; that is, either prove it or give a counterexample:

If \(g\) is convex, then \(\operatorname{Prob}(Y\le a)\) is a log-concave function of \(a\).
problem_finally:
Let \(X\) be an \(\mathbf{R}^n\)-valued random variable with log-concave density \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to [0,\infty)\). Let \(g:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be convex, and define \(Y=g(X)\).

Determine whether the following statement is true or false. Either prove it or give a counterexample:

1. The distribution function of \(Y\),
 \(F(a)=\operatorname{Prob}(Y \le a)=\operatorname{Prob}(g(X)\le a), \qquad a \in \mathbf{R},\)
 is log-concave as a function of \(a\). Equivalently, for all \(a_1,a_2 \in \mathbf{R}\) and all \(\lambda \in [0,1]\),
 \[
 F\bigl(\lambda a_1+(1-\lambda)a_2\bigr)
 \ge
 F(a_1)^{\lambda}F(a_2)^{1-\lambda}.
 \]

problem:
Exercise 2.27
Functions of a random variable with log-concave density. Suppose the random variable $X$ on $\mathbf{R}^n$ has log-concave density, and let $Y = g(X)$, where $g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$. For each of the following statements, either give a counterexample, or show that the statement is true.

(c) If $g$ is concave, then $\mathbf{E}\,((Y-a)_+)$ is a convex and log-concave function of $a$. (This quantity is called the tail expectation of $Y$; you can assume it exists. We define $(s)_+$ as $(s)_+ = \max\{s,0\}$.)
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.27
Functions of a random variable with log-concave density. Suppose the random variable \(X\) on \(\mathbf{R}^n\) has log-concave density, and let \(Y = g(X)\), where \(g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\). For each of the following statements, either give a counterexample, or show that the statement is true.

(c) If \(g\) is concave, then \(\mathbf{E}\,((Y-a)_+)\) is a convex and log-concave function of \(a\). (This quantity is called the tail expectation of \(Y\); you can assume it exists. We define \((s)_+\) as \((s)_+ = \max\{s,0\}\).)
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(X\) be a random variable on \(\mathbf{R}^n\) with log-concave density, and let \(Y=g(X)\), where \(g:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\). In the case where \(g\) is concave, determine whether the following statement is true or false: the tail expectation
\(\mathbf{E}\bigl((Y-a)_+\bigr),\qquad (s)_+=\max\{s,0\},\)
viewed as a function of \(a\), is convex and log-concave. You may assume that this expectation exists. Either prove the statement or give a counterexample.
problem_finally:
Let \(X\) be an \(\mathbf{R}^n\)-valued random variable with a log-concave density on \(\mathbf{R}^n\). Let \(g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be concave, and define \(Y = g(X)\). Assume that for every \(a \in \mathbf{R}\),
\(T(a) := \mathbf{E}\bigl((Y-a)_+\bigr) < \infty, \qquad (s)_+ := \max\{s,0\}.\)
Determine whether the following statement is true or false:

1. The function \(T : \mathbf{R} \to [0,\infty)\), \(a \mapsto \mathbf{E}\bigl((Y-a)_+\bigr)\), is convex on \(\mathbf{R}\).
2. The function \(T\) is log-concave on \(\mathbf{R}\).

If the statement is true, prove it. If it is false, provide a counterexample.

problem:
Exercise 2.28
Majorization. Define $C$ as the set of all permutations of a given $n$-vector $a$, i.e., the set of vectors $(a_{\pi_1}, a_{\pi_2}, \ldots, a_{\pi_n})$ where $(\pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots, \pi_n)$ is one of the $n!$ permutations of $(1,2,\ldots,n)$.

(a) The support function of $C$ is defined as $S_C(y) = \max_{x \in C} y^T x$. Show that

\[
S_C(y) = a_{[1]}y_{[1]} + a_{[2]}y_{[2]} + \cdots + a_{[n]}y_{[n]}.
\]

($u_{[1]}, u_{[2]}, \ldots, u_{[n]}$ denote the components of an $n$-vector $u$ in nonincreasing order.)
Hint. To find the maximum of $y^T x$ over $x \in C$, write the inner product as

\[
y^T x
=
(y_1-y_2)x_1 + (y_2-y_3)(x_1+x_2) + (y_3-y_4)(x_1+x_2+x_3) + \cdots
\]


\[
\qquad + (y_{n-1}-y_n)(x_1+x_2+\cdots+x_{n-1}) + y_n(x_1+x_2+\cdots+x_n)
\]

and assume that the components of $y$ are sorted in nonincreasing order.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.28
Majorization. Define \(C\) as the set of all permutations of a given \(n\)-vector \(a\), i.e., the set of vectors \((a_{\pi_1}, a_{\pi_2}, \ldots, a_{\pi_n})\) where \((\pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots, \pi_n)\) is one of the \(n!\) permutations of \((1,2,\ldots,n)\).

(a) The support function of \(C\) is defined as \(S_C(y) = \max_{x \in C} y^T x\). Show that

\(S_C(y) = a_{[1]}y_{[1]} + a_{[2]}y_{[2]} + \cdots + a_{[n]}y_{[n]}.\)

(\(u_{[1]}, u_{[2]}, \ldots, u_{[n]}\) denote the components of an \(n\)-vector \(u\) in nonincreasing order.)
Hint. To find the maximum of \(y^T x\) over \(x \in C\), write the inner product as

\[
y^T x
=
(y_1-y_2)x_1 + (y_2-y_3)(x_1+x_2) + (y_3-y_4)(x_1+x_2+x_3) + \cdots
\]


\(\qquad + (y_{n-1}-y_n)(x_1+x_2+\cdots+x_{n-1}) + y_n(x_1+x_2+\cdots+x_n)\)

and assume that the components of \(y\) are sorted in nonincreasing order.
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(C\) be the set of all permutations of a given vector \(a \in \mathbf{R}^n\), i.e.,
\(C = \{(a_{\pi_1}, a_{\pi_2}, \ldots, a_{\pi_n}) \mid (\pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots, \pi_n) \text{ is a permutation of } (1,2,\ldots,n)\}.\)
The support function of \(C\) is
\(S_C(y) = \max_{x \in C} y^T x.\)
Show that
\(S_C(y) = a_{[1]}y_{[1]} + a_{[2]}y_{[2]} + \cdots + a_{[n]}y_{[n]},\)
where \(u_{[1]}, u_{[2]}, \ldots, u_{[n]}\) denote the components of a vector \(u\) arranged in nonincreasing order.

Hint: To maximize \(y^T x\) over \(x \in C\), assume that the components of \(y\) are sorted in nonincreasing order and write
\(y^T x = (y_1-y_2)x_1 + (y_2-y_3)(x_1+x_2) + (y_3-y_4)(x_1+x_2+x_3) + \cdots\)
\(\qquad + (y_{n-1}-y_n)(x_1+x_2+\cdots+x_{n-1}) + y_n(x_1+x_2+\cdots+x_n).\)
problem_finally:
Let \(a = (a_1,\ldots,a_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n\), and define
\(C = \left\{ (a_{\pi_1},\ldots,a_{\pi_n}) \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid (\pi_1,\ldots,\pi_n) \text{ is a permutation of } (1,\ldots,n) \right\}.\)
For \(y \in \mathbf{R}^n\), define the support function of \(C\) by
\(S_C(y) = \max_{x \in C} y^T x.\)
For any \(u \in \mathbf{R}^n\), let
\(u_{[1]} \ge u_{[2]} \ge \cdots \ge u_{[n]}\)
denote the components of \(u\) rearranged in nonincreasing order.

Prove that for every \(y \in \mathbf{R}^n\),
\(S_C(y) = \sum_{i=1}^n a_{[i]} y_{[i]}.\)

Hint: In maximizing \(y^T x\) over \(x \in C\), one may assume without loss of generality that
\(y_1 \ge y_2 \ge \cdots \ge y_n,\)
and then use the identity
\(y^T x = (y_1-y_2)x_1 + (y_2-y_3)(x_1+x_2) + \cdots + (y_{n-1}-y_n)(x_1+\cdots+x_{n-1}) + y_n(x_1+\cdots+x_n).\)

problem:
Exercise 2.28
Majorization. Define $C$ as the set of all permutations of a given $n$-vector $a$, i.e., the set of vectors $(a_{\pi_1}, a_{\pi_2}, \ldots, a_{\pi_n})$ where $(\pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots, \pi_n)$ is one of the $n!$ permutations of $(1,2,\ldots,n)$.

(b) Show that $x$ satisfies $x^T y \leq S_C(y)$ for all $y$ if and only if

\[
s_k(x) \leq s_k(a), \quad k = 1,\ldots,n-1, \qquad s_n(x) = s_n(a),
\]

where $s_k$ denotes the function $s_k(x)=x_{[1]}+x_{[2]}+\cdots+x_{[k]}$. When these inequalities hold, we say the vector $a$ majorizes the vector $x$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.28
Majorization. Define \(C\) as the set of all permutations of a given \(n\)-vector \(a\), i.e., the set of vectors \((a_{\pi_1}, a_{\pi_2}, \ldots, a_{\pi_n})\) where \((\pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots, \pi_n)\) is one of the \(n!\) permutations of \((1,2,\ldots,n)\).

(b) Show that \(x\) satisfies \(x^T y \leq S_C(y)\) for all \(y\) if and only if

\(s_k(x) \leq s_k(a), \quad k = 1,\ldots,n-1, \qquad s_n(x) = s_n(a),\)

where \(s_k\) denotes the function \(s_k(x)=x_{[1]}+x_{[2]}+\cdots+x_{[k]}\). When these inequalities hold, we say the vector \(a\) majorizes the vector \(x\).
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(C\) be the set of all permutations of a given \(n\)-vector \(a\), i.e.,
\(C=\{(a_{\pi_1},a_{\pi_2},\ldots,a_{\pi_n}) \mid (\pi_1,\pi_2,\ldots,\pi_n) \text{ is a permutation of } (1,2,\ldots,n)\}.\)
Show that \(x\) satisfies
\(x^T y \leq S_C(y) \quad \text{for all } y\)
if and only if
\(s_k(x) \leq s_k(a), \quad k=1,\ldots,n-1, \qquad s_n(x)=s_n(a),\)
where
\(s_k(x)=x_{[1]}+x_{[2]}+\cdots+x_{[k]}.\)
When these inequalities hold, the vector \(a\) is said to majorize the vector \(x\).
problem_finally:
Let \(a=(a_1,\ldots,a_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n\) be fixed. Define
\(C=\left\{(a_{\pi_1},a_{\pi_2},\ldots,a_{\pi_n}) \;\middle|\; (\pi_1,\pi_2,\ldots,\pi_n) \text{ is a permutation of } (1,2,\ldots,n)\right\} \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n.\)
For \(y \in \mathbf{R}^n\), define the support function
\(S_C(y)=\sup_{z \in C} z^T y.\)
For any \(x=(x_1,\ldots,x_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n\), let \(x_{[1]},x_{[2]},\ldots,x_{[n]}\) be the components of \(x\) rearranged in nonincreasing order:
\(x_{[1]} \ge x_{[2]} \ge \cdots \ge x_{[n]}.\)
For \(k=1,\ldots,n\), define
\(s_k(x)=\sum_{i=1}^k x_{[i]}.\)
Similarly, define \(a_{[1]},\ldots,a_{[n]}\) and \(s_k(a)\).

Prove that for \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\), the following are equivalent:
1. \(x^T y \le S_C(y) \qquad \text{for all } y \in \mathbf{R}^n.\)
2. \(s_k(x) \le s_k(a), \qquad k=1,\ldots,n-1,\)\nand
\(s_n(x)=s_n(a).\)
When condition 2 holds, \(a\) is said to majorize \(x\).

problem:
Exercise 2.28
Majorization. Define $C$ as the set of all permutations of a given $n$-vector $a$, i.e., the set of vectors $(a_{\pi_1}, a_{\pi_2}, \ldots, a_{\pi_n})$ where $(\pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots, \pi_n)$ is one of the $n!$ permutations of $(1,2,\ldots,n)$.

(c) Conclude from this that the conjugate of $S_C$ is given by

\[
S_C^*(x)=
\begin{cases}
0 & \text{if } x \text{ is majorized by } a\\
+\infty & \text{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
\]

Since $S_C^*$ is the indicator function of the convex hull of $C$, this establishes the following result: $x$ is a convex combination of the permutations of $a$ if and only if $a$ majorizes $x$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.28
Majorization. Define \(C\) as the set of all permutations of a given \(n\)-vector \(a\), i.e., the set of vectors \((a_{\pi_1}, a_{\pi_2}, \ldots, a_{\pi_n})\) where \((\pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots, \pi_n)\) is one of the \(n!\) permutations of \((1,2,\ldots,n)\).

(c) Conclude from this that the conjugate of \(S_C\) is given by

\[
S_C^*(x)=
\begin{cases}
0 & \text{if } x \text{ is majorized by } a\\
+\infty & \text{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
\]

Since \(S_C^*\) is the indicator function of the convex hull of \(C\), this establishes the following result: \(x\) is a convex combination of the permutations of \(a\) if and only if \(a\) majorizes \(x\).
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(C\) be the set of all permutations of a given \(n\)-vector \(a\), i.e.,
\(C=\{(a_{\pi_1},a_{\pi_2},\ldots,a_{\pi_n})\mid (\pi_1,\pi_2,\ldots,\pi_n)\text{ is a permutation of }(1,2,\ldots,n)\}.\)
Conclude that the conjugate of \(S_C\) is
\[
S_C^*(x)=
\begin{cases}
0, & \text{if } x \text{ is majorized by } a,\\
+\infty, & \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(S_C^*\) is the indicator function of the convex hull of \(C\), this establishes that \(x\) is a convex combination of the permutations of \(a\) if and only if \(a\) majorizes \(x\).
problem_finally:
Let \(a = (a_1,\ldots,a_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n\) be fixed, and let \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n\) be the set of all permutations of \(a\), namely
\(C=\{(a_{\pi_1},a_{\pi_2},\ldots,a_{\pi_n}) \mid (\pi_1,\pi_2,\ldots,\pi_n)\text{ is a permutation of }(1,2,\ldots,n)\}.\)
Let \(S_C : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\cup\{+\infty\}\) denote the support function of \(C\),
\(S_C(y)=\sup_{z\in C} y^T z,\)\nand let its convex conjugate \(S_C^* : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\cup\{+\infty\}\) be defined by
\(S_C^*(x)=\sup_{y\in \mathbf{R}^n}\{x^T y - S_C(y)\}.\)
Recall that a vector \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\) is majorized by \(a\) if, after rearranging the components of \(x\) and \(a\) in nonincreasing order,
\(x_{[1]} \ge x_{[2]} \ge \cdots \ge x_{[n]}, \qquad a_{[1]} \ge a_{[2]} \ge \cdots \ge a_{[n]},\)\none has
\(\sum_{i=1}^k x_{[i]} \le \sum_{i=1}^k a_{[i]} \quad \text{for } k=1,\ldots,n-1,\)\nand
\(\sum_{i=1}^n x_i = \sum_{i=1}^n a_i.\)

Prove the following:

1. The convex conjugate of \(S_C\) is given by
\[
S_C^*(x)=
\begin{cases}
0, & \text{if } x \text{ is majorized by } a,\\\\
+\infty, & \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

2. Deduce that \(S_C^*\) is the indicator function of \(\operatorname{conv}(C)\), the convex hull of \(C\).

3. Conclude that \(x \in \operatorname{conv}(C)\) if and only if \(a\) majorizes \(x\); equivalently, \(x\) is a convex combination of the permutations of \(a\) if and only if \(x\) is majorized by \(a\).

problem:
Exercise 2.29
\emph{Convexity of products of powers.} This problem concerns the product of powers function $f:\mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ given by $f(x)=x_1^{\theta_1}\cdots x_n^{\theta_n}$, where $\theta \in \mathbf{R}^n$ is a vector of powers. We are interested in finding values of $\theta$ for which $f$ is convex or concave. You already know a few, for example when $n=2$ and $\theta=(2,-1)$, $f$ is convex (the quadratic-over-linear function), and when $\theta=(1/n)1$, $f$ is concave (geometric mean). Of course, if $n=1$, $f$ is convex when $\theta \geq 1$ or $\theta \leq 0$, and concave when $0 \leq \theta \leq 1$.

Show each of the statements below. We will not read long or complicated proofs, or ones that involve Hessians. We are looking for short, snappy ones, that (where possible) use composition rules, perspective, partial minimization, or other operations, together with known convex or concave functions, such as the ones listed in the previous paragraph. Feel free to use the results of earlier statements in later ones.

(a) When $n=2$, $\theta \succeq 0$, and $1^T\theta=1$, $f$ is concave.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.29
\emph{Convexity of products of powers.} This problem concerns the product of powers function \(f:\mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) given by \(f(x)=x_1^{\theta_1}\cdots x_n^{\theta_n}\), where \(\theta \in \mathbf{R}^n\) is a vector of powers. We are interested in finding values of \(\theta\) for which \(f\) is convex or concave. You already know a few, for example when \(n=2\) and \(\theta=(2,-1)\), \(f\) is convex (the quadratic-over-linear function), and when \(\theta=(1/n)1\), \(f\) is concave (geometric mean). Of course, if \(n=1\), \(f\) is convex when \(\theta \geq 1\) or \(\theta \leq 0\), and concave when \(0 \leq \theta \leq 1\).

Show each of the statements below. We will not read long or complicated proofs, or ones that involve Hessians. We are looking for short, snappy ones, that (where possible) use composition rules, perspective, partial minimization, or other operations, together with known convex or concave functions, such as the ones listed in the previous paragraph. Feel free to use the results of earlier statements in later ones.

(a) When \(n=2\), \(\theta \succeq 0\), and \(1^T\theta=1\), \(f\) is concave.
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(f:\mathbf{R}_{++}^2 \to \mathbf{R}\) be the product-of-powers function
\(f(x)=x_1^{\theta_1}x_2^{\theta_2},\)\nwhere \(\theta=(\theta_1,\theta_2)\in \mathbf{R}^2\). Show that if \(\theta \succeq 0\) and \(1^T\theta=1\) (i.e., \(\theta_1,\theta_2 \ge 0\) and \(\theta_1+\theta_2=1\)), then \(f\) is concave.
problem_finally:
Let \(f : \mathbf{R}_{++}^2 \to \mathbf{R}\) be defined by
\(f(x_1,x_2) = x_1^{\theta_1} x_2^{\theta_2},\)
where
\(\mathbf{R}_{++}^2 = \{(x_1,x_2) \in \mathbf{R}^2 : x_1 > 0,\ x_2 > 0\},\)
and the parameters \(\theta_1, \theta_2 \in \mathbf{R}\) satisfy
\(\theta_1 \ge 0, \qquad \theta_2 \ge 0, \qquad \theta_1 + \theta_2 = 1.\)
Prove that \(f\) is concave on \(\mathbf{R}_{++}^2\); equivalently, show that for all \(x,y \in \mathbf{R}_{++}^2\) and all \(\lambda \in [0,1]\),
\(f(\lambda x + (1-\lambda)y) \ge \lambda f(x) + (1-\lambda) f(y).\)

problem_standardized_math:
Let \(f:\mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be defined by
\(f(x)=x_1^{\theta_1}\cdots x_n^{\theta_n},\)\nwhere \(\theta \in \mathbf{R}^n\). Show that if \(\theta \succeq 0\) and \(1^T\theta=1\), then \(f\) is concave.
problem_finally:
Let \(n \in \mathbf{N}\), let \(\mathbf{R}_{++}^n = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n : x_i > 0 \text{ for all } i=1,\dots,n\}\), and let \(\theta = (\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n\). Define
\(f : \mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}, \qquad f(x) = \prod_{i=1}^n x_i^{\theta_i}.\)
Assume that
\(\theta_i \ge 0 \quad \text{for all } i=1,\dots,n, \qquad \sum_{i=1}^n \theta_i = 1.\)
Show that \(f\) is concave on \(\mathbf{R}_{++}^n\).

problem_standardized_math:
Let \(f:\mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be defined by
\(f(x)=x_1^{\theta_1}\cdots x_n^{\theta_n},\)\nwhere \(\theta \in \mathbf{R}^n\). Show that \(f\) is convex when \(1^T\theta=1\) and exactly one component of \(\theta\) is positive.
problem_finally:
Let \(n \in \mathbf{N}\), let \(\theta = (\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n\), and define
\(f : \mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}, \qquad f(x) = \prod_{i=1}^n x_i^{\theta_i},\)
where
\(\mathbf{R}_{++}^n = \{x = (x_1,\ldots,x_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n : x_i > 0 \text{ for all } i=1,\ldots,n\}.\)
Assume that
\(\sum_{i=1}^n \theta_i = 1,\)
and that there exists a unique index \(k \in \{1,\ldots,n\}\) such that
\(\theta_k > 0, \qquad \theta_i \le 0 \quad \text{for all } i \ne k.\)
Prove that \(f\) is convex on \(\mathbf{R}_{++}^n\).

problem:
Exercise 2.29
\emph{Convexity of products of powers.} This problem concerns the product of powers function $f:\mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ given by $f(x)=x_1^{\theta_1}\cdots x_n^{\theta_n}$, where $\theta \in \mathbf{R}^n$ is a vector of powers. We are interested in finding values of $\theta$ for which $f$ is convex or concave. You already know a few, for example when $n=2$ and $\theta=(2,-1)$, $f$ is convex (the quadratic-over-linear function), and when $\theta=(1/n)1$, $f$ is concave (geometric mean). Of course, if $n=1$, $f$ is convex when $\theta \geq 1$ or $\theta \leq 0$, and concave when $0 \leq \theta \leq 1$.

Show each of the statements below. We will not read long or complicated proofs, or ones that involve Hessians. We are looking for short, snappy ones, that (where possible) use composition rules, perspective, partial minimization, or other operations, together with known convex or concave functions, such as the ones listed in the previous paragraph. Feel free to use the results of earlier statements in later ones.

(f) When $1^T\theta \geq 1$ and exactly one of the elements of $\theta$ is positive, $f$ is convex.

\emph{Remark.} Parts (c), (d), and (f) exactly characterize the cases when $f$ is either convex or concave. That is, if none of these conditions on $\theta$ hold, $f$ is neither convex nor concave. Your teaching staff has, however, kindly refrained from asking you to show this.

problem_finally:
Let \(n \in \mathbf{N}\) and let \(\theta = (\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n\). Define \(f : \mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) by

where

problem:
Exercise 2.30
Huber penalty. The infimal convolution of two functions $f$ and $g$ on $\mathbf{R}^n$ is defined as

\[
h(x)=\inf_y \left(f(y)+g(x-y)\right)
\]

(see exercise 2.17). Show that the infimal convolution of $f(x)=\|x\|_1$ and $g(x)=(1/2)\|x\|_2^2$, i.e., the function

\[
h(x)=\inf_y \left(f(y)+g(x-y)\right)
=\inf_y \left(\|y\|_1+\frac{1}{2}\|x-y\|_2^2\right),
\]

is the Huber penalty

\[
h(x)=\sum_{i=1}^n \phi(x_i), \qquad
\phi(u)=
\begin{cases}
u^2/2 & |u|\leq 1\\
|u|-1/2 & |u|>1.
\end{cases}
\]
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.30
Huber penalty. The infimal convolution of two functions \(f\) and \(g\) on \(\mathbf{R}^n\) is defined as

\(h(x)=\inf_y \left(f(y)+g(x-y)\right)\)

(see exercise 2.17). Show that the infimal convolution of \(f(x)=\|x\|_1\) and \(g(x)=(1/2)\|x\|_2^2\), i.e., the function

\[
h(x)=\inf_y \left(f(y)+g(x-y)\right)
=\inf_y \left(\|y\|_1+\frac{1}{2}\|x-y\|_2^2\right),
\]

is the Huber penalty

\[
h(x)=\sum_{i=1}^n \phi(x_i), \qquad
\phi(u)=
\begin{cases}
u^2/2 & |u|\leq 1\\
|u|-1/2 & |u|>1.
\end{cases}
\]
problem_standardized_math:
Let the infimal convolution of two functions \(f\) and \(g\) on \(\mathbf{R}^n\) be defined by
\(h(x)=\inf_y \bigl(f(y)+g(x-y)\bigr).\)
Show that for \(f(x)=\|x\|_1\) and \(g(x)=\tfrac{1}{2}\|x\|_2^2\), i.e.,
\(h(x)=\inf_y \left(\|y\|_1+\frac{1}{2}\|x-y\|_2^2\right),\)\none has
\[\nh(x)=\sum_{i=1}^n \phi(x_i), \qquad
\phi(u)=\begin{cases}
\dfrac{u^2}{2}, & |u|\leq 1,\\\\
|u|-\dfrac{1}{2}, & |u|>1.
\end{cases}
\]
That is, the infimal convolution is the Huber penalty.
problem_finally:
Let \(f,g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be defined by
\(f(x)=\|x\|_1=\sum_{i=1}^n |x_i|, \qquad g(x)=\frac{1}{2}\|x\|_2^2=\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2.\)
For each \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\), define the infimal convolution
\[
(f \square g)(x)=\inf_{y \in \mathbf{R}^n} \left( f(y)+g(x-y) \right)
=\inf_{y \in \mathbf{R}^n} \left( \|y\|_1+\frac{1}{2}\|x-y\|_2^2 \right).
\]
Define
\(h(x):=(f \square g)(x).\)
Prove the following:

1. The function \(h\) is separable across coordinates, i.e.
\(h(x)=\sum_{i=1}^n \phi(x_i)\)
for some scalar function \(\phi : \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{R}\).

2. The scalar function \(\phi\) is given by
\[
\phi(u)=\begin{cases}
\dfrac{u^2}{2}, & |u| \le 1,\\
|u|-\dfrac{1}{2}, & |u|>1.
\end{cases}
\]

3. Conclude that the infimal convolution of \(\|\cdot\|_1\) and \(\frac{1}{2}\|\cdot\|_2^2\) is the Huber penalty.

problem:
Exercise 2.31
Suppose the function $h:\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{R}$ is convex, nondecreasing, with $\operatorname{dom} h=\mathbf{R}$, and $h(t)=h(0)$ for $t\leq 0$.

(a) Show that the function $f(x)=h(\|x\|_2)$ is convex on $\mathbf{R}^n$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.31
Suppose the function \(h:\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{R}\) is convex, nondecreasing, with \(\operatorname{dom} h=\mathbf{R}\), and \(h(t)=h(0)\) for \(t\leq 0\).

(a) Show that the function \(f(x)=h(\|x\|_2)\) is convex on \(\mathbf{R}^n\).
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(h:\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{R}\) be convex and nondecreasing, with \(\operatorname{dom} h=\mathbf{R}\), and suppose that \(h(t)=h(0)\) for all \(t\leq 0\). Show that the function \(f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\) defined by
\(f(x)=h(\|x\|_2)\)\nis convex on \(\mathbf{R}^n\).
problem_finally:
Let \(n \in \mathbf{N}\). Let \(h : \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{R}\) satisfy:
- \(h\) is convex,
- \(h\) is nondecreasing,
- \(\operatorname{dom} h = \mathbf{R}\),
- \(h(t) = h(0)\) for all \(t \le 0\).

Define \(f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) by
\(f(x) = h(\|x\|_2), \qquad \|x\|_2 = \left( \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2 \right)^{1/2}.\)

Prove that \(f\) is convex on \(\mathbf{R}^n\), i.e., show that for all \(x,y \in \mathbf{R}^n\) and all \(\theta \in [0,1]\),
\(f(\theta x + (1-\theta)y) \le \theta f(x) + (1-\theta)f(y).\)

problem:
Exercise 2.31
Suppose the function $h:\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{R}$ is convex, nondecreasing, with $\operatorname{dom} h=\mathbf{R}$, and $h(t)=h(0)$ for $t\leq 0$.

(b) Show that the conjugate of $f$ is $f^*(y)=h^*(\|y\|_2)$.

\(f^*(y)=h^*(\|y\|_2).\)
problem_finally:
Let \(h:\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{R}\) be convex and nondecreasing, with \(\operatorname{dom} h=\mathbf{R}\), and assume
\(h(t)=h(0)\quad \text{for all } t\le 0.\)
Define \(f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}\) by
\(f(x)=h(\|x\|_2), \qquad x\in \mathbf{R}^n.\)
Let the convex conjugates \(f^*\) and \(h^*\) be
\(f^*(y)=\sup_{x\in \mathbf{R}^n}\bigl(y^T x-f(x)\bigr), \qquad y\in \mathbf{R}^n,\)\nand
\(h^*(s)=\sup_{t\in \mathbf{R}}\bigl(st-h(t)\bigr), \qquad s\in \mathbf{R}.\)
Prove that for every \(y\in \mathbf{R}^n\),
\(f^*(y)=h^*(\|y\|_2).\)

problem:
Exercise 2.31
Suppose the function $h:\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{R}$ is convex, nondecreasing, with $\operatorname{dom} h=\mathbf{R}$, and $h(t)=h(0)$ for $t\leq 0$.

(c) As an example, derive the conjugate of $f(x)=(1/p)\|x\|_2^p$ for $p>1$, by applying the result of part (b) with the function

\[
h(t)=\frac{1}{p}\max\{0,t\}^p=
\begin{cases}
\frac{1}{p}t^p & t\geq 0\\
0 & t<0.
\end{cases}
\]
problem_with_context:
Exercise 2.31
Suppose the function \(h:\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{R}\) is convex, nondecreasing, with \(\operatorname{dom} h=\mathbf{R}\), and \(h(t)=h(0)\) for \(t\leq 0\).

\[

problem_finally:
Let \(h : \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{R}\) be convex and nondecreasing, with \(\operatorname{dom} h = \mathbf{R}\), and assume
\(h(t) = h(0) \quad \text{for all } t \le 0.\)
Assume that a general formula is available for the Fenchel conjugate of functions of the form
\(x \mapsto h(\|x\|_2), \qquad x \in \mathbf{R}^n,\)
where \(\|\cdot\|_2\) is the Euclidean norm.

Using that formula, compute the Fenchel conjugate of
\(f(x) = \frac{1}{p} \|x\|_2^p, \qquad p > 1,\)
by taking
\[
h(t) = \frac{1}{p} \max\{0,t\}^p =
\begin{cases}
\frac{1}{p} t^p, & t \ge 0, \\
0, & t < 0.
\end{cases}

Determine \(f^*(y)\) for all \(y \in \mathbf{R}^n\).

problem:
Exercise 3.1
\emph{Minimizing a function over the probability simplex.} Find simple necessary and sufficient conditions for $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$ to minimize a differentiable convex function $f$ over the probability simplex, $\{x \mid \mathbf{1}^T x = 1,\ x \succeq 0\}$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 3.1
\emph{Minimizing a function over the probability simplex.} Find simple necessary and sufficient conditions for \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\) to minimize a differentiable convex function \(f\) over the probability simplex, \(\{x \mid \mathbf{1}^T x = 1,\ x \succeq 0\}\).
problem_standardized_math:
Find simple necessary and sufficient conditions for a point \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\) to minimize a differentiable convex function \(f\) over the probability simplex
\(\{\,x \mid \mathbf{1}^T x = 1,\; x \succeq 0\,\}.\)
problem_finally:
Let \(f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be differentiable and convex. Let
\(\Delta = \{ x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid \mathbf{1}^T x = 1,\ x_i \ge 0\ \text{for } i=1,\dots,n \},\)
where \(\mathbf{1} = (1,\dots,1)^T \in \mathbf{R}^n\).

Determine necessary and sufficient conditions, expressed explicitly in terms of a feasible point \(x \in \Delta\) and its gradient \(abla f(x)\), such that \(x\) is a minimizer of
\(\min \{ f(x) \mid x \in \Delta \}.\)
The conditions must characterize exactly when a feasible point \(x \in \Delta\) minimizes \(f\) over \(\Delta\).

problem:
Exercise 3.3
\emph{Reformulating constraints in CVX}. Each of the following CVX code fragments describes a convex constraint on the scalar variables $x$, $y$, and $z$, but violates the CVX rule set, and so is invalid. Briefly explain why each fragment is invalid. Then, rewrite each one in an equivalent form that conforms to the CVX rule set. In your reformulations, you can use linear equality and inequality constraints, and inequalities constructed using CVX functions. You can also introduce additional variables, or use LMIs. Be sure to explain (briefly) why your reformulation is equivalent to the original constraint, if it is not obvious.

Check your reformulations by creating a small problem that includes these constraints, and solving it using CVX. Your test problem doesn't have to be feasible; it's enough to verify that CVX processes your constraints without error.

\emph{Remark}. This \emph{looks} like a problem about `how to use CVX software', or `tricks for using CVX'. But it really checks whether you understand the various composition rules, convex analysis, and constraint reformulation rules.

(a) $\operatorname{norm}([x + 2y, x - y]) == 0$

(a) \(\operatorname{norm}([x + 2y, x - y]) == 0\)
problem_standardized_math:
For the scalar variables \(x\), \(y\), and \(z\), the CVX code fragment
\(\operatorname{norm}([x+2y,\;x-y]) = 0\)\ndescribes a convex constraint but violates the CVX rule set, and hence is invalid. Briefly explain why this fragment is invalid. Then rewrite it in an equivalent form that conforms to the CVX rule set. In the reformulation, you may use linear equality and inequality constraints, inequalities constructed using CVX functions, additional variables, or LMIs. If the equivalence to the original constraint is not obvious, briefly explain why it is equivalent.

Check your reformulation by creating a small problem that includes this constraint and solving it using CVX. The test problem need not be feasible; it is enough to verify that CVX accepts the constraint without error.
problem_finally:
Let \(x\), \(y\), and \(z\) be scalar decision variables. Consider the constraint
\(\operatorname{norm}([x+2y,\;x-y]) = 0.\)
This constraint defines a convex feasible set, but it is not accepted by the CVX disciplined convex programming rules.

1. Explain briefly why the equality constraint
\(\operatorname{norm}([x+2y,\;x-y]) = 0\)\nis rejected by CVX, despite being convex.

2. Give an equivalent reformulation of this constraint that is accepted by CVX and preserves exactly the same feasible set. The reformulation may use linear equalities, linear inequalities, inequalities involving CVX atoms, auxiliary variables, or linear matrix inequalities. If the equivalence is not immediate, justify it briefly.

3. Write a small CVX model in the variables \(x\), \(y\), and \(z\) that includes the reformulated constraint, and use it to verify computationally that CVX accepts the model without a disciplined convex programming rule-set error. The test model need not be feasible; it is sufficient that it is syntactically valid and accepted by CVX.

The constraint is convex, but its direct entry in CVX violates the disciplined convex programming (DCP) rules.

Rewrite the exercise as follows:

1. Explain briefly why the constraint
\(\frac{(x+y)^2}{\sqrt{y}} \le x-y+5\)\ncannot be entered directly in CVX under the DCP rules.

2. Give a mathematically equivalent reformulation that is accepted by CVX. The reformulation may use auxiliary variables, linear equalities or inequalities, inequalities involving standard CVX atoms, or linear matrix inequalities. Preserve all necessary domain restrictions, including \(y>0\).

3. If the equivalence of the reformulation with the original constraint is not immediate, justify briefly why they are equivalent.

4. Provide a small CVX test problem containing the reformulated constraint that can be submitted to CVX without a DCP error. The test problem need not be feasible or otherwise meaningful; it is sufficient that CVX accepts the formulation.

problem:
Exercise 3.3
\emph{Reformulating constraints in CVX}. Each of the following CVX code fragments describes a convex constraint on the scalar variables $x$, $y$, and $z$, but violates the CVX rule set, and so is invalid. Briefly explain why each fragment is invalid. Then, rewrite each one in an equivalent form that conforms to the CVX rule set. In your reformulations, you can use linear equality and inequality constraints, and inequalities constructed using CVX functions. You can also introduce additional variables, or use LMIs. Be sure to explain (briefly) why your reformulation is equivalent to the original constraint, if it is not obvious.

Check your reformulations by creating a small problem that includes these constraints, and solving it using CVX. Your test problem doesn't have to be feasible; it's enough to verify that CVX processes your constraints without error.

\emph{Remark}. This \emph{looks} like a problem about `how to use CVX software', or `tricks for using CVX'. But it really checks whether you understand the various composition rules, convex analysis, and constraint reformulation rules.

(g) $x^3 + y^3 \leq 1; \ x \geq 0; \ y \geq 0$

problem_standardized_math:
For the scalar variables \(x\), \(y\), and \(z\), consider the constraint
\(x^3+y^3\leq 1,\qquad x\geq 0,\qquad y\geq 0.\)
This CVX code fragment describes a convex constraint but violates the CVX rule set, and is therefore invalid. Briefly explain why it is invalid. Then rewrite it in an equivalent form that conforms to the CVX rule set. In your reformulation, you may use linear equality and inequality constraints and inequalities constructed using CVX functions. You may also introduce additional variables or use LMIs. If the equivalence of your reformulation to the original constraint is not obvious, briefly explain why they are equivalent.

Check your reformulation by creating a small problem that includes this constraint and solving it using CVX. The test problem need not be feasible; it is enough to verify that CVX accepts the constraint without error.
problem_finally:
Let \(x,y,z \in \mathbf{R}\) with constraints
\(x^3 + y^3 \le 1, \qquad x \ge 0, \qquad y \ge 0.\)
A direct CVX encoding of \(x^3 + y^3 \le 1\) is rejected by CVX disciplined convex programming rules, although the feasible set is convex.

Answer the following:

1. Explain briefly why the direct CVX constraint
\(x^3 + y^3 \le 1\)\nis invalid under the CVX rule set.

2. Give a mathematically equivalent reformulation of the constraint set that is accepted by CVX. The reformulation may use additional scalar variables and any of the following:
 - linear equality constraints,
 - linear inequality constraints,
 - inequalities involving CVX-recognized convex or concave functions,
 - linear matrix inequalities.

3. If the equivalence of the reformulation to the original constraint is not immediate, justify briefly that both formulations define the same feasible set.

4. Write a small CVX test model in variables \(x,y,z\) that includes the reformulated constraint, and state explicitly that the purpose of the model is only to verify that CVX accepts the formulation without error. The test model need not be feasible, and no numerical solution is required.

(h) \(x + z \leq 1 + \sqrt{xy - z^2};\ x \geq 0;\ y \geq 0\)
problem_standardized_math:
Consider the CVX constraint fragment on the scalar variables \(x\), \(y\), and \(z\):
\(x+z \le 1+\sqrt{xy-z^2},\qquad x\ge 0,\qquad y\ge 0.\)
This fragment describes a convex constraint but violates the CVX rule set. Briefly explain why it is invalid, and then rewrite it in an equivalent form that conforms to the CVX rule set. In your reformulation, you may use linear equality and inequality constraints, inequalities constructed using CVX functions, additional variables, or LMIs. If the equivalence of your reformulation to the original constraint is not obvious, briefly explain why they are equivalent.

problem_finally:
Consider scalar decision variables \(x\), \(y\), and \(z\) with constraints
\(x + z \le 1 + \sqrt{xy - z^2}, \qquad x \ge 0, \qquad y \ge 0.\)
It is known that this constraint system defines a convex feasible set, but the inequality as written is not compliant with the disciplined convex programming (DCP) rules of CVX.

1. Briefly explain why the constraint
\(x + z \le 1 + \sqrt{xy - z^2}\)\nis not accepted by the CVX rule set, despite being convex when combined with \(x \ge 0\) and \(y \ge 0\).

2. Reformulate the constraint system into an equivalent representation that is valid under the CVX rule set. The reformulation must preserve the feasible set exactly. You may use:
\(\text{(a) linear equalities or inequalities,} \qquad \text{(b) inequalities involving recognized CVX atoms,}\)
\(\text{(c) auxiliary scalar variables,} \qquad \text{(d) linear matrix inequalities (LMIs).}\)
If equivalence is not immediate, include a brief mathematical justification.

3. Provide a small CVX model containing the reformulated constraints that can be submitted to CVX without DCP violations. The optimization problem may be feasible or infeasible; the objective is only to verify that CVX accepts the reformulation.

problem:
Exercise 3.4
\emph{Optimal activity levels.} Solve the optimal activity level problem described in exercise 4.17 in \emph{Convex Optimization}, for the instance with problem data

\[
A =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 2 & 0 & 1 \\
0 & 0 & 3 & 1 \\
0 & 3 & 1 & 1 \\
2 & 1 & 2 & 5 \\
1 & 0 & 3 & 2
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
c^{\max} =
\begin{bmatrix}
100 \\
100 \\
100 \\
100 \\
100
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
p =
\begin{bmatrix}
3 \\
2 \\
7 \\
6
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
p^{\mathrm{disc}} =
\begin{bmatrix}
2 \\
1 \\
4 \\
2
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
q =
\begin{bmatrix}
4 \\
10 \\
5 \\
10
\end{bmatrix}.
\]

You can do this by forming the LP you found in your solution of exercise 4.17, or more directly, using CVX. Give the optimal activity levels, the revenue generated by each one, and the total revenue generated by the optimal solution. Also, give the average price per unit for each activity level, i.e., the ratio of the revenue associated with an activity, to the activity level. (These numbers

should be between the basic and discounted prices for each activity.) Give a very brief story explaining, or at least commenting on, the solution you find.

problem_finally:
Consider the optimization problem with decision variable \(x \in \mathbf{R}^4\), where \(x_j\) is the level of activity \(j\). Resource usage is \(Ax \in \mathbf{R}^5\), with
\[
A=
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 2 & 0 & 1 \\\\
0 & 0 & 3 & 1 \\\\
0 & 3 & 1 & 1 \\\\
2 & 1 & 2 & 5 \\\\
1 & 0 & 3 & 2
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad\nc^{\max}=
\begin{bmatrix}
100 \\\\
100 \\\\
100 \\\\
100 \\\\
100
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad\np=
\begin{bmatrix}
3 \\\\
2 \\\\
7 \\\\
6
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad\np^{\mathrm{disc}}=
\begin{bmatrix}
2 \\\\
1 \\\\
4 \\\\
2
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad\nq=
\begin{bmatrix}
4 \\\\
10 \\\\
5 \\\\
10
\end{bmatrix}.
\]
For each \(j=1,2,3,4\), the revenue from activity \(j\) is the piecewise-linear concave function
\[\nr_j(x_j)=
\begin{cases}\np_j x_j, & 0 \le x_j \le q_j,\\np_j q_j + p_j^{\mathrm{disc}}(x_j-q_j), & x_j \ge q_j.
\end{cases}
\]
The total revenue is
\(\sum_{j=1}^4 r_j(x_j).\)
The constraints are
\(Ax \le c^{\max}, \qquad x \ge 0.\)
Rewrite this problem in a formal optimization form, for example as an equivalent linear program, and solve it.

1. Find an optimal activity vector \(x^{\star} \in \mathbf{R}^4\).

2. For each \(j=1,2,3,4\), compute \(r_j(x_j^{\star})\), and compute the optimal total revenue
\(\sum_{j=1}^4 r_j(x_j^{\star}).\)

3. For each \(j\) such that \(x_j^{\star} > 0\), compute the average price per unit
\(\frac{r_j(x_j^{\star})}{x_j^{\star}},\)\nand verify that it lies between \(p_j^{\mathrm{disc}}\) and \(p_j\).

4. Give a brief interpretation of the optimal solution.

problem:
Exercise 3.5
Minimizing the ratio of convex and concave piecewise-linear functions. We consider the problem

\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\mbox{minimize} & \dfrac{\max_{i=1,\ldots,m}(a_i^T x+b_i)}{\min_{i=1,\ldots,p}(c_i^T x+d_i)} \\
\mbox{subject to} & Fx \preceq g,
\end{array}
\]

with variable $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$. We assume that $c_i^T x + d_i > 0$ and $\max_{i=1,\ldots,m}(a_i^T x + b_i) \geq 0$ for all $x$ satisfying $F x \preceq g$, and that the feasible set is nonempty and bounded. This problem is quasiconvex, and can be solved using bisection, with each iteration involving a feasibility LP. Show how the problem can be solved by solving one LP, using a trick similar to one described in \S4.3.2.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 3.5
Minimizing the ratio of convex and concave piecewise-linear functions. We consider the problem

\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\mbox{minimize} & \dfrac{\max_{i=1,\ldots,m}(a_i^T x+b_i)}{\min_{i=1,\ldots,p}(c_i^T x+d_i)} \\
\mbox{subject to} & Fx \preceq g,
\end{array}
\]

with variable \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\). We assume that \(c_i^T x + d_i > 0\) and \(\max_{i=1,\ldots,m}(a_i^T x + b_i) \geq 0\) for all \(x\) satisfying \(F x \preceq g\), and that the feasible set is nonempty and bounded. This problem is quasiconvex, and can be solved using bisection, with each iteration involving a feasibility LP. Show how the problem can be solved by solving one LP, using a trick similar to one described in \S4.3.2.
problem_standardized_math:
Consider the problem
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\text{minimize} & \displaystyle \frac{\max_{i=1,\ldots,m}(a_i^T x+b_i)}{\min_{i=1,\ldots,p}(c_i^T x+d_i)} \\
\text{subject to} & Fx \preceq g,
\end{array}
\]
with variable \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\). Assume that, for all \(x\) satisfying \(Fx \preceq g\), we have
\(c_i^T x + d_i > 0, \qquad i=1,\ldots,p,\)
and
\(\max_{i=1,\ldots,m}(a_i^T x+b_i) \ge 0,\)
and also that the feasible set is nonempty and bounded. Show how this problem can be solved by solving a single linear program, using a trick similar to that described in \S4.3.2.
problem_finally:
Let
\(a_i \in \mathbf{R}^n,\quad b_i \in \mathbf{R},\quad i=1,\ldots,m,\)
\(c_i \in \mathbf{R}^n,\quad d_i \in \mathbf{R},\quad i=1,\ldots,p,\)
and
\(F \in \mathbf{R}^{q \times n},\quad g \in \mathbf{R}^q.\)
Consider
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\text{minimize} & \displaystyle \frac{\max_{i=1,\ldots,m}(a_i^T x + b_i)}{\min_{i=1,\ldots,p}(c_i^T x + d_i)} \\
\text{subject to} & Fx \le g,
\end{array}
\]
with decision variable \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\).

Assume:
\(\{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid Fx \le g\}\)
is nonempty and bounded, and for every feasible \(x\),
\(c_i^T x + d_i > 0, \quad i=1,\ldots,p,\)
and
\(\max_{i=1,\ldots,m}(a_i^T x + b_i) \ge 0.\)

1. Show that the problem can be transformed into an equivalent linear program.
2. Derive a single linear program whose optimal solution can be used to recover an optimal solution of the original problem.

problem:
Exercise 3.6
Two problems involving two norms. We consider the problem

\[
\mbox{minimize} \quad \frac{\|Ax-b\|_1}{1-\|x\|_\infty},
\tag{1}
\]

and the very closely related problem

\[
\mbox{minimize} \quad \frac{\|Ax-b\|_1^2}{1-\|x\|_\infty}.
\tag{2}
\]

In both problems, the variable is $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$, and the data are $A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}$ and $b \in \mathbf{R}^m$. Note that the only difference between problem (1) and (2) is the square in the numerator. In both problems, the constraint $\|x\|_\infty < 1$ is implicit. You can assume that $b \notin \mathcal{R}(A)$, in which case the constraint $\|x\|_\infty < 1$ can be replaced with $\|x\|_\infty \leq 1$.

Answer the following two questions, for each of the two problems. (So you will answer four questions all together.)

(a) Is the problem, exactly as stated (and for all problem data), convex? If not, is it quasiconvex? Justify your answer.

problem_finally:

problem:
Exercise 3.6
Two problems involving two norms. We consider the problem

\[
\mbox{minimize} \quad \frac{\|Ax-b\|_1}{1-\|x\|_\infty},
\tag{1}
\]

and the very closely related problem

\[
\mbox{minimize} \quad \frac{\|Ax-b\|_1^2}{1-\|x\|_\infty}.
\tag{2}
\]

In both problems, the variable is $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$, and the data are $A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}$ and $b \in \mathbf{R}^m$. Note that the only difference between problem (1) and (2) is the square in the numerator. In both problems, the constraint $\|x\|_\infty < 1$ is implicit. You can assume that $b \notin \mathcal{R}(A)$, in which case the constraint $\|x\|_\infty < 1$ can be replaced with $\|x\|_\infty \leq 1$.

Answer the following two questions, for each of the two problems. (So you will answer four questions all together.)

(b) Explain how to solve the problem. Your method can involve an SDP solver, an SOCP solver, an LP solver, or any combination. You can include a one-parameter bisection, if necessary. (For example, you can solve the problem by bisection on a parameter, where each iteration consists of solving an SOCP feasibility problem.) Give the best method you can. In judging best, we use the following rules:

- Bisection methods are worse than `one-shot' methods. Any method that solves the problem above by solving one LP, SOCP, or SDP problem is better than any method that uses a one-parameter bisection. In other words, use a bisection method only if you cannot find a `one-shot' method.
- Use the simplest solver needed to solve the problem. We consider an LP solver to be simpler than an SOCP solver, which is considered simpler than an SDP solver. Thus, a method that uses an LP solver is better than a method that uses an SOCP solver, which in turn is better than a method that uses an SDP solver.
