The vehicle is required to arrive at waypoint \(i\), for \(i=1,\ldots,n\), between times \(\tau_i^{\min}\) and \(\tau_i^{\max}\), which are given. The vehicle consumes fuel over segment \(i\) at a rate that depends on its speed, \(\Phi(s_i)\), where \(\Phi\) is positive, increasing, and convex, and has units of kg/s.

You are given the data \(d\) (segment travel distances), \(s^{\min}\) and \(s^{\max}\) (speed bounds), \(\tau^{\min}\) and \(\tau^{\max}\) (waypoint arrival time bounds), and the fuel use function \(\Phi:\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{R}\). You are to choose the speeds \(s_1,\ldots,s_n\) so as to minimize the total fuel consumed in kg.

(a) Show how to pose this as a convex optimization problem. If you introduce new variables, or change variables, you must explain how to recover the optimal speeds from the solution of your problem. If convexity of the objective or any constraint function in your formulation is not obvious, explain why it is convex.
problem_standardized_math:
A vehicle travels along a fixed path consisting of \(n\) segments between \(n+1\) waypoints labeled \(0,\ldots,n\). Segment \(i\) starts at waypoint \(i-1\) and ends at waypoint \(i\). The vehicle starts at time \(t=0\) at waypoint \(0\). On segment \(i\), it travels at a constant nonnegative speed \(s_i\), subject to the bounds \(s^{\min} \leq s_i \leq s^{\max}\). The vehicle does not stop at the waypoints. The length of segment \(i\) is \(d_i>0\), so the travel time on segment \(i\) is \(d_i/s_i\). Let \(\tau_i\), for \(i=1,\ldots,n\), denote the arrival time at waypoint \(i\). These arrival times must satisfy
\(\tau_i^{\min} \leq \tau_i \leq \tau_i^{\max}, \qquad i=1,\ldots,n.\)
The fuel consumption rate on segment \(i\) is \(\Phi(s_i)\), where \(\Phi:\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{R}\) is positive, increasing, and convex, with units of kg/s.

Given the data \(d\), \(s^{\min}\), \(s^{\max}\), \(\tau^{\min}\), \(\tau^{\max}\), and \(\Phi\), choose the speeds \(s_1,\ldots,s_n\) to minimize the total fuel consumed.

(a) Show how to formulate this problem as a convex optimization problem. If you introduce new variables or make a change of variables, explain how to recover the optimal speeds from the solution of your formulation. If the convexity of the objective or of any constraint function in your formulation is not immediate, explain why it is convex.
problem_finally:
Let \(n \in \mathbf{N}\). A vehicle traverses \(n\) consecutive path segments with waypoint indices \(0,1,\ldots,n\). Segment \(i\), for \(i=1,\ldots,n\), has length \(d_i > 0\). The vehicle starts at waypoint \(0\) at time \(0\), does not stop at intermediate waypoints, and travels on segment \(i\) at constant speed \(s_i\).

The speed bounds are
\(s^{\min} \le s_i \le s^{\max}, \qquad i=1,\ldots,n,\)\nwith \(0 < s^{\min} \le s^{\max}\).

The travel time on segment \(i\) is \(d_i/s_i\), so the arrival times at waypoints \(i=1,\ldots,n\) are
\(\tau_i = \sum_{j=1}^i \frac{d_j}{s_j}, \qquad i=1,\ldots,n.\)
These must satisfy the time-window constraints
\(\tau_i^{\min} \le \tau_i \le \tau_i^{\max}, \qquad i=1,\ldots,n.\)

The fuel consumption rate on segment \(i\) is \(\Phi(s_i)\), where \(\Phi : \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{R}\) is positive, increasing, and convex. Hence the fuel consumed on segment \(i\) is
\(\Phi(s_i) \cdot \frac{d_i}{s_i},\)\nand the total fuel consumption is
\(\sum_{i=1}^n \Phi(s_i) \frac{d_i}{s_i}.\)

Given
\((d_1,\ldots,d_n), \quad s^{\min}, \quad s^{\max}, \quad (\tau_1^{\min},\ldots,\tau_n^{\min}), \quad (\tau_1^{\max},\ldots,\tau_n^{\max}), \quad \Phi,\)\ncomplete the following task:

1. Formulate the problem of choosing \(s_1,\ldots,s_n\) to minimize total fuel consumption as a convex optimization problem. If a change of variables or auxiliary variables is used, state the new formulation explicitly and explain how to recover the optimal speeds \(s_1,\ldots,s_n\) from a solution of the convex formulation. If convexity of the objective or any constraint is not immediate, justify it.

problem:
Exercise 3.20
Optimal vehicle speed scheduling. A vehicle (say, an airplane) travels along a fixed path of $n$ segments, between $n+1$ waypoints labeled $0,\ldots,n$. Segment $i$ starts at waypoint $i-1$ and terminates at waypoint $i$. The vehicle starts at time $t=0$ at waypoint 0. It travels over each segment at a constant (nonnegative) speed; $s_i$ is the speed on segment $i$. We have lower and upper limits on the speeds: $s^{\min} \leq s \leq s^{\max}$. The vehicle does not stop at the waypoints; it simply proceeds to the next segment. The travel distance of segment $i$ is $d_i$ (which is positive), so the travel time over segment $i$ is $d_i/s_i$. We let $\tau_i$, $i=1,\ldots,n$, denote the time at which the vehicle arrives at waypoint $i$. The vehicle is required to arrive at waypoint $i$, for $i=1,\ldots,n$, between times $\tau_i^{\min}$ and $\tau_i^{\max}$, which are given. The vehicle consumes fuel over segment $i$ at a rate that depends on its speed, $\Phi(s_i)$, where $\Phi$ is positive, increasing, and convex, and has units of kg/s.

You are given the data $d$ (segment travel distances), $s^{\min}$ and $s^{\max}$ (speed bounds), $\tau^{\min}$ and $\tau^{\max}$ (waypoint arrival time bounds), and the fuel use function $\Phi:\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{R}$. You are to choose the speeds $s_1,\ldots,s_n$ so as to minimize the total fuel consumed in kg.

(b) Carry out the method of part (a) on the problem instance with data in \texttt{veh\_speed\_sched\_data.m}. Use the fuel use function $\Phi(s_i)=as_i^2+bs_i+c$ (the parameters $a$, $b$, and $c$ are defined in the data file). What is the optimal fuel consumption? Plot the optimal speed versus segment, using the matlab command \texttt{stairs} or the function \texttt{step} from matplotlib in Python and Julia to better show constant speed over the segments.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 3.20
Optimal vehicle speed scheduling. A vehicle (say, an airplane) travels along a fixed path of \(n\) segments, between \(n+1\) waypoints labeled \(0,\ldots,n\). Segment \(i\) starts at waypoint \(i-1\) and terminates at waypoint \(i\). The vehicle starts at time \(t=0\) at waypoint 0. It travels over each segment at a constant (nonnegative) speed; \(s_i\) is the speed on segment \(i\). We have lower and upper limits on the speeds: \(s^{\min} \leq s \leq s^{\max}\). The vehicle does not stop at the waypoints; it simply proceeds to the next segment. The travel distance of segment \(i\) is \(d_i\) (which is positive), so the travel time over segment \(i\) is \(d_i/s_i\). We let \(\tau_i\), \(i=1,\ldots,n\), denote the time at which the vehicle arrives at waypoint \(i\). The vehicle is required to arrive at waypoint \(i\), for \(i=1,\ldots,n\), between times \(\tau_i^{\min}\) and \(\tau_i^{\max}\), which are given. The vehicle consumes fuel over segment \(i\) at a rate that depends on its speed, \(\Phi(s_i)\), where \(\Phi\) is positive, increasing, and convex, and has units of kg/s.

You are given the data \(d\) (segment travel distances), \(s^{\min}\) and \(s^{\max}\) (speed bounds), \(\tau^{\min}\) and \(\tau^{\max}\) (waypoint arrival time bounds), and the fuel use function \(\Phi:\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{R}\). You are to choose the speeds \(s_1,\ldots,s_n\) so as to minimize the total fuel consumed in kg.

(b) Carry out the method of part (a) on the problem instance with data in \texttt{veh\_speed\_sched\_data.m}. Use the fuel use function \(\Phi(s_i)=as_i^2+bs_i+c\) (the parameters \(a\), \(b\), and \(c\) are defined in the data file). What is the optimal fuel consumption? Plot the optimal speed versus segment, using the matlab command \texttt{stairs} or the function \texttt{step} from matplotlib in Python and Julia to better show constant speed over the segments.
problem_standardized_math:
Consider a vehicle traveling along a fixed path of \(n\) segments between waypoints \(0,\ldots,n\). Segment \(i\) goes from waypoint \(i-1\) to waypoint \(i\). The vehicle starts at time \(t=0\) at waypoint \(0\), travels over segment \(i\) at constant nonnegative speed \(s_i\), and does not stop at the waypoints. The segment distance is \(d_i>0\), so the travel time over segment \(i\) is \(d_i/s_i\). Let \(\tau_i\), \(i=1,\ldots,n\), denote the arrival time at waypoint \(i\). The speeds must satisfy the bounds \(s^{\min} \leq s_i \leq s^{\max}\), and the arrival times must satisfy \(\tau_i^{\min} \leq \tau_i \leq \tau_i^{\max}\) for \(i=1,\ldots,n\). The fuel-use rate on segment \(i\) is \(\Phi(s_i)\), and in this instance \(\Phi(s_i)=a s_i^2+b s_i+c\), where \(a\), \(b\), and \(c\) are given in \texttt{veh\\_speed\\_sched\\_data.m}.

Using the data \(d\), \(s^{\min}\), \(s^{\max}\), \(\tau^{\min}\), and \(\tau^{\max}\) from \texttt{veh\\_speed\\_sched\\_data.m}, carry out the method of part (a) to choose \(s_1,\ldots,s_n\) so as to minimize the total fuel consumed. What is the optimal fuel consumption? Plot the optimal speed versus segment, using a step plot (e.g., \texttt{stairs} in MATLAB or \texttt{step} in matplotlib) to show the constant speed over each segment.
problem_finally:
Given a route with waypoints \(0,1,\ldots,n\), for each segment \(i=1,\ldots,n\): segment \(i\) connects waypoint \(i-1\) to waypoint \(i\), has length \(d_i>0\), and is traversed at constant speed \(s_i\). The vehicle starts at waypoint \(0\) at time \(\tau_0=0\), and does not stop at intermediate waypoints.

The decision variables are \(s_1,\ldots,s_n\), subject to
\(s^{\min} \le s_i \le s^{\max}, \qquad i=1,\ldots,n.\)
Arrival times satisfy
\(\tau_i = \sum_{k=1}^i \frac{d_k}{s_k}, \qquad i=1,\ldots,n,\)\nand the time-window constraints are
\(\tau_i^{\min} \le \tau_i \le \tau_i^{\max}, \qquad i=1,\ldots,n.\)

The fuel-use rate on segment \(i\) is
\(\Phi(s_i)=a s_i^2 + b s_i + c,\)\nso the fuel consumed on segment \(i\) is
\(\Phi(s_i)\frac{d_i}{s_i} = \left(a s_i^2 + b s_i + c\right)\frac{d_i}{s_i}.\)
Hence the total fuel consumption is
\(\sum_{i=1}^n \left(a s_i^2 + b s_i + c\right)\frac{d_i}{s_i}.\)

Using the numerical data \(d\), \(s^{\min}\), \(s^{\max}\), \(\tau^{\min}\), \(\tau^{\max}\), and \(a,b,c\) provided in \texttt{veh\\_speed\\_sched\\_data.m}, formulate and solve the optimization problem
\[
\begin{aligned}
\min_{s_1,\ldots,s_n} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^n \left(a s_i^2 + b s_i + c\right)\frac{d_i}{s_i} \\\\
\text{s.t.} \quad & s^{\min} \le s_i \le s^{\max}, \qquad i=1,\ldots,n, \\\\
& \tau_i = \sum_{k=1}^i \frac{d_k}{s_k}, \qquad i=1,\ldots,n, \\\\
& \tau_i^{\min} \le \tau_i \le \tau_i^{\max}, \qquad i=1,\ldots,n.
\end{aligned}
\]

1. Compute the optimal speeds \(s_1,\ldots,s_n\).
2. Report the corresponding optimal total fuel consumption.
3. Plot the optimal speed versus segment index using a step plot, with constant speed on each segment shown explicitly.

problem:
Exercise 3.21
Norm approximation via SOCP, for $\ell_p$-norms with rational $p$.

(a) Use the observation at the beginning of exercise 4.26 in \emph{Convex Optimization} to express the constraint

\[
y \leq \sqrt{z_1z_2}, \qquad y,z_1,z_2 \geq 0,
\]

with variables $y$, $z_1$, $z_2$, as a second-order cone constraint. Then extend your result to the constraint

\[
y \leq (z_1z_2\cdots z_n)^{1/n}, \qquad y \geq 0, \qquad z \succeq 0,
\]

where $n$ is a positive integer, and the variables are $y\in\mathbf{R}$ and $z\in\mathbf{R}^n$. First assume that $n$ is a power of two, and then generalize your formulation to arbitrary positive integers.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 3.21
Norm approximation via SOCP, for \(\ell_p\)-norms with rational \(p\).

(a) Use the observation at the beginning of exercise 4.26 in \emph{Convex Optimization} to express the constraint

\(y \leq \sqrt{z_1z_2}, \qquad y,z_1,z_2 \geq 0,\)

with variables \(y\), \(z_1\), \(z_2\), as a second-order cone constraint. Then extend your result to the constraint

\(y \leq (z_1z_2\cdots z_n)^{1/n}, \qquad y \geq 0, \qquad z \succeq 0,\)

where \(n\) is a positive integer, and the variables are \(y\in\mathbf{R}\) and \(z\in\mathbf{R}^n\). First assume that \(n\) is a power of two, and then generalize your formulation to arbitrary positive integers.
problem_standardized_math:
Express the constraint
\(y \leq \sqrt{z_1 z_2}, \qquad y,z_1,z_2 \geq 0,\)\nin the variables \(y, z_1, z_2\) as a second-order cone constraint. Then extend this to the constraint
\(y \leq (z_1 z_2 \cdots z_n)^{1/n}, \qquad y \geq 0, \qquad z \succeq 0,\)\nwhere \(n\) is a positive integer and the variables are \(y \in \mathbf{R}\) and \(z \in \mathbf{R}^n\). First treat the case in which \(n\) is a power of two, and then generalize the formulation to arbitrary positive integers.
problem_finally:
Let \(y \in \mathbf{R}\) and \(z = (z_1,\dots,z_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n\).

Rewrite each of the following geometric-mean constraints as equivalent second-order cone representable constraints.

1. For \(n=2\), give an equivalent second-order cone formulation in the variables \(y,z_1,z_2\) of
\(y \le (z_1 z_2)^{1/2}, \qquad y \ge 0, \qquad z_1 \ge 0, \qquad z_2 \ge 0.\)

2. Assume \(n = 2^k\) for some \(k \in \mathbf{N}\). Formulate as an equivalent system of second-order cone constraints the condition
\(y \le (z_1 z_2 \cdots z_n)^{1/n}, \qquad y \ge 0, \qquad z_i \ge 0 \quad \text{for } i=1,\dots,n.\)
Auxiliary variables may be introduced if needed.

3. Extend the construction in item 2 to arbitrary \(n \in \mathbf{N}\). Give an equivalent second-order cone representation of
\(y \le (z_1 z_2 \cdots z_n)^{1/n}, \qquad y \ge 0, \qquad z_i \ge 0 \quad \text{for } i=1,\dots,n.\)
Auxiliary variables may be introduced if needed, but the formulation must remain equivalent to the original constraint.

problem:
Exercise 3.21
Norm approximation via SOCP, for $\ell_p$-norms with rational $p$.

(b) Express the constraint

\[
f(x)\leq t
\]

as a second-order cone constraint, for the following two convex functions $f$:

\[
f(x)=\begin{cases}
x^\alpha & x\geq 0\\
0 & x<0,
\end{cases}
\]

where $\alpha$ is rational and greater than or equal to one, and

\[
f(x)=x^\alpha,\qquad \mathbf{dom}\,f=\mathbf{R}_{++},
\]

where $\alpha$ is rational and negative.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 3.21
Norm approximation via SOCP, for \(\ell_p\)-norms with rational \(p\).

(b) Express the constraint

\(f(x)\leq t\)

as a second-order cone constraint, for the following two convex functions \(f\):

f(x)=\begin{cases}
x^\alpha & x\geq 0\\
0 & x<0,

\]

where \(\alpha\) is rational and greater than or equal to one, and

\(f(x)=x^\alpha,\qquad \mathbf{dom}\,f=\mathbf{R}_{++},\)

where \(\alpha\) is rational and negative.
problem_standardized_math:
Express the constraint \(f(x) \le t\) as a second-order cone constraint for each of the following convex functions \(f\):

1.
\[\nf(x)=\begin{cases}\nx^\alpha, & x \ge 0,\\\\
0, & x < 0,
\end{cases}
\]\nwhere \(\alpha\) is rational and \(\alpha \ge 1\).

2.
\(f(x)=x^\alpha, \qquad \operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{R}_{++},\)\nwhere \(\alpha\) is rational and negative.
problem_finally:
Express the constraint \(f(x) \le t\) as a second-order cone constraint for each of the following convex functions \(f\):

1.
\[\nf(x)=\begin{cases}\nx^\alpha, & x \ge 0,\\\\
0, & x < 0,

2.
\(f(x)=x^\alpha, \qquad \operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{R}_{++},\)\nwhere \(\alpha\) is rational and negative.

problem:
Exercise 3.21
Norm approximation via SOCP, for $\ell_p$-norms with rational $p$.

(c) Formulate the norm approximation problem

\[
\text{minimize}\quad \|Ax-b\|_p
\]

as a second-order cone program, where $p$ is a rational number greater than or equal to one. The variable in the optimization problem is $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$. The matrix $A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}$ and the vector $b \in \mathbf{R}^m$ are given. For an $m$-vector $y$, the norm $\|y\|_p$ is defined as

\[
\|y\|_p=\left(\sum_{k=1}^m |y_k|^p\right)^{1/p}
\]

when $p \geq 1$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 3.21
Norm approximation via SOCP, for \(\ell_p\)-norms with rational \(p\).

(c) Formulate the norm approximation problem

\(\text{minimize}\quad \|Ax-b\|_p\)

as a second-order cone program, where \(p\) is a rational number greater than or equal to one. The variable in the optimization problem is \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\). The matrix \(A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}\) and the vector \(b \in \mathbf{R}^m\) are given. For an \(m\)-vector \(y\), the norm \(\|y\|_p\) is defined as

\(\|y\|_p=\left(\sum_{k=1}^m |y_k|^p\right)^{1/p}\)

when \(p \geq 1\).
problem_standardized_math:
Formulate the norm approximation problem
\(\min_{x \in \mathbf{R}^n} \|Ax-b\|_p\)
as a second-order cone program, where \(A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}\) and \(b \in \mathbf{R}^m\) are given, and \(p\) is a rational number satisfying \(p \ge 1\). For \(y \in \mathbf{R}^m\), the \(\ell_p\)-norm is defined by
\(\|y\|_p = \left( \sum_{k=1}^m |y_k|^p \right)^{1/p}.\)
problem_finally:
Let \(A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}\), \(b \in \mathbf{R}^m\), and \(p \in \mathbf{Q}\) with \(p \ge 1\). For \(y = (y_1,\dots,y_m) \in \mathbf{R}^m\), define
\(\|y\|_p = \left(\sum_{k=1}^m |y_k|^p\right)^{1/p}.\)
Formulate the optimization problem
\(\min_{x \in \mathbf{R}^n} \|Ax-b\|_p\)
as a second-order cone program. Give a self-contained formulation with all auxiliary variables and constraints written explicitly in standard second-order cone form, or using equivalent second-order cone representable constraints.

problem:
Exercise 3.22
Linear optimization over the complement of a convex set. Suppose $C\subseteq \mathbf{R}_+^n$ is a closed bounded convex set with $0\in C$, and $c\in \mathbf{R}_+^n$. We define

\[
\tilde C=\mathrm{cl}(\mathbf{R}_+^n\setminus C)=\mathrm{cl}\{x\in \mathbf{R}_+^n\mid x\notin C\},
\]

which is the closure of the complement of $C$ in $\mathbf{R}_+^n$.

Show that $c^T x$ has a minimizer over $\tilde C$ of the form $\alpha e_k$, where $\alpha\geq 0$ and $e_k$ is the $k$th standard unit vector. (If you have not had a course on analysis, you can give an intuitive argument.)

If follows that we can minimize $c^T x$ over $\tilde C$ by solving $n$ one-dimensional optimization problems (which, indeed, can each be solved by bisection, provided we can check whether a point is in $C$ or not).
problem_with_context:
Exercise 3.22
Linear optimization over the complement of a convex set. Suppose \(C\subseteq \mathbf{R}_+^n\) is a closed bounded convex set with \(0\in C\), and \(c\in \mathbf{R}_+^n\). We define

\(\tilde C=\mathrm{cl}(\mathbf{R}_+^n\setminus C)=\mathrm{cl}\{x\in \mathbf{R}_+^n\mid x\notin C\},\)

which is the closure of the complement of \(C\) in \(\mathbf{R}_+^n\).

Show that \(c^T x\) has a minimizer over \(\tilde C\) of the form \(\alpha e_k\), where \(\alpha\geq 0\) and \(e_k\) is the \(k\)th standard unit vector. (If you have not had a course on analysis, you can give an intuitive argument.)

If follows that we can minimize \(c^T x\) over \(\tilde C\) by solving \(n\) one-dimensional optimization problems (which, indeed, can each be solved by bisection, provided we can check whether a point is in \(C\) or not).
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}_+^n\) be a closed, bounded, convex set with \(0 \in C\), and let \(c \in \mathbf{R}_+^n\). Define
\(\tilde C = \mathrm{cl}(\mathbf{R}_+^n \setminus C)=\mathrm{cl}\{x \in \mathbf{R}_+^n \mid x \notin C\},\)\nthe closure of the complement of \(C\) in \(\mathbf{R}_+^n\).

Show that the function \(c^T x\) has a minimizer over \(\tilde C\) of the form \(\alpha e_k\), where \(\alpha \ge 0\) and \(e_k\) is the \(k\)th standard unit vector.

It follows that minimizing \(c^T x\) over \(\tilde C\) can be reduced to solving \(n\) one-dimensional optimization problems, each of which can be solved by bisection provided membership in \(C\) can be tested.
problem_finally:
Let \(n \in \mathbf{N}\). Let \(C \subseteq \mathbf{R}_+^n\) be nonempty, closed, bounded, and convex, with \(0 \in C\), where
\(\mathbf{R}_+^n := \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid x_i \ge 0,\ i=1,\dots,n\}.\)
Let \(c \in \mathbf{R}_+^n\), and define
\[
\tilde C := \operatorname{cl}(\mathbf{R}_+^n \setminus C)
= \operatorname{cl}\bigl(\{x \in \mathbf{R}_+^n \mid x \notin C\}\bigr),
\]\nwhere the closure is taken in \(\mathbf{R}^n\).

Prove the following.

1. The linear function \(x \mapsto c^T x\) attains its minimum over \(\tilde C\).

2. There exists a minimizer \(x \in \tilde C\) of the form
\(x = \alpha e_k\)\nfor some \(\alpha \ge 0\) and some \(k \in \{1,\dots,n\}\), where \(e_k \in \mathbf{R}^n\) is the \(k\)-th standard unit vector.

3. Deduce that
\(\min\{c^T x \mid x \in \tilde C\}\)\ncan be reduced to the \(n\) one-dimensional problems
\(\min\{c^T(\alpha e_k) \mid \alpha \ge 0,\ \alpha e_k \in \tilde C\}, \qquad k=1,\dots,n.\)
Explain why each of these one-dimensional problems can be solved by bisection if membership in \(C\) can be tested.

problem:
Exercise 3.23
Jensen's inequality for posynomials. Suppose $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to \mathbf{R}$ is a posynomial function, $x,y\in \mathbf{R}_{++}^n$, and $\theta\in[0,1]$. Define $z\in \mathbf{R}_{++}^n$ by $z_i=x_i^\theta y_i^{1-\theta}$, $i=1,\ldots,n$. Show that $f(z)\leq f(x)^\theta f(y)^{1-\theta}$.

\emph{Interpretation}. We can think of $z$ as a $\theta$-weighted geometric mean between $x$ and $y$. So the statement above is that a posynomial, evaluated at a weighted geometric mean of two points, is no more than the weighted geometric mean of the posynomial evaluated at the two points.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 3.23
Jensen's inequality for posynomials. Suppose \(f:\mathbf{R}^n\to \mathbf{R}\) is a posynomial function, \(x,y\in \mathbf{R}_{++}^n\), and \(\theta\in[0,1]\). Define \(z\in \mathbf{R}_{++}^n\) by \(z_i=x_i^\theta y_i^{1-\theta}\), \(i=1,\ldots,n\). Show that \(f(z)\leq f(x)^\theta f(y)^{1-\theta}\).

\emph{Interpretation}. We can think of \(z\) as a \(\theta\)-weighted geometric mean between \(x\) and \(y\). So the statement above is that a posynomial, evaluated at a weighted geometric mean of two points, is no more than the weighted geometric mean of the posynomial evaluated at the two points.
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be a posynomial function, let \(x,y \in \mathbf{R}_{++}^n\), and let \(\theta \in [0,1]\). Define \(z \in \mathbf{R}_{++}^n\) by
\(z_i = x_i^\theta y_i^{1-\theta}, \qquad i=1,\ldots,n.\)
Show that
\(f(z) \leq f(x)^\theta f(y)^{1-\theta}.\)
problem_finally:
Let \(f : \mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}_{++}\) be a posynomial, i.e., there exist \(m \in \mathbf{N}\), coefficients \(c_k > 0\), and exponent vectors \(a_k = (a_{k1}, \ldots, a_{kn}) \in \mathbf{R}^n\) for \(k=1, \ldots, m\) such that
\(f(x) = \sum_{k=1}^m c_k x_1^{a_{k1}} \cdots x_n^{a_{kn}}, \qquad x = (x_1, \ldots, x_n) \in \mathbf{R}_{++}^n.\)
Given \(x, y \in \mathbf{R}_{++}^n\) and \(\theta \in [0,1]\), define \(z \in \mathbf{R}_{++}^n\) by
\(z_i = x_i^{\theta} y_i^{1-\theta}, \qquad i=1, \ldots, n.\)
Prove that
\(f(z) \le f(x)^{\theta} f(y)^{1-\theta}.\)

problem:
Exercise 3.24
CVX implementation of a concave function. Consider the concave function $f:\mathbf{R}\to \mathbf{R}$ defined by

\[
f(x)=\begin{cases}
(x+1)/2 & x\geq 1\\
\sqrt{x} & 0\leq x\leq 1,
\end{cases}
\]

with $\mathbf{dom}\,f=\mathbf{R}_+$. Give a CVX implementation of $f$, via a partially specified optimization problem. Check your implementation by maximizing $f(x)+f(a-x)$ for several interesting values of $a$ (say, $a=-1$, $a=1$, and $a=3$).
problem_with_context:
Exercise 3.24
CVX implementation of a concave function. Consider the concave function \(f:\mathbf{R}\to \mathbf{R}\) defined by

\[
f(x)=\begin{cases}
(x+1)/2 & x\geq 1\\
\sqrt{x} & 0\leq x\leq 1,
\end{cases}
\]

with \(\mathbf{dom}\,f=\mathbf{R}_+\). Give a CVX implementation of \(f\), via a partially specified optimization problem. Check your implementation by maximizing \(f(x)+f(a-x)\) for several interesting values of \(a\) (say, \(a=-1\), \(a=1\), and \(a=3\)).
problem_standardized_math:
Consider the concave function \(f:\mathbf{R}\to\mathbf{R}\) with domain \(\mathbf{R}_+\), defined by
\[\nf(x)=
\begin{cases}
(x+1)/2, & x\geq 1,\\\\
\sqrt{x}, & 0\leq x\leq 1.
\end{cases}
\]
Give a CVX implementation of \(f\) via a partially specified optimization problem. Verify your implementation by maximizing \(f(x)+f(a-x)\) for several values of \(a\), for example \(a=-1\), \(a=1\), and \(a=3\).
problem_finally:
Let \(f : \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{R}\) be a concave function with effective domain \(\mathbf{R}_+ = \{x \in \mathbf{R} \mid x \ge 0\}\), defined by
\[
f(x) =
\begin{cases}
(x+1)/2, & x \ge 1,\\
\sqrt{x}, & 0 \le x \le 1.
\end{cases}
\]

1. Construct a CVX implementation of \(f\) using a partially specified optimization problem. Specifically, give an optimization model whose optimal value equals \(f(x)\) for every \(x \in \mathbf{R}_+\).

2. Verify the implementation numerically by solving, for prescribed parameters \(a \in \mathbf{R}\), the optimization problem
\(\max_{x \in \mathbf{R}} \; f(x) + f(a-x).\)
Include all domain constraints required so that both \(f(x)\) and \(f(a-x)\) are well defined.

3. Perform the verification for several values of \(a\), for example
\(a = -1, \qquad a = 1, \qquad a = 3.\)

problem:
Exercise 3.25
The following optimization problem arises in portfolio optimization:

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{maximize}\quad & \dfrac{r^T x + d}{\|Rx+q\|_2} \\
\text{subject to}\quad & \sum_{i=1}^n f_i(x_i) \leq b \\
& x \succeq c.
\end{aligned}
\]

The variable is $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$. The functions $f_i$ are defined as

\[
f_i(x)=\alpha_i x_i + \beta_i |x_i| + \gamma_i |x_i|^{3/2},
\]

with $\beta_i > |\alpha_i|$, $\gamma_i > 0$. We assume there exists a feasible $x$ with $r^T x + d > 0$. Show that this problem can be solved by solving an SOCP (if possible) or a sequence of SOCP feasibility problems (otherwise).
problem_with_context:
Exercise 3.25
The following optimization problem arises in portfolio optimization:

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{maximize}\quad & \dfrac{r^T x + d}{\|Rx+q\|_2} \\
\text{subject to}\quad & \sum_{i=1}^n f_i(x_i) \leq b \\
& x \succeq c.
\end{aligned}
\]

The variable is \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\). The functions \(f_i\) are defined as

\(f_i(x)=\alpha_i x_i + \beta_i |x_i| + \gamma_i |x_i|^{3/2},\)

with \(\beta_i > |\alpha_i|\), \(\gamma_i > 0\). We assume there exists a feasible \(x\) with \(r^T x + d > 0\). Show that this problem can be solved by solving an SOCP (if possible) or a sequence of SOCP feasibility problems (otherwise).
problem_standardized_math:
Consider the optimization problem
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{maximize} \quad & \frac{r^T x + d}{\\|Rx+q\\|_2} \\\\
\text{subject to} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^n f_i(x_i) \le b, \\\\
& x \succeq c,
\end{aligned}
\]\nwhere the variable is \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\), and
\(f_i(x_i)=\alpha_i x_i+\beta_i |x_i|+\gamma_i |x_i|^{3/2},\)\nwith \(\beta_i > |\alpha_i|\) and \(\gamma_i > 0\). Assume there exists a feasible \(x\) such that \(r^T x + d > 0\). Show that this problem can be solved either by solving a single second-order cone program (if possible) or by solving a sequence of second-order cone feasibility problems (otherwise).
problem_finally:
Consider the optimization problem
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{maximize} \quad & \frac{r^T x + d}{\\|Rx+q\\|_2} \\\\
\text{subject to} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^n f_i(x_i) \le b, \\\\
& x \ge c,
\end{aligned}
\]\nwith decision variable \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\), data
\(r \in \mathbf{R}^n, \quad d \in \mathbf{R}, \quad R \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}, \quad q \in \mathbf{R}^m, \quad c \in \mathbf{R}^n, \quad b \in \mathbf{R},\)\nand
\(f_i(x_i) = \alpha_i x_i + \beta_i |x_i| + \gamma_i |x_i|^{3/2}, \qquad i=1,\dots,n,\)\nwhere
\(\alpha_i, \beta_i, \gamma_i \in \mathbf{R}, \qquad \beta_i > |\alpha_i|, \qquad \gamma_i > 0.\)
Assume the feasible set is nonempty, and there exists a feasible point \(x\) such that \(r^T x + d > 0\).

Formulate an SOCP-based solution method for this problem. In particular:

1. Show that, under an appropriate reformulation condition, the problem can be reduced to a single second-order cone program.

2. Otherwise, show that the problem can be solved via a sequence of second-order cone feasibility problems.

3. Make explicit the second-order cone representation of both:
 \(\frac{r^T x + d}{\\|Rx+q\\|_2}\)
 and the constraint
 \(\sum_{i=1}^n \left(\alpha_i x_i + \beta_i |x_i| + \gamma_i |x_i|^{3/2}\right) \le b.\)
The reformulation must preserve the original problem exactly.

problem:
Exercise 3.26
\emph{Positive nonconvex QCQP.} We consider a (possibly nonconvex) QCQP, with nonnegative variable $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$,

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & f_0(x) \\
\text{subject to}\quad & f_i(x) \leq 0, \quad i=1,\ldots,m \\
& x \succeq 0,
\end{aligned}
\]

where $f_i(x) = (1/2)x^T P_i x + q_i^T x + r_i$, with $P_i \in \mathbf{S}^n$, $q_i \in \mathbf{R}^n$, and $r_i \in \mathbf{R}$, for $i=0,\ldots,m$. We do \emph{not} assume that $P_i \succeq 0$, so this need not be a convex problem. Suppose that $q_i \preceq 0$, and $P_i$ have nonpositive off-diagonal entries, \emph{i.e.}, they satisfy

\[
(P_i)_{jk} \leq 0, \quad j \neq k, \qquad j,k=1,\ldots,n,
\]

for $i=0,\ldots,m$. (A matrix with nonpositive off-diagonal entries is called a \emph{Z-matrix}.) Explain how to reformulate this problem as a convex problem.

\emph{Hint.} Change variables using $y_j = \phi(x_j)$, for some suitable function $\phi$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 3.26
\emph{Positive nonconvex QCQP.} We consider a (possibly nonconvex) QCQP, with nonnegative variable \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\),

\[
\begin{aligned}

\text{subject to}\quad & f_i(x) \leq 0, \quad i=1,\ldots,m \\

\end{aligned}
\]

where \(f_i(x) = (1/2)x^T P_i x + q_i^T x + r_i\), with \(P_i \in \mathbf{S}^n\), \(q_i \in \mathbf{R}^n\), and \(r_i \in \mathbf{R}\), for \(i=0,\ldots,m\). We do \emph{not} assume that \(P_i \succeq 0\), so this need not be a convex problem. Suppose that \(q_i \preceq 0\), and \(P_i\) have nonpositive off-diagonal entries, \emph{i.e.}, they satisfy

\((P_i)_{jk} \leq 0, \quad j \neq k, \qquad j,k=1,\ldots,n,\)

for \(i=0,\ldots,m\). (A matrix with nonpositive off-diagonal entries is called a \emph{Z-matrix}.) Explain how to reformulate this problem as a convex problem.

\emph{Hint.} Change variables using \(y_j = \phi(x_j)\), for some suitable function \(\phi\).
problem_standardized_math:
Consider the QCQP with nonnegative variable \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\):
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & f_0(x) \\\\
\text{subject to} \quad & f_i(x) \leq 0, \quad i=1,\ldots,m, \\\\
& x \succeq 0,
\end{aligned}
\]\nwhere, for \(i=0,\ldots,m\),
\(f_i(x)=\frac{1}{2}x^T P_i x+q_i^T x+r_i,\)\nwith \(P_i \in \mathbf{S}^n\), \(q_i \in \mathbf{R}^n\), and \(r_i \in \mathbf{R}\). Do not assume that \(P_i \succeq 0\), so the problem need not be convex. Suppose that \(q_i \preceq 0\) and each \(P_i\) has nonpositive off-diagonal entries, i.e.,
\((P_i)_{jk} \leq 0, \quad j \neq k, \qquad j,k=1,\ldots,n,\)\nfor \(i=0,\ldots,m\). Explain how to reformulate this problem as a convex problem.

Hint: Use a change of variables of the form \(y_j=\phi(x_j)\) for a suitable function \(\phi\).
problem_finally:
Consider the quadratically constrained quadratic program
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & f_0(x) \\\\
\text{subject to} \quad & f_i(x) \le 0, \quad i=1,\ldots,m, \\\\
& x \ge 0,
\end{aligned}
\]\nwith decision variable \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\), where for each \(i=0,1,\ldots,m\),
\(f_i(x) = \frac{1}{2} x^T P_i x + q_i^T x + r_i,\)\nand data satisfy
\(P_i \in \mathbf{S}^n, \qquad q_i \in \mathbf{R}^n, \qquad r_i \in \mathbf{R}.\)
Do not assume \(P_i \succeq 0\), so the problem need not be convex.

Assume that for every \(i=0,1,\ldots,m\),
\(q_i \le 0\)\ncomponentwise, and all off-diagonal entries of \(P_i\) are nonpositive:
\((P_i)_{jk} \le 0, \qquad j \ne k, \qquad j,k=1,\ldots,n.\)

1. Find a change of variables of the form
\(y_j = \phi(x_j), \qquad j=1,\ldots,n,\)\nfor a suitable function \(\phi\), that reformulates the problem equivalently.

2. Write the optimization problem explicitly in the new variables, including the transformed objective and all transformed constraints.

3. Show that the reformulated problem is convex.

problem:
Exercise 3.27
\emph{Affine policy.} We consider a family of LPs, parametrized by the random variable $u$, which is uniformly distributed on $U = [-1,1]^p$,

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & c^T x \\
\text{subject to}\quad & Ax \preceq b(u),
\end{aligned}
\]

where $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$, $A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}$, and $b(u) = b_0 + Bu \in \mathbf{R}^m$ is an affine function of $u$. You can think of $u_i$ as representing a deviation of the $i$th parameter from its nominal value. The parameters might represent (deviations in) levels of resources available, or other varying limits.

The problem is to be solved many times; in each time, the value of $u$ (i.e., a sample) is given, and then the decision variable $x$ is chosen. The mapping from $u$ into the decision variable $x(u)$ is called the \emph{policy}, since it gives the decision variable value for each value of $u$. When enough time and computing hardware is available, we can simply solve the LP for each new value of $u$; this is an optimal policy, which we denote $x^\star(u)$.

In some applications, however, the decision $x(u)$ must be made very quickly, so solving the LP is not an option. Instead we seek a suboptimal policy, which is affine: $x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u) = x_0 + Ku$, where $x_0$ is

called the nominal decision and $K \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times p}$ is called the feedback gain matrix. (Roughly speaking, $x_0$ is our guess of $x$ before the value of $u$ has been revealed; $Ku$ is our modification of this guess, once we know $u$.) We determine the policy (i.e., suitable values for $x_0$ and $K$) ahead of time; we can then evaluate the policy (that is, find $x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u)$ given $u$) very quickly, by matrix multiplication and addition.

We will choose $x_0$ and $K$ in order to minimize the expected value of the objective, while insisting that for any value of $u$, feasibility is maintained:

\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\mbox{minimize} & \mathbf{E}\, c^T x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u) \\
\mbox{subject to} & Ax^{\mathrm{aff}}(u) \preceq b(u) \quad \forall u \in U.
\end{array}
\]

The variables here are $x_0$ and $K$. The expectation in the objective is over $u$, and the constraint requires that $Ax^{\mathrm{aff}}(u) \preceq b(u)$ hold almost surely.

(a) Explain how to find optimal values of $x_0$ and $K$ by solving a standard explicit convex optimization problem (i.e., one that does not involve an expectation or an infinite number of constraints, as the one above does.) The numbers of variables or constraints in your formulation should not grow exponentially with the problem dimensions $n$, $p$, or $m$.
problem_with_context:

called the nominal decision and \(K \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times p}\) is called the feedback gain matrix. (Roughly speaking, \(x_0\) is our guess of \(x\) before the value of \(u\) has been revealed; \(Ku\) is our modification of this guess, once we know \(u\).) We determine the policy (i.e., suitable values for \(x_0\) and \(K\)) ahead of time; we can then evaluate the policy (that is, find \(x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u)\) given \(u\)) very quickly, by matrix multiplication and addition.

We will choose \(x_0\) and \(K\) in order to minimize the expected value of the objective, while insisting that for any value of \(u\), feasibility is maintained:

\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\mbox{minimize} & \mathbf{E}\, c^T x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u) \\
\mbox{subject to} & Ax^{\mathrm{aff}}(u) \preceq b(u) \quad \forall u \in U.
\end{array}
\]

The variables here are \(x_0\) and \(K\). The expectation in the objective is over \(u\), and the constraint requires that \(Ax^{\mathrm{aff}}(u) \preceq b(u)\) hold almost surely.

(a) Explain how to find optimal values of \(x_0\) and \(K\) by solving a standard explicit convex optimization problem (i.e., one that does not involve an expectation or an infinite number of constraints, as the one above does.) The numbers of variables or constraints in your formulation should not grow exponentially with the problem dimensions \(n\), \(p\), or \(m\).
problem_standardized_math:
Consider the family of linear programs parameterized by a random vector \(u\), uniformly distributed on \(U=[-1,1]^p\):
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & c^T x \\\\
\text{subject to} \quad & Ax \preceq b(u),
\end{aligned}
\]\nwhere \(x\in\mathbf{R}^n\), \(A\in\mathbf{R}^{m\times n}\), and \(b(u)=b_0+Bu\in\mathbf{R}^m\) is affine in \(u\).

We restrict attention to affine policies of the form
\(x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u)=x_0+Ku,\)\nwhere \(x_0\in\mathbf{R}^n\) and \(K\in\mathbf{R}^{n\times p}\). Choose \(x_0\) and \(K\) to solve
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\text{minimize} & \mathbf{E}\\, c^T x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u) \\\\
\text{subject to} & A x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u) \preceq b(u) \quad \forall u\in U,
\end{array}
\]\nwhere the expectation is with respect to the uniform distribution on \(U=[-1,1]^p\).

Explain how to determine optimal values of \(x_0\) and \(K\) by solving a standard explicit convex optimization problem, i.e., one that does not involve an expectation or infinitely many constraints. The numbers of variables and constraints in your formulation should not grow exponentially with \(n\), \(p\), or \(m\).
problem_finally:
Let \(p,m,n \in \mathbf{N}\). Given \(c \in \mathbf{R}^n\), \(A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}\), \(b_0 \in \mathbf{R}^m\), and \(B \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times p}\), let \(u \in \mathbf{R}^p\) be uniformly distributed on
\(U = [-1,1]^p = \{u \in \mathbf{R}^p : -1 \le u_j \le 1,\ j=1,\dots,p\}.\)
Define
\(b(u) = b_0 + Bu \in \mathbf{R}^m.\)
For each \(u\), consider the linear program
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & c^T x \\\\
\text{subject to} \quad & Ax \le b(u),
\end{aligned}
\]\nwith decision variable \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\).

Restrict the decision rule to the affine form
\(x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u) = x_0 + Ku,\)\nwhere \(x_0 \in \mathbf{R}^n\) and \(K \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times p}\) are decision variables.

Formulate an explicit finite-dimensional convex optimization problem in the variables \(x_0\) and \(K\) that is exactly equivalent to
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\text{minimize} & \mathbf{E}\big[c^T x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u)\big] \\\\
\text{subject to} & A x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u) \le b(u) \quad \forall u \in U,
\end{array}
\]\nwhere the expectation is with respect to the uniform distribution on \(U\).

The reformulation must satisfy:
1. It is a standard finite-dimensional convex optimization problem with no expectation in the objective and no constraint of the form \(\forall u \in U\).
2. It is exactly equivalent to the affine-policy problem above.
3. Its number of decision variables and constraints does not grow exponentially with \(n\), \(p\), or \(m\).

Give the explicit objective and constraints of the resulting problem.

To evaluate your affine policy, generate 100 independent samples of \(u\), and for each value, compute the objective value of the affine policy, \(c^T x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u)\), and of the optimal policy, \(c^T x^\star(u)\). Scatter plot the objective value of the affine policy (\(y\)-axis) versus the objective value of the optimal policy (\(x\)-axis), and include the line \(y=x\) on the plot. problem_standardized_math:
Consider the family of linear programs parameterized by a random variable \(u\), uniformly distributed on \(U=[-1,1]^p\):
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & c^T x \\\\
\text{subject to} \quad & Ax \preceq b(u),
\end{aligned}
\]\nwhere \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\), \(A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}\), and \(b(u)=b_0+Bu \in \mathbf{R}^m\). Let \(x^\star(u)\) denote the optimal policy, obtained by solving the LP for each realized \(u\).

Consider an affine policy of the form
\(x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u)=x_0+Ku,\)\nwhere \(x_0 \in \mathbf{R}^n\) and \(K \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times p}\). The policy is chosen by solving
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\text{minimize} & \mathbf{E}\\, c^T x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u) \\\\
\text{subject to} & A x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u) \preceq b(u) \quad \forall u \in U.
\end{array}
\]

Using the data in \texttt{affine\\_pol\\_data.m}, carry out your method for determining the affine policy. Then generate 100 independent samples of \(u\). For each sample, compute the objective values \(c^T x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u)\) and \(c^T x^\star(u)\). Make a scatter plot of the affine-policy objective value (on the \(y\)-axis) versus the optimal-policy objective value (on the \(x\)-axis), and include the line \(y=x\). Report the sample averages of \(c^T x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u)\) and \(c^T x^\star(u)\). These are estimates of \(\mathbf{E}\\, c^T x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u)\) and \(\mathbf{E}\\, c^T x^\star(u)\).
problem_finally:
Let \(U = [-1,1]^p \subseteq \mathbf{R}^p\), and let \(u \sim \mathrm{Unif}(U)\). Consider the parameterized linear program
\[
\begin{aligned}
\min_{x \in \mathbf{R}^n} \quad & c^T x \\\\
\text{s.t.} \quad & A x \le b(u),
\end{aligned}
\]\nwhere \(A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}\), \(c \in \mathbf{R}^n\), and
\(b(u) = b_0 + B u \in \mathbf{R}^m,\)\nwith \(b_0 \in \mathbf{R}^m\) and \(B \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times p}\). For each \(u \in U\), let \(x^{\star}(u)\) denote an optimal solution.

Consider affine decision rules
\(x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u) = x_0 + K u,\)\nwith \(x_0 \in \mathbf{R}^n\) and \(K \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times p}\), chosen by solving
\[
\begin{aligned}
\min_{x_0, K} \quad & \mathbf{E}\big[c^T x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u)\big] \\\\
\text{s.t.} \quad & A x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u) \le b(u), \quad \forall u \in U.
\end{aligned}
\]
Use the numerical data in \texttt{affine\\_pol\\_data.m}.

1. Formulate and solve the above optimization problem to determine the affine policy \(x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u) = x_0 + K u\).

2. Generate \(100\) independent samples \(u^{(1)}, \dots, u^{(100)}\), each uniformly distributed on \(U\). For each \(i = 1, \dots, 100\), compute
\(c^T x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u^{(i)}) \quad \text{and} \quad c^T x^{\star}(u^{(i)}).\)

3. Create a scatter plot with \(c^T x^{\star}(u^{(i)})\) on the \(x\)-axis and \(c^T x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u^{(i)})\) on the \(y\)-axis for \(i = 1, \dots, 100\), and include the reference line \(y = x\).

4. Compute and report the sample averages
\[
\frac{1}{100} \sum_{i=1}^{100} c^T x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u^{(i)})
\quad \text{and} \quad
\frac{1}{100} \sum_{i=1}^{100} c^T x^{\star}(u^{(i)}).
\]
Interpret these as empirical estimates of
\[
\mathbf{E}\big[c^T x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u)\big]
\quad \text{and} \quad
\mathbf{E}\big[c^T x^{\star}(u)\big].
\]

problem:
Exercise 3.28
Probability bounds. Consider random variables $X_1, X_2, X_3, X_4$ that take values in $\{0,1\}$. We are given the following marginal and conditional probabilities:

\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{prob}(X_1 = 1) &= 0.9, \\
\mathbf{prob}(X_2 = 1) &= 0.9, \\
\mathbf{prob}(X_3 = 1) &= 0.1, \\
\mathbf{prob}(X_1 = 1, X_4 = 0 \mid X_3 = 1) &= 0.7, \\
\mathbf{prob}(X_4 = 1 \mid X_2 = 1, X_3 = 0) &= 0.6.
\end{aligned}
\]

Explain how to find the minimum and maximum possible values of $\mathbf{prob}(X_4 = 1)$, over all (joint) probability distributions consistent with the given data. Find these values and report them.

Hints. (You should feel free to ignore these hints.)

- Matlab:
  - CVX supports multidimensional arrays; for example, \texttt{variable p(2,2,2,2)} declares a 4-dimensional array of variables, with each of the four indices taking the values 1 or 2.
  - The function \texttt{sum(p,i)} sums a multidimensional array $p$ along the $i$th index.
  - The expression \texttt{sum(a(:))} gives the sum of all entries of a multidimensional array $a$. You might want to use the function definition \texttt{sum\_all = @(A) sum( A(:));}, so \texttt{sum\_all(a)} gives the sum of all entries in the multidimensional array $a$.

- Python:
  - Create a 1-d Variable and manually index the entries. You should come up with a reasonable scheme to avoid confusion.

- Julia:
  - You can create a multidimensional array of variables in Convex.jl. For example, the following creates a 4-dimensional array of variables, with each of the four indices taking the values 1 or 2.

\texttt{p = [Variable() for i in 1:16];}\\
\texttt{p = reshape(p, 2, 2, 2, 2)}

  - You can use the function \texttt{sum} to sum over various indices in the multidimensional array.

\texttt{sum(p[:,:,:,:]) \# sum all entries}\\
\texttt{sum(p[1,:,2,:]) \# fix first and third indices}

  - To create constraints with the variables in the array, you need to access each variable independently. Something like \texttt{p >= 0} will not work.

problem_standardized_math:
Let \(X_1,X_2,X_3,X_4\) be random variables taking values in \(\{0,1\}\). Suppose the following marginal and conditional probabilities are given:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{prob}(X_1=1) &= 0.9,\\
\mathbf{prob}(X_2=1) &= 0.9,\\
\mathbf{prob}(X_3=1) &= 0.1,\\
\mathbf{prob}(X_1=1,\,X_4=0\mid X_3=1) &= 0.7,\\
\mathbf{prob}(X_4=1\mid X_2=1,\,X_3=0) &= 0.6.
\end{aligned}
\]
Determine how to compute the minimum and maximum possible values of \(\mathbf{prob}(X_4=1)\) over all joint probability distributions of \((X_1,X_2,X_3,X_4)\) that are consistent with the given data. Find these minimum and maximum values and report them.
problem_finally:
Let \((X_1,X_2,X_3,X_4)\) be a random vector with values in \(\{0,1\}^4\). Consider all joint distributions of \((X_1,X_2,X_3,X_4)\) satisfying
\(\mathbf{P}(X_1=1)=0.9, \qquad \mathbf{P}(X_2=1)=0.9, \qquad \mathbf{P}(X_3=1)=0.1,\)
\(\mathbf{P}(X_1=1, X_4=0 \mid X_3=1)=0.7, \qquad \mathbf{P}(X_4=1 \mid X_2=1, X_3=0)=0.6.\)
Assume the conditional probabilities are well-defined, i.e.
\(\mathbf{P}(X_3=1)>0, \qquad \mathbf{P}(X_2=1, X_3=0)>0.\)
Determine the extremal values of the marginal probability \(\mathbf{P}(X_4=1)\) over all such joint distributions.

1. Formulate the optimization problem for
\(\min \mathbf{P}(X_4=1)\)
subject to the above probability constraints.

2. Formulate the optimization problem for
\(\max \mathbf{P}(X_4=1)\)
subject to the above probability constraints.

3. Compute the minimum and maximum possible values of \(\mathbf{P}(X_4=1)\).

problem:
Exercise 3.29
Robust quadratic programming. In this problem, we consider a robust variation of the (convex) quadratic program

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & (1/2)x^T P x + q^T x + r \\
\text{subject to}\quad & Ax \preceq b.
\end{aligned}
\]

For simplicity we assume that only the matrix $P$ is subject to errors, and the other parameters $(q, r, A, b)$ are exactly known. The robust quadratic program is defined as

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & \sup_{P \in \mathcal{E}} ((1/2)x^T P x + q^T x + r) \\
\text{subject to}\quad & Ax \preceq b
\end{aligned}
\]

where $\mathcal{E}$ is the set of possible matrices $P$.

For each of the following sets $\mathcal{E}$, express the robust QP as a tractable convex problem. Be as specific as you can. (Here, tractable means that the problem can be reduced to an LP, QP, QCQP, SOCP, or SDP. But you do not have to work out the reduction, if it is complicated; it is enough to argue that it can be reduced to one of these.)

(a) A finite set of matrices:

\[
\mathcal{E} = \{P_1,\ldots,P_K\},
\]

where $P_i \in \mathbf{S}_+^n$, $i = 1,\ldots,K$.

(Here, tractable means that the problem can be reduced to an LP, QP, QCQP, SOCP, or SDP. But you do not have to work out the reduction, if it is complicated; it is enough to argue that it can be reduced to one of these.)

problem_standardized_math:
Consider the robust quadratic program
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & \sup_{P \in \mathcal{E}} \left( \frac{1}{2} x^T P x + q^T x + r \right) \\\\
\text{subject to} \quad & Ax \preceq b,
\end{aligned}
\]\nwhere only the matrix \(P\) is uncertain, while \(q\), \(r\), \(A\), and \(b\) are exactly known. For the case
\(\mathcal{E} = \{P_1, \ldots, P_K\},\)\nwith \(P_i \in \mathbf{S}_+^n\) for \(i = 1, \ldots, K\), express the robust quadratic program as a tractable convex problem. Be as specific as possible. Here, tractable means that the problem can be reduced to an LP, QP, QCQP, SOCP, or SDP; however, if the reduction is complicated, it is sufficient to argue that such a reduction is possible.
problem_finally:
Let \(n,m,K \in \mathbf{N}\). The decision variable is \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\). The data are \(q \in \mathbf{R}^n\), \(r \in \mathbf{R}\), \(A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}\), \(b \in \mathbf{R}^m\), and a finite uncertainty set
\(\mathcal{E} = \{P_1,\ldots,P_K\},\)\nwhere \(P_i \in \mathbf{S}_+^n\) for all \(i=1,\ldots,K\), and \(\mathbf{S}_+^n\) denotes the set of \(n \times n\) real symmetric positive semidefinite matrices.

Consider the robust optimization problem
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & \sup_{P \in \mathcal{E}} \left( \frac{1}{2} x^T P x + q^T x + r \right) \\\\
\text{subject to} \quad & Ax \le b.
\end{aligned}
\]

Rewrite this problem as an explicit tractable convex optimization problem.

1. Give a reformulation with explicit auxiliary variable(s) and constraints that exactly represents the objective \(\sup_{P \in \mathcal{E}} \left( \frac{1}{2} x^T P x + q^T x + r \right)\).
2. State the resulting optimization problem in a standard convex form as explicitly as possible.
3. Specify which tractable class the reformulated problem belongs to, or explain precisely how it can be reduced to one of the following classes: LP, QP, QCQP, SOCP, or SDP.

3. If several reformulation steps are needed, it is sufficient to give an equivalent convex problem and state why it belongs to one of the above classes.

problem:
Exercise 3.30
\emph{Smallest confidence ellipsoid.} Suppose the random variable $X$ on $\mathbf{R}^n$ has log-concave density $p$. Formulate the following problem as a convex optimization problem: Find an ellipsoid $\mathcal{E}$ that satisfies $\operatorname{prob}(X \in \mathcal{E}) \geq 0.95$ and is smallest, in the sense of minimizing the sum of the squares of its semi-axis lengths. You do not need to worry about how to solve the resulting convex optimization problem; it is enough to formulate the smallest confidence ellipsoid problem as the problem of minimizing a convex function over a convex set involving the parameters that define $\mathcal{E}$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 3.30
\emph{Smallest confidence ellipsoid.} Suppose the random variable \(X\) on \(\mathbf{R}^n\) has log-concave density \(p\). Formulate the following problem as a convex optimization problem: Find an ellipsoid \(\mathcal{E}\) that satisfies \(\operatorname{prob}(X \in \mathcal{E}) \geq 0.95\) and is smallest, in the sense of minimizing the sum of the squares of its semi-axis lengths. You do not need to worry about how to solve the resulting convex optimization problem; it is enough to formulate the smallest confidence ellipsoid problem as the problem of minimizing a convex function over a convex set involving the parameters that define \(\mathcal{E}\).
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(X\) be a random variable on \(\mathbf{R}^n\) with log-concave density \(p\). Formulate as a convex optimization problem the problem of finding an ellipsoid \(\mathcal{E}\) such that
\(\operatorname{prob}(X \in \mathcal{E}) \geq 0.95,\)\nand \(\mathcal{E}\) is smallest in the sense that the sum of the squares of its semi-axis lengths is minimized. It is sufficient to express the smallest confidence ellipsoid problem as the minimization of a convex function over a convex set in terms of the parameters defining \(\mathcal{E}\); you do not need to address how to solve the resulting convex optimization problem.
problem_finally:
Let \(X\) be an \(\mathbf{R}^n\)-valued random variable with log-concave probability density \(p : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}_+\). Consider ellipsoids
\(\mathcal{E}(c,P) = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid (x-c)^T P^{-1} (x-c) \le 1\},\)
where \(c \in \mathbf{R}^n\) and \(P \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n}\) is symmetric positive definite. The squared semi-axis lengths of \(\mathcal{E}(c,P)\) are the eigenvalues of \(P\), so their sum is \(\operatorname{tr}(P)\).

Formulate the following as a convex optimization problem in the variables \(c\) and \(P\):

1. Minimize the size of the ellipsoid, measured by \(\operatorname{tr}(P)\).
2. Impose the confidence constraint
\(\operatorname{Prob}(X \in \mathcal{E}(c,P)) \ge 0.95.\)
3. Write the final formulation explicitly as minimization of a convex objective over a convex feasible set in \((c,P)\).

You may use without proof that, because \(p\) is log-concave, the constraint on the probability content of the ellipsoid can be represented so that the feasible set in \((c,P)\) is convex. Do not solve the problem or discuss numerical methods.

problem:
Exercise 3.31
Stochastic optimization via Monte Carlo sampling. In (convex) stochastic optimization, the goal is to minimize a cost function of the form $F(x) = \mathbf{E}\, f(x,\omega)$, where $\omega$ is a random variable on $\Omega$, and $f:\mathbf{R}^n \times \Omega \to \mathbf{R}$ is convex in its first argument for each $\omega \in \Omega$. (For simplicity we consider the unconstrained problem; it is not hard to include constraints.) Evidently $F$ is convex. Let $p^\star$ denote the optimal value, i.e., $p^\star = \inf_x F(x)$ (which we assume is finite).

In a few very simple cases we can work out what $F$ is analytically, but in general this is not possible. Moreover in many applications, we do not know the distribution of $\omega$; we only have access to an oracle that can generate independent samples from the distribution.

A standard method for approximately solving the stochastic optimization problem is based on Monte Carlo sampling. We first generate $N$ independent samples, $\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_N$, and form the empirical expectation

\[
\hat{F}(x) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N f(x,\omega_i).
\]

This is a random function, since it depends on the particular samples drawn. For each $x$, we have $\mathbf{E}\,\hat{F}(x) = F(x)$, and also $\mathbf{E}(\hat{F}(x)-F(x))^2 \propto 1/N$. Roughly speaking, for $N$ large enough, $\hat{F}(x) \approx F(x)$.

To (approximately) minimize $F$, we instead minimize $\hat{F}(x)$. The minimizer, $\hat{x}^\star$, and the optimal value $\hat{p}^\star = \hat{F}(\hat{x}^\star)$, are also random variables. The hope is that for $N$ large enough, we have $\hat{p}^\star \approx p^\star$. (In practice, stochastic optimization via Monte Carlo sampling works very well, even when $N$ is not that big.)

One way to check the result of Monte Carlo sampling is to carry it out multiple times. We repeatedly generate different batches of samples, and for each batch, we find $\hat{x}^\star$ and $\hat{p}^\star$. If the values of $\hat{p}^\star$ are near each other, it's reasonable to believe that we have (approximately) minimized $F$. If they are not, it means our value of $N$ is too small.

Show that $\mathbf{E}\,\hat{p}^\star \leq p^\star$.

Hint. Show that for any function $G:\mathbf{R}^n \times \Omega \to \mathbf{R}$ (convex or not in its first argument), and any random variable $\omega$ on $\Omega$, we have

\[
\inf_x \mathbf{E}\,G(x,\omega) \geq \mathbf{E}\inf_x G(x,\omega).
\]
problem_with_context:
Exercise 3.31
Stochastic optimization via Monte Carlo sampling. In (convex) stochastic optimization, the goal is to minimize a cost function of the form \(F(x) = \mathbf{E}\, f(x,\omega)\), where \(\omega\) is a random variable on \(\Omega\), and \(f:\mathbf{R}^n \times \Omega \to \mathbf{R}\) is convex in its first argument for each \(\omega \in \Omega\). (For simplicity we consider the unconstrained problem; it is not hard to include constraints.) Evidently \(F\) is convex. Let \(p^\star\) denote the optimal value, i.e., \(p^\star = \inf_x F(x)\) (which we assume is finite).

In a few very simple cases we can work out what \(F\) is analytically, but in general this is not possible. Moreover in many applications, we do not know the distribution of \(\omega\); we only have access to an oracle that can generate independent samples from the distribution.

A standard method for approximately solving the stochastic optimization problem is based on Monte Carlo sampling. We first generate \(N\) independent samples, \(\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_N\), and form the empirical expectation

\(\hat{F}(x) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N f(x,\omega_i).\)

This is a random function, since it depends on the particular samples drawn. For each \(x\), we have \(\mathbf{E}\,\hat{F}(x) = F(x)\), and also \(\mathbf{E}(\hat{F}(x)-F(x))^2 \propto 1/N\). Roughly speaking, for \(N\) large enough, \(\hat{F}(x) \approx F(x)\).

To (approximately) minimize \(F\), we instead minimize \(\hat{F}(x)\). The minimizer, \(\hat{x}^\star\), and the optimal value \(\hat{p}^\star = \hat{F}(\hat{x}^\star)\), are also random variables. The hope is that for \(N\) large enough, we have \(\hat{p}^\star \approx p^\star\). (In practice, stochastic optimization via Monte Carlo sampling works very well, even when \(N\) is not that big.)

One way to check the result of Monte Carlo sampling is to carry it out multiple times. We repeatedly generate different batches of samples, and for each batch, we find \(\hat{x}^\star\) and \(\hat{p}^\star\). If the values of \(\hat{p}^\star\) are near each other, it's reasonable to believe that we have (approximately) minimized \(F\). If they are not, it means our value of \(N\) is too small.

Show that \(\mathbf{E}\,\hat{p}^\star \leq p^\star\).

Hint. Show that for any function \(G:\mathbf{R}^n \times \Omega \to \mathbf{R}\) (convex or not in its first argument), and any random variable \(\omega\) on \(\Omega\), we have

\(\inf_x \mathbf{E}\,G(x,\omega) \geq \mathbf{E}\inf_x G(x,\omega).\)
problem_standardized_math:
Let
\(F(x)=\mathbf{E}\,f(x,\omega),\)\nwhere \(\omega\) is a random variable on \(\Omega\), and \(f:\mathbf{R}^n\times\Omega\to\mathbf{R}\) is convex in its first argument for each \(\omega\in\Omega\). Consider the unconstrained stochastic optimization problem
\(p^\star=\inf_x F(x),\)\nand assume \(p^\star\) is finite.

Generate \(N\) independent samples \(\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_N\), and define the empirical objective
\(\hat{F}(x)=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N f(x,\omega_i).\)
Let \(\hat{x}^\star\) be a minimizer of \(\hat{F}\), and let
\(\hat{p}^\star=\hat{F}(\hat{x}^\star).\)
Show that
\(\mathbf{E}\\,\hat{p}^\star\leq p^\star.\)

Hint: Show that for any function \(G:\mathbf{R}^n\times\Omega\to\mathbf{R}\) (whether or not it is convex in its first argument), and any random variable \(\omega\) on \(\Omega\),
\(\inf_x \mathbf{E}\,G(x,\omega)\geq \mathbf{E}\inf_x G(x,\omega).\)
problem_finally:
Let \(f : \mathbf{R}^n \times \Omega \to \mathbf{R}\) satisfy: for every \(\omega \in \Omega\), the function \(x \mapsto f(x,\omega)\) is convex on \(\mathbf{R}^n\). Let \(\omega\) be a random variable on \(\Omega\), and define
\(F(x) = \mathbf{E}\,f(x,\omega), \qquad x \in \mathbf{R}^n.\)
Consider
\(p^\star = \inf_{x \in \mathbf{R}^n} F(x),\)\nand assume \(p^\star > -\infty\).

Let \(\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_N\) be independent random variables with the same distribution as \(\omega\), and define
\(\hat{F}(x) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N f(x,\omega_i), \qquad x \in \mathbf{R}^n.\)
Assume \(\hat{F}\) attains its minimum on \(\mathbf{R}^n\). Let \(\hat{x}^\star \in \mathbf{R}^n\) be any minimizer, and define
\(\hat{p}^\star = \hat{F}(\hat{x}^\star) = \inf_{x \in \mathbf{R}^n} \hat{F}(x).\)

1. Prove the general inequality: for any function \(G : \mathbf{R}^n \times \Omega \to \mathbf{R}\) and any random variable \(\omega\) on \(\Omega\),
\(\inf_{x \in \mathbf{R}^n} \mathbf{E}\,G(x,\omega) \ge \mathbf{E}\,\inf_{x \in \mathbf{R}^n} G(x,\omega).\)

2. Using the above inequality, show that
\(\mathbf{E}\,\hat{p}^\star \le p^\star.\)

problem:
Exercise 3.32
\emph{Satisfying a minimum number of constraints.} Consider the problem

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & f_0(x) \\
\text{subject to}\quad & f_i(x) \leq 0 \text{ holds for at least } k \text{ values of } i,
\end{aligned}
\]

with variable $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$, where the objective $f_0$ and the constraint functions $f_i$, $i = 1, \ldots, m$ (with $m \geq k$), are convex. Here we require that only $k$ of the constraints hold, instead of all $m$ of them. In general this is a hard combinatorial problem; the brute force solution is to solve all $\binom{m}{k}$ convex problems obtained by choosing subsets of $k$ constraints to impose, and selecting one with smallest objective value.

In this problem we explore a convex restriction that can be an effective heuristic for the problem.

(a) Suppose $\lambda > 0$. Show that the constraint

\[
\sum_{i=1}^m (1+\lambda f_i(x))_+ \leq m-k
\]

guarantees that $f_i(x) \leq 0$ holds for at least $k$ values of $i$. ($(u)_+$ means $\max\{u,0\}$.)

Hint. For each $u \in \mathbf{R}$, $(1+\lambda u)_+ \geq 1(u>0)$, where $1(u>0)=1$ for $u>0$, and $1(u>0)=0$ for $u \leq 0$.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 3.32
\emph{Satisfying a minimum number of constraints.} Consider the problem

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & f_0(x) \\
\text{subject to}\quad & f_i(x) \leq 0 \text{ holds for at least } k \text{ values of } i,
\end{aligned}
\]

with variable \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\), where the objective \(f_0\) and the constraint functions \(f_i\), \(i = 1, \ldots, m\) (with \(m \geq k\)), are convex. Here we require that only \(k\) of the constraints hold, instead of all \(m\) of them. In general this is a hard combinatorial problem; the brute force solution is to solve all \(\binom{m}{k}\) convex problems obtained by choosing subsets of \(k\) constraints to impose, and selecting one with smallest objective value.

In this problem we explore a convex restriction that can be an effective heuristic for the problem.

(a) Suppose \(\lambda > 0\). Show that the constraint

\(\sum_{i=1}^m (1+\lambda f_i(x))_+ \leq m-k\)

guarantees that \(f_i(x) \leq 0\) holds for at least \(k\) values of \(i\). (\((u)_+\) means \(\max\{u,0\}\).)

Hint. For each \(u \in \mathbf{R}\), \((1+\lambda u)_+ \geq 1(u>0)\), where \(1(u>0)=1\) for \(u>0\), and \(1(u>0)=0\) for \(u \leq 0\).
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\), and let \(f_0\) and \(f_i\), \(i=1,\ldots,m\), be convex functions, with \(m \geq k\). Consider the problem
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & f_0(x) \\\\
\text{subject to} \quad & f_i(x) \leq 0 \text{ for at least } k \text{ values of } i.
\end{aligned}
\]
Suppose \(\lambda > 0\). Show that the constraint
\(\sum_{i=1}^m (1+\lambda f_i(x))_+ \leq m-k\)\nguarantees that \(f_i(x) \leq 0\) holds for at least \(k\) values of \(i\), where \((u)_+ = \max\{u,0\}\).

Hint: for each \(u \in \mathbf{R}\),
\((1+\lambda u)_+ \geq 1(u>0),\)\nwhere \(1(u>0)=1\) for \(u>0\) and \(1(u>0)=0\) for \(u \leq 0\).
problem_finally:
Let \(n,m,k \in \mathbf{N}\) with \(1 \le k \le m\), let \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\), and let \(f_0,f_1,\ldots,f_m : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be convex functions. Consider
\[
\begin{aligned}
\min_{x \in \mathbf{R}^n} \quad & f_0(x) \\\\
\text{s.t.} \quad & f_i(x) \le 0 \text{ for at least } k \text{ indices } i \in \{1,\ldots,m\}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Let \(\lambda > 0\). Prove that the constraint
\(\sum_{i=1}^m (1 + \lambda f_i(x))_+ \le m-k,\)\nwhere
\((u)_+ = \max\{u,0\}, \qquad u \in \mathbf{R},\)\nimplies that \(f_i(x) \le 0\) holds for at least \(k\) indices \(i \in \{1,\ldots,m\}\).

You may use that for every \(u \in \mathbf{R}\),
\((1+\lambda u)_+ \ge 1(u>0),\)\nwhere
\[
1(u>0) = \begin{cases}
1, & u>0, \\\\
0, & u \le 0.
\end{cases}
\]

problem:
Exercise 3.32
\emph{Satisfying a minimum number of constraints.} Consider the problem

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & f_0(x) \\
\text{subject to}\quad & f_i(x) \leq 0 \text{ holds for at least } k \text{ values of } i,
\end{aligned}
\]

with variable $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$, where the objective $f_0$ and the constraint functions $f_i$, $i = 1, \ldots, m$ (with $m \geq k$), are convex. Here we require that only $k$ of the constraints hold, instead of all $m$ of them. In general this is a hard combinatorial problem; the brute force solution is to solve all $\binom{m}{k}$ convex problems obtained by choosing subsets of $k$ constraints to impose, and selecting one with smallest objective value.

In this problem we explore a convex restriction that can be an effective heuristic for the problem.

(b) Consider the problem

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & f_0(x) \\
\text{subject to}\quad & \sum_{i=1}^m (1+\lambda f_i(x))_+ \leq m-k \\
& \lambda > 0,
\end{aligned}
\]

with variables $x$ and $\lambda$. This is a restriction of the original problem: If $(x,\lambda)$ are feasible for it, then $x$ is feasible for the original problem. Show how to solve this problem using convex optimization. (This may involve a change of variables.)

Exercise 3.32
\emph{Satisfying a minimum number of constraints.} Consider the problem

\[

with variable \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\), where the objective \(f_0\) and the constraint functions \(f_i\), \(i = 1, \ldots, m\) (with \(m \geq k\)), are convex. Here we require that only \(k\) of the constraints hold, instead of all \(m\) of them. In general this is a hard combinatorial problem; the brute force solution is to solve all \(\binom{m}{k}\) convex problems obtained by choosing subsets of \(k\) constraints to impose, and selecting one with smallest objective value.

In this problem we explore a convex restriction that can be an effective heuristic for the problem.

(b) Consider the problem

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & f_0(x) \\
\text{subject to}\quad & \sum_{i=1}^m (1+\lambda f_i(x))_+ \leq m-k \\
& \lambda > 0,
\end{aligned}
\]

with variables \(x\) and \(\lambda\). This is a restriction of the original problem: If \((x,\lambda)\) are feasible for it, then \(x\) is feasible for the original problem. Show how to solve this problem using convex optimization. (This may involve a change of variables.)
problem_standardized_math:
Let \(f_0\) and \(f_i\), \(i=1,\ldots,m\), be convex functions, with \(m \geq k\), and consider the original problem
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & f_0(x) \\\\
\text{subject to} \quad & f_i(x) \leq 0 \quad \text{for at least } k \text{ values of } i,
\end{aligned}
\]\nwith variable \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\).

Now consider the restriction
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & f_0(x) \\\\
\text{subject to} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^m (1+\lambda f_i(x))_+ \leq m-k, \\\\
& \lambda > 0,
\end{aligned}
\]\nwith variables \(x\) and \(\lambda\). If \((x,\lambda)\) is feasible for this problem, then \(x\) is feasible for the original problem. Show how to solve this problem using convex optimization. (This may involve a change of variables.)
problem_finally:
Let \(f_0,f_1,\ldots,f_m : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) be convex functions, with \(m,k \in \mathbf{N}\) and \(m \ge k\).

Define the original problem:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & f_0(x) \\\\
\text{subject to} \quad & f_i(x) \le 0 \text{ for at least } k \text{ indices } i \in \{1,\ldots,m\},
\end{aligned}
\]\nwith decision variable \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\).

Consider the restricted problem:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & f_0(x) \\\\
\text{subject to} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^m (1+\lambda f_i(x))_+ \le m-k, \\\\
& \lambda > 0,
\end{aligned}
\]\nwith decision variables \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\) and \(\lambda \in \mathbf{R}\), where
\((t)_+ = \max\{t,0\}, \qquad t \in \mathbf{R}.\)
Assume that every feasible pair \((x,\lambda)\) of the restricted problem yields an \(x\) that is feasible for the original problem.

1. Reformulate the restricted problem explicitly as a convex optimization problem.
2. The reformulation may use a change of variables, but the new variables and constraints must be stated explicitly.
3. The final formulation must be a standard convex optimization problem that can be solved by convex optimization methods.

problem:
Exercise 3.32
\emph{Satisfying a minimum number of constraints.} Consider the problem

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & f_0(x) \\
\text{subject to}\quad & f_i(x) \leq 0 \text{ holds for at least } k \text{ values of } i,
\end{aligned}
\]

with variable $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$, where the objective $f_0$ and the constraint functions $f_i$, $i = 1, \ldots, m$ (with $m \geq k$), are convex. Here we require that only $k$ of the constraints hold, instead of all $m$ of them. In general this is a hard combinatorial problem; the brute force solution is to solve all $\binom{m}{k}$ convex problems obtained by choosing subsets of $k$ constraints to impose, and selecting one with smallest objective value.

In this problem we explore a convex restriction that can be an effective heuristic for the problem.

(c) Apply the method of part (b) to the problem instance

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & c^T x \\
\text{subject to}\quad & a_i^T x \leq b_i \text{ holds for at least } k \text{ values of } i,
\end{aligned}
\]

with $m=70$, $k=58$, and $n=12$. The vectors $b$, $c$ and the matrix $A$ with rows $a_i^T$ are given in the file \texttt{satisfy\_some\_constraints\_data.*}.

Report the optimal value of $\lambda$, the objective value, and the actual number of constraints that are satisfied (which should be larger than or equal to $k$). To determine if a constraint is satisfied, you can use the tolerance $a_i^T x - b_i \leq \epsilon^{\mathrm{feas}}$, with $\epsilon^{\mathrm{feas}} = 10^{-5}$.

A standard trick is to take this tentative solution, choose the $k$ constraints with the smallest values of $f_i(x)$, and then minimize $f_0(x)$ subject to these $k$ constraints (i.e., ignoring the other $m-k$ constraints). This improves the objective value over the one found using the restriction. Carry this out for the problem instance, and report the objective value obtained.

\]

with \(m=70\), \(k=58\), and \(n=12\). The vectors \(b\), \(c\) and the matrix \(A\) with rows \(a_i^T\) are given in the file \texttt{satisfy\_some\_constraints\_data.*}.

Report the optimal value of \(\lambda\), the objective value, and the actual number of constraints that are satisfied (which should be larger than or equal to \(k\)). To determine if a constraint is satisfied, you can use the tolerance \(a_i^T x - b_i \leq \epsilon^{\mathrm{feas}}\), with \(\epsilon^{\mathrm{feas}} = 10^{-5}\).

problem_standardized_math:
Apply the method of part (b) to the problem instance
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & c^T x \\\\
\text{subject to} \quad & a_i^T x \leq b_i \quad \text{for at least } k \text{ values of } i,
\end{aligned}
\]\nwith \(m = 70\), \(k = 58\), and \(n = 12\). The vectors \(b\), \(c\), and the matrix \(A\) with rows \(a_i^T\) are given in the file \texttt{satisfy\\_some\\_constraints\\_data.*}.

Report the optimal value of \(\lambda\), the objective value, and the actual number of constraints satisfied, which should be at least \(k\). To determine whether a constraint is satisfied, use the tolerance
\(a_i^T x - b_i \leq \epsilon^{\mathrm{feas}}, \qquad \epsilon^{\mathrm{feas}} = 10^{-5}.\)

Then take the tentative solution obtained from the restriction, choose the \(k\) constraints with the smallest values of \(f_i(x)\), and minimize \(f_0(x)\) subject to these \(k\) constraints only, ignoring the other \(m-k\) constraints. Carry this out for the given problem instance and report the resulting objective value.
problem_finally:
Consider the optimization problem
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & c^T x \\\\
\text{subject to} \quad & a_i^T x \le b_i \quad \text{for at least } k \text{ indices } i \in \{1,\dots,m\},
\end{aligned}
\]\nwith variable \(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\), where \(m=70\), \(k=58\), \(n=12\). The data are \(c \in \mathbf{R}^{12}\), \(b \in \mathbf{R}^{70}\), and \(A \in \mathbf{R}^{70 \times 12}\), whose \(i\)-th row is \(a_i^T\). These data are given in the file \texttt{satisfy\\_some\\_constraints\\_data.*}.

Using the method developed in part (b), do the following.

1. Compute a solution using the method of part (b), and report
\(\lambda, \qquad c^T x, \qquad \text{and the number of satisfied constraints}.\)
A constraint \(a_i^T x \le b_i\) is counted as satisfied if and only if
\(a_i^T x - b_i \le \epsilon^{\mathrm{feas}}, \qquad \epsilon^{\mathrm{feas}} = 10^{-5}.\)
The reported number of satisfied constraints must be at least \(k\).

2. Let \(x\) be the tentative solution obtained from the restriction in part (b). Define
\(f_0(x) = c^T x, \qquad f_i(x) = a_i^T x - b_i, \quad i=1,\dots,m.\)
Select the \(k\) constraints corresponding to the \(k\) smallest values of \(f_i(x)\). Then solve the reduced problem formed by keeping only these \(k\) constraints:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & f_0(x) \\\\
\text{subject to} \quad & f_i(x) \le 0 \quad \text{for the selected } k \text{ indices}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Report the resulting objective value.

problem:
Exercise 3.33
[Barvinok, Pataki] A standard form semidefinite program is defined as

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & \operatorname{tr}(CX) \\
\text{subject to}\quad & \operatorname{tr}(A_iX) = b_i,\quad i=1,\ldots,m \\
& X \succeq 0.
\end{aligned}
\tag{4}
\]

The variable $X$ and the coefficients $A_1,\ldots,A_m$ are symmetric $n \times n$ matrices.

A matrix $\hat X$ is called an \emph{extreme point} of the feasible set of (4) if $\hat X$ is feasible and if the only matrix $V \in \mathbf{S}^n$ that satisfies the conditions

\[
\operatorname{tr}(A_iV)=0,\quad i=1,\ldots,m,\qquad \hat X+V \succeq 0,\qquad \hat X-V \succeq 0
\tag{5}
\]

is $V=0$. In this problem we work out a bound on the rank of extreme points.

(a) Suppose $\hat X$ is feasible for (4) and has rank $r$. Define an eigenvalue decomposition

\[
\hat X=
\begin{bmatrix}
Q_1 & Q_2
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
\Lambda_1 & 0\\
0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
Q_1 & Q_2
\end{bmatrix}^T,
\]

where $\begin{bmatrix}Q_1 & Q_2\end{bmatrix}$ is orthogonal, $Q_1$ has $r$ columns, and $\Lambda_1$ is a diagonal $r\times r$ matrix with positive diagonal elements. Show that $V$ satisfies (5) if and only if it can be expressed as $V = Q_1 Y Q_1^T$ where $Y \in \mathbf{S}^r$ satisfies

\[
\operatorname{tr}(Q_1^TA_iQ_1Y)=0,\quad i=1,\ldots,m,\qquad \Lambda_1+Y \succeq 0,\qquad \Lambda_1-Y \succeq 0.
\]

problem_standardized_math:
Consider the standard-form semidefinite program
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & \operatorname{tr}(CX) \\\\
\text{subject to} \quad & \operatorname{tr}(A_iX)=b_i, \quad i=1,\ldots,m,\\\\
& X \succeq 0,
\end{aligned}
\]\nwhere the variable \(X\) and the coefficients \(A_1,\ldots,A_m\) are symmetric \(n \times n\) matrices. A matrix \(\hat X\) is feasible if it satisfies these constraints. For \(V \in \mathbf{S}^n\), consider the conditions
\(\operatorname{tr}(A_iV)=0, \quad i=1,\ldots,m, \qquad \hat X+V \succeq 0, \qquad \hat X-V \succeq 0.\)
Suppose \(\hat X\) is feasible and has rank \(r\). Let
\[
\hat X=
\begin{bmatrix}
Q_1 & Q_2
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
\Lambda_1 & 0 \\\\
0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
Q_1 & Q_2
\end{bmatrix}^T,
\]\nwhere \(\begin{bmatrix}Q_1 & Q_2\end{bmatrix}\) is orthogonal, \(Q_1\) has \(r\) columns, and \(\Lambda_1\) is a diagonal \(r \times r\) matrix with positive diagonal entries. Show that \(V\) satisfies
\(\operatorname{tr}(A_iV)=0, \quad i=1,\ldots,m, \qquad \hat X+V \succeq 0, \qquad \hat X-V \succeq 0\)\nif and only if it can be written as
\(V=Q_1YQ_1^T,\)\nwhere \(Y \in \mathbf{S}^r\) satisfies
\(\operatorname{tr}(Q_1^TA_iQ_1Y)=0, \quad i=1,\ldots,m, \qquad \Lambda_1+Y \succeq 0, \qquad \Lambda_1-Y \succeq 0.\)
problem_finally:
Consider the semidefinite program
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize} \quad & \operatorname{tr}(CX) \\\\
\text{subject to} \quad & \operatorname{tr}(A_i X) = b_i, \quad i = 1, \ldots, m, \\\\
& X \succeq 0,
\end{aligned}
\]\nwith variable \(X \in \mathbf{S}^n\), data \(C, A_1, \ldots, A_m \in \mathbf{S}^n\), and \(b_i \in \mathbf{R}\) for \(i = 1, \ldots, m\). A matrix \(\hat X \in \mathbf{S}^n\) is feasible if
\(\operatorname{tr}(A_i \hat X) = b_i, \quad i = 1, \ldots, m, \\, \quad \hat X \succeq 0.\)

Fix a feasible matrix \(\hat X\) of rank \(r\), with spectral decomposition
\[
\hat X =
\begin{bmatrix}
Q_1 & Q_2
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
\Lambda_1 & 0 \\\\
0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
Q_1 & Q_2
\end{bmatrix}^T,
\]\nwhere \(\begin{bmatrix} Q_1 & Q_2 \end{bmatrix}\) is an orthogonal \(n \times n\) matrix, \(Q_1 \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times r}\), \(Q_2 \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times (n-r)}\), and \(\Lambda_1 \in \mathbf{S}^r\) is diagonal with strictly positive diagonal entries.

Let \(V \in \mathbf{S}^n\). Prove that the following are equivalent:

1. \(V\) satisfies
\(\operatorname{tr}(A_i V) = 0, \quad i = 1, \ldots, m, \qquad \hat X + V \succeq 0, \qquad \hat X - V \succeq 0.\)

2. There exists \(Y \in \mathbf{S}^r\) such that
\(V = Q_1 Y Q_1^T,\)\nand
\(\operatorname{tr}(Q_1^T A_i Q_1 \\, Y) = 0, \quad i = 1, \ldots, m, \qquad \Lambda_1 + Y \succeq 0, \qquad \Lambda_1 - Y \succeq 0.\)

problem:
Exercise 3.33
[Barvinok, Pataki] A standard form semidefinite program is defined as

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & \operatorname{tr}(CX) \\
\text{subject to}\quad & \operatorname{tr}(A_iX) = b_i,\quad i=1,\ldots,m \\
& X \succeq 0.
\end{aligned}
\tag{4}
\]

The variable $X$ and the coefficients $A_1,\ldots,A_m$ are symmetric $n \times n$ matrices.

A matrix $\hat X$ is called an \emph{extreme point} of the feasible set of (4) if $\hat X$ is feasible and if the only matrix $V \in \mathbf{S}^n$ that satisfies the conditions

\[
\operatorname{tr}(A_iV)=0,\quad i=1,\ldots,m,\qquad \hat X+V \succeq 0,\qquad \hat X-V \succeq 0
\tag{5}
\]

is $V=0$. In this problem we work out a bound on the rank of extreme points.

(b) Show that if $r(r+1)/2>m$, then $\hat X$ is not an extreme point.
problem_with_context:
Exercise 3.33
[Barvinok, Pataki] A standard form semidefinite program is defined as

\[
